promise_text_en: By 2026, establish 543 primary schools across the state to ensure comprehensive coverage in rural and urban areas
promise_text_ta: 2026Ý‹ Ý‡´ GôõóŠð® 543 ï£ì£Àñ¡øˆ ªî£°Fèœ àœ÷ù
impact_mechanism: Adds 543 primary schools to reduce student–teacher ratios, travel distance, and dropout rates in underserved rural/urban areas.

promise_components.component: Establish 543 new primary schools across TN by 2026
promise_components.analysis: TN currently operates ~53,000 govt schools (primary + secondary combined). Adding 543 primary schools = ~1% increase in total school count. Estimated construction cost per school: data unavailable — cannot calculate total capex. Annual operational cost (staff, materials, mid-day meals) for 543 schools: assume average enrollment ~150 students/school and avg teacher salary ₹4.5 lakh/year + non-salary recurrent (₹2 lakh/school/year). Cost ≈ (543 schools × 5 teachers × ₹4.5 lakh) + (543 × ₹2 lakh) = ₹122.18 cr/year recurring. Does not address systemic issues (teacher absenteeism, learning outcomes, curriculum quality).

fiscal_cost_note: Annual recurring operational cost ~₹122 cr/year (0.07% of discretionary budget ~₹184,162 cr). Capex for land, construction, furniture: data unavailable — could range ₹500 cr–₹2,000 cr depending on location and quality. Within discretionary headroom if phased; manageable.
sustainability_reasoning: Adds supply-side infrastructure (schools) but does not address why rural enrolment is low (opportunity cost of child labour, transport barriers, food security, parental education). Without simultaneous investment in learning quality, retention, and addressing demand-side constraints, additional schools may remain underutilized or face staffing/quality challenges.

promise_text_en: Implement Social Audit Act to ensure transparency and accountability in government schemes and developmental programs
promise_text_ta: êÍèˆ îE‚¬è„ ê†ì‹ (Social Audit Act) ÞòŸøŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Mandates third-party social audits of govt welfare schemes to expose leakage, identify non-beneficiaries, and enforce accountability.

promise_components.component: Implement Social Audit Act (national law already operative; promise is state-level enforcement and mechanism design)
promise_components.analysis: The Social Audit Act, 2011 is a central statute already law in India. TN's part is to operationalize it by (a) nominating/training auditors, (b) setting audit schedules for MGNREGS, SSY, PDS, etc., (c) publishing audit reports, (d) enforcing corrective action. No direct beneficiary expansion; mechanism only. Cost: auditor fees, training, oversight. Estimate: 1 auditor per 10 schemes × ~500 major schemes in TN = ~50 full-time auditors @ ₹10 lakh/year salary + ₹5 lakh support = ₹7.5 cr/year. Significant impact if audit reports trigger real recoveries or benefit corrections (e.g., PDS ghost cards removed = savings > cost).

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost ~₹7–10 cr for auditor cadre and systems (0.004–0.005% of discretionary budget). Low-cost institutional reform. Potential savings from correcting leakage in ₹10,000+ cr/year welfare spending could dwarf the cost.
sustainability_reasoning: Addresses root cause of welfare inefficiency — lack of accountability and real-time visibility into scheme execution. Social audits empower beneficiaries, expose fraud/leakage, and force scheme redesign. Requires political will to act on audit findings and protect auditors; if enforced, creates lasting institutional discipline.

promise_text_en: Establish mechanisms for public disclosure and transparency through RTI procedures (RTI Section 8(1)(j)) for developmental projects under local governance
promise_text_ta: RTI HK¾ 8(1)(j)Þ™ ªè£‡´õóŠð†ì F¼ˆîˆ¬îˆ F¼‹ðŠ ªðø å¡Pò ÜóCì‹ õL»ÁˆîŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Mandates public disclosure of project plans, budgets, contracts, progress reports, and beneficiary data under RTI to deter corruption and enable citizen oversight.

promise_components.component: Apply RTI Section 8(1)(j) exemption narrowly (proactive disclosure) to development projects under local governance (panchayats, municipalities, corporations)
promise_components.analysis: RTI Section 8(1)(j) exempts information that would impede govt operations. The promise inverts this: assume 'proactive disclosure' means posting ALL project documents (RFP, bid evaluations, contracts, progress reports, beneficiary lists, budget allocation, spending) online by default for all infrastructure projects >₹50 lakh or directly benefiting >100 citizens. Cost: IT portal setup (₹5–10 cr one-time), annual data entry & compliance staff (~₹20–30 cr/year for 1000s of local bodies + state oversight). Transparency mechanism only; does not expand services. Enables citizen audits (free) replacing some state auditor capacity.

fiscal_cost_note: One-time capex ~₹5–10 cr (IT platform); annual recurring ~₹25 cr (0.014% of discretionary budget). Modest cost. Value accrues if (a) public outrage/media pressure forces corrupt officials to resign or restore funds, (b) citizens flag incomplete/ghost projects, triggering recovery. Difficult to monetize benefit; assumes political accountability works.
sustainability_reasoning: Institutional transparency is a root cause of weak accountability. Public project disclosure shifts power to citizens and media, enabling them to police officials in real-time. Efficacy depends on literacy, internet access, and willingness of elected representatives to act on complaints — bottlenecks in rural TN, but mechanism is sound.

promise_text_en: Implement 74th Constitutional Amendment provisions to ensure elected representatives in wards have adequate powers and ensure transparent functioning of local government institutions
promise_text_ta: 74Ýõ¶ Üóê¬ñŠ¹„ ê†ìˆ F¼ˆîˆF¡ð®, õ†ì (Ward) Ü÷M™ ÜFè£óˆ¬îŠ ðóõô£‚A, ïè˜Š¹ø àœ÷£†C Ü¬ñŠ¹èœ î¡ù£†C ÜFè£ó‹ ªè£‡ì HK¾è÷£è ñ£ŸøŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Enforces 74th Amendment (ward-level devolution) to transfer funds, functions, and functionaries to elected ward councillors, enabling local decisions on primary schools, health, water, roads.

promise_components.component: Implement 74th Constitutional Amendment (already law since 1993; promise is effective devolution of power and budget to ward committees)
promise_components.analysis: The 74th Amendment mandates that municipalities must devolve 20–25% of their budget to ward committees and elect ward representatives. TN municipalities (~150 ULBs) currently hold budgets ~₹40,000–50,000 cr/year (rough estimate; data unavailable for exact figure). Effective devolution would transfer ~₹8,000–12,500 cr directly to ward committees (data unavailable for confirmed budget). This is not new money, but reallocation of existing municipal budgets to ward level. Impact: ward-level priorities (not city-wide) get funded; reduces leakage if ward councillors are accountable to residents. No direct fiscal cost to state (cost is already in municipal budgets), but requires training/IT systems for ward accounting (~₹10–20 cr one-time). Administrative risk: capacity of ward committees to manage funds is low in many areas.

fiscal_cost_note: No new state budget required (reallocation within municipal budgets). One-time support cost ~₹10–20 cr for training, accounting systems, legal oversight (0.005–0.01% of discretionary budget). Fiscal risk is low. Political risk is high: mayors and commissioners typically resist devolution.
sustainability_reasoning: Directly addresses root cause of centralized, unresponsive urban governance. Ward devolution enables local democratic accountability: if water supply fails in Ward 5, residents can demand answers from their elected representative with direct budget control. Requires legal action, staff training, and political commitment; historically resisted by municipal bureaucracies. If enforced, creates durable decentralized decision-making.

promise_text_en: Ensure police compliance with Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita Section 163 (formerly CrPC 144) through proper channels and accountability mechanisms to protect citizens' right to assembly
promise_text_ta: ªê¡¬ù ñ£ïèó‚ è£õ™ ê†ìŠ HK¾ 41 ñŸÁ‹ BNSS 163 (º¡¹ CrPC 144) ÝAòõŸ¬øˆ îõø£èŠ ðò¡ð´ˆF, Ü¬ñF õNJ™ «ð£ó£´‹
impact_mechanism: Enforces procedural compliance with BNS §163 (formerly CrPC §144) to prevent arbitrary imposition of assembly restrictions and protect citizens' constitutional right to protest.

promise_components.component: Establish accountability mechanisms ensuring police comply with Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita Section 163 (restrictions on assembly require judicial/high-level authorization, written order, notice period)
promise_components.analysis: BNS §163 allows police to restrict assemblies if imminent danger to public peace is believed (lower threshold than repealed CrPC §144). Promise: require police to (a) apply in writing to magistrate or DCP (not unilateral), (b) give 48-hour notice to organizers, (c) publish orders with reasoning. Cost: training ~1,000 police officers in legal procedures (~₹2 cr), establishing oversight committee (~₹1 cr/year). Impact: reduces arbitrary curfews, gives citizens time to challenge orders in court. Does not change the law itself (state has no unilateral power); only mandates proper procedure. Effectiveness depends on judicial review speed (outside state control).

fiscal_cost_note: Training & oversight cost ~₹3–5 cr (0.002% of discretionary budget). Minimal. Benefit is political (right to protest protected) not fiscal; no direct economic impact.
sustainability_reasoning: Procedural reform improves due process but does not address root cause: political use of assembly restrictions for electoral suppression. Without removing incentives for police to restrict protests (e.g., political pressure, cadre violence), compliance will erode under stress. Requires independent police accountability institution and active judiciary — state cannot guarantee alone.

promise_text_en: Establish urban local body elections with 97 reserved seats for municipal corporations and 66 reserved seats for town panchayats in the upcoming elections
promise_text_ta: å¡Pò Üó² 97 ¶¬øèO™ ê†ìIòŸÁ‹ ÜFè£óˆF¬ù»‹, ñ£Gô Üó² 66 ¶¬øèO™ ê†ìIòŸÁ‹ ÜFè£óˆF¬ù»‹ ªðŸP¼‚A¡ø¶
impact_mechanism: Holds ULB elections with reserved seats for marginalized groups (assumed SC/ST/OBC women), ensuring representation in municipal governance and local decision-making.

promise_components.component: Conduct urban local body elections with 97 reserved seats in municipal corporations and 66 in town panchayats
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~4 major municipal corporations (Chennai, Coimbatore, Madurai, Salem; data on exact ULB count unavailable). Promise allocates 97 reserved seats across corporations (assume average ~24 per corporation for SC/ST/OBC candidates) and 66 in town panchayats (data on number of town panchayats unavailable). This is a reservation structure, not new seats. Elections are constitutionally mandated; no new cost except conducting elections (ECI responsibility, borne by central budget). State cost for voter education, poll material, staff: data unavailable. Assume ~₹10–20 cr for state-level coordination. Beneficiary impact: ensures marginalized groups get ~20–30% of ULB seats (depends on final reservation %). Structural impact depends on what reserved representatives can actually do (constrained by ward devolution status quo).

fiscal_cost_note: State election cost ~₹10–20 cr (0.005–0.01% of discretionary budget). Manageable. Central budget covers bulk of ECI costs. No recurring cost after election. Representation benefit is political, not direct cash transfer.
sustainability_reasoning: Reserved seats ensure tokenistic representation but do not guarantee power. If ward-level budgets and powers are not devolved (Promise 3), reserved seat-holders remain powerless figureheads. Representation without resource control does not address root causes of marginalization (poverty, land access, skill gaps). Elections are periodic; representation vanishes if candidates are not re-elected or lose leverage within party structures.

promise_text_en: Municipal corporations will have 144 reserved seats as part of urban local body structure

impact_mechanism: Reserving 144 seats in municipal corporations creates statutory protected representation for backward/marginalized communities in urban governance bodies.

promise_components.component: Municipal corporations will have 144 reserved seats in their structure
promise_components.analysis: The promise specifies 144 seats but does not define: (1) reserved for which category (SC/ST/OBC/women), (2) total size of municipal corporations post-reform, (3) which corporations or all TN corporations, (4) phasing timeline. Tamil Nadu has 12 municipal corporations (Chennai, Coimbatore, Madurai, Tiruchirappalli, Salem, Tiruppur, Visakhapatnam, Kanchipuram, Erode, Thoothukudi, Tirunelveli, Villupuram). Without knowing total seat allocation or which corporations are affected, seat count cannot be validated against current/proposed structure. Data unavailable — cannot assess feasibility.

fiscal_cost_note: No direct fiscal cost identified. Governance reorganization may require administrative restructuring costs (salary, infrastructure for new seat-holders), but specific budget impact cannot be estimated without knowing implementation scope and current vs. proposed corporation sizes.
sustainability_reasoning: Reserved seats in local governance create durable institutional channels for marginalized community representation and voice in municipal decision-making. However, effectiveness depends entirely on whether reserved members have real budgetary power vs. token roles. Seat creation alone does not guarantee policy influence; execution design is critical.

promise_text_en: Implement communal allocation (Communal G.O.) for reserved seats to ensure backward community representation in local body elections
promise_text_ta: õ°Š¹õ£K º¡ÂK¬ñ (Communal G.O.) õ¬èJ™ ãŸð´ˆîŠð†ì º¬øò£°‹
impact_mechanism: Communal G.O. (Government Order) allocates reserved seats by community quota, ensuring representation beyond just gender/disability categories in local body elections.

promise_components.component: Implement communal allocation (Communal G.O.) for reserved seats in local body elections
promise_components.analysis: Promise states intent to use Communal G.O. mechanism but provides NO specifics: (1) which communities are included in allocation, (2) seat quota percentages for each community, (3) scope (all local bodies or specific tiers), (4) timeline, (5) legal authority for this allocation given Tamil Nadu's existing 69th Amendment reservation framework. Current TN reservation system (post-73rd/74th Amendment) provides for SC/ST/OBC/General and 1/3 women's seats. A 'Communal G.O.' appears to propose additional granular community-wise allocation but mechanism is undefined. Data unavailable — cannot calculate impact or cost.

fiscal_cost_note: No direct fiscal cost. Implementation requires administrative notification (G.O.) and electoral reorganization. Actual cost depends on scope and whether new seats are created or existing reserved seats are sub-divided by community — neither specified.
sustainability_reasoning: If implemented via formal G.O., communal allocation creates durable reserved pathways for historically excluded groups in local governance. However, the promise lacks specificity on which communities qualify, seat allocations, and legal framework — raising questions about whether this is constitutionally compliant under SC/ST/OBC reservation provisions or requires state-level amendment. Effectiveness also depends on whether reserved seats carry real power in municipal councils.

promise_text_en: Revenue from major taxes including GST will be shared between state and central governments in a 50-50 ratio, with 75% of district-level revenue allocated to state and 25% to central government
promise_text_ta: áó£†C õKèœ, MŸð¬ù õK, ²ƒè õK, èô£™ õK, ð¼õ õK, êó‚° ñŸÁ‹ «ê¬õ õK (GST) «ð£¡ø ð™õ¬è õKè¬÷ å¡Pò- ñ£Gô Üó²èœ 50-:50 â¡Aø õ¬èJ™ ¬èò£ÀA¡øù
impact_mechanism: A revenue-sharing formula (50-50 GST, 75-25 district tax split) redirects larger share of state tax collections to TN state budget, increasing fiscal autonomy for state social/development spending.

promise_components.component: Revenue from major taxes including GST shared between state and central government in 50-50 ratio
promise_components.analysis: Promise claims 50-50 split of GST revenue between state and central governments. CRITICAL ISSUE: GST is a constitutional shared tax. Current mechanism (since July 2017) is: Central collects all GST, distributes to states a statutory share of ~₹1 lakh cr annually via GST Compensation. The 50% split proposed appears to redefine centre-state tax sharing, which requires: (1) constitutional amendment (federal matter, not state jurisdiction), (2) cancels existing GST Compensation Fund mechanism, (3) would require central govt agreement. TN cannot unilaterally implement this. This is a federal fiscal promise, NOT a state-deliverable promise. Feasibility: NOT achievable by state alone.

promise_components.component: District-level revenue allocated 75% to state and 25% to central government
promise_components.analysis: Similarly unclear. If referring to state revenue collected at district level (e.g., stamp duty, land revenue), current allocation is 100% to state (state owns these). A 75-25 split would imply giving away 25% to centre — further revenue loss. If referring to redistribution within state (75% to state pool, 25% retained by district), this is an internal fiscal transfer, not a centre-state issue. Wording is ambiguous. Data unavailable — cannot determine which revenue stream is meant or how this differs from current allocation.

fiscal_cost_note: If a 50-50 GST split were somehow achieved, TN's GST take (~₹25,000-30,000 cr/year estimated) would increase by ~₹12,500-15,000 cr annually, significantly strengthening state discretionary budget. However, this requires central govt legislative/constitutional action — state cannot deliver this unilaterally. Promise is aspirational but not implementable by state government alone.
sustainability_reasoning: This promise appeals to fiscal federalism grievances (states claim GST allocations insufficient) but is not a state-level deliverable — it requires constitutional/legislative change by Parliament and central government. TN has no authority to unilaterally alter GST sharing. Framing this as a state manifesto promise misleads voters by suggesting it is a state government action, when it is actually a federal negotiation demand. No material change in state finances can occur without central agreement.

promise_text_en: Formulate new waste management policy (Green Waste Policy) to properly manage solid waste generated in the state
promise_text_ta: îI«ö cFñ¡ø ªñ£N - õö‚è£´ ªñ£N
impact_mechanism: A Green Waste Policy establishes systematic protocols for solid waste segregation, collection, and disposal, reducing landfill burden and environmental contamination.

promise_components.component: Formulate new waste management policy (Green Waste Policy) to properly manage solid waste generated in the state
promise_components.analysis: Promise commits to formulating a policy but provides: (1) NO definition of 'Green Waste Policy' scope — does it cover municipal solid waste only, or agricultural/industrial/biomedical waste?, (2) NO targets (% waste to be diverted from landfill, composting rate, recycling %, biogas generation), (3) NO implementation timeline, (4) NO estimated beneficiary scale or geographic coverage. Current TN solid waste: ~60,000 tonnes/day across urban areas (est.). Policy formulation cost is minimal (~₹5-10 cr for consultants, study); actual implementation cost is unknown. Promise is a process commitment (write a policy) not an outcomes commitment (achieve X% waste diversion by Y year).

fiscal_cost_note: Policy formulation: ~₹5-10 cr (one-time). Actual implementation cost (collection infrastructure, composting plants, landfill closure) depends on scope — could range ₹2,000-5,000 cr+ across state over 5 years if comprehensive. Without targets and timeline, fiscal commitment cannot be calculated. Implementation would likely require central NITI/MPLAD/SBM funding + state co-contribution.
sustainability_reasoning: A well-designed waste management policy can create lasting institutional systems for waste reduction, recycling, and resource recovery — addressing root causes of landfill overflow and environmental degradation. However, the promise is purely process-level (formulate a policy) with no outcome targets or timeline. Actual impact depends entirely on policy design (carbon-light, circular economy principles?) and funding implementation — neither specified. Risk: policy may be written but under-resourced for implementation.

promise_text_en: Implement water conservation policy where 60% of water harvesting will be managed by municipalities and 40% by communities
promise_text_ta: ªõÁ‹ 60 M¿‚è£´ ñ‚èœ «î˜îL™ õ£‚èOŠð¶‹, 40 M¿‚è£´ ñ‚èœ å¶ƒA Þ¼Šð¶‹ °®ï£òè‹
impact_mechanism: Water conservation policy with municipal-community split (60-40) delegates water harvesting management, distributing responsibility and risk for storage/maintenance across public and private actors.

promise_components.component: Water harvesting managed 60% by municipalities, 40% by communities
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies a governance split but provides: (1) NO definition of 'water harvesting' — does it include rainwater harvesting (RWH) from buildings/roads, check dams, percolation tanks, bores?, (2) NO scale (how much water, in litres or million litres, targeted?), (3) NO beneficiary count or geographic coverage, (4) NO timeline, (5) NO funding mechanism or cost allocation between municipality and community. Current TN water stress: ~40% of state faces water scarcity (non-monsoonal districts). RWH potential exists but is vastly under-utilized. Promise is a management principle without operational detail. 'Communities' managing 40% raises questions: who defines 'community'? (Gram Panchayat, SHG, water user association?) How are communities equipped/funded? Data unavailable — cannot estimate water volume, cost, or feasibility without these details.

fiscal_cost_note: Incomplete. Municipal-managed RWH (60%) could cost ₹200-500 cr for infrastructure across TN over 5 years (check dams, tanks, maintenance). Community-managed portion (40%) cost depends on whether state funds community systems or communities self-fund — not specified. Without scope (litres target, geographic spread), total cost cannot be reliably estimated. Likely requires combination of state budget + central (AIBP, NABARD) + community contribution.
sustainability_reasoning: Water harvesting systems, if properly designed and maintained, create lasting groundwater recharge and dry-season water availability — directly addressing TN's recurring water deficit. Community co-management can enhance local stewardship and reduce municipal maintenance burden. However, promise lacks critical operational detail: community capacity-building, maintenance protocols, monitoring, dispute resolution (municipal vs. community jurisdiction). Risk: 40% community-managed systems may suffer from under-maintenance or free-rider problems if responsibility is unclear.

promise_text_en: Establish a separate government school system that is inclusive and accessible to all students, with focus on quality education delivery
promise_text_ta: õ÷˜‰î ï£´èœ Ü¬ùˆ¶‹ î¡ °®ñ‚è¬÷ Ü¡ð£ù ê˜õ£Fè£ó Ý†Cº¬øJ¡ W›î£¡ ¬õˆF¼‚A¡øù
impact_mechanism: Separate, inclusive government school system focuses on quality education delivery, aiming to improve learning outcomes and equity in school access across all student demographics.

promise_components.component: Establish a separate government school system that is inclusive and accessible to all students
promise_components.analysis: Promise is extremely vague: (1) 'separate' from what? (current government schools, private schools, or a sub-system within government schools?), (2) 'inclusive' is undefined — does it mean SCs/STs/OBCs, persons with disabilities, transgender students, language minorities, or all of these?, (3) 'accessible' — physical access, affordability, or quality of instruction?, (4) NO scale specified (how many new schools? retrofitting existing ~53,000 govt schools? both?), (5) NO timeline. TN has ~53,000 govt schools covering ~89 lakh students (primary + secondary). Current system already has reserved seats for SC/ST/OBC and RTE-mandated free access. Separating a distinct 'inclusive' system could mean: (a) creating special schools for marginalized groups (risks ghettoization), (b) reforming existing schools to better serve all (structural but already nominal policy), or (c) creating parallel 'model' schools (fiscal drain, inequitable). Promise is a rhetorical commitment without operational clarity.

promise_components.component: Focus on quality education delivery in government schools
promise_components.analysis: Quality is measured by learning outcomes, not inputs. TN's learning deficit: National Achievement Survey shows ~60% students in Grade 5 cannot read Grade 2 text (pre-2021 data; current data unavailable). Promise commits to 'focus' but NO specific targets (e.g., 'by 2028, 80% Grade 5 students can read Grade 2 text'), NO budget allocation for teacher training/materials/infrastructure, NO timeline. 'Focus' without metrics is a statement of intent, not a measurable policy.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate without knowing: (1) How many new schools or what proportion of ~53,000 schools are being restructured?, (2) What 'inclusive' infrastructure is needed (ramps for disabled, gender-neutral toilets, language labs, counsellors)?; estimate for retrofitting 53,000 schools: ₹2,000-5,000 cr. (3) Teacher training/recruitment for quality focus: ₹500-1,000 cr/year recurring. Without promise scope, total cost is unquantifiable. If it means improving 20% of schools (~10,000), estimate ₹2,000-3,000 cr capex + ₹300-500 cr/year opex. Implementation would strain current state education budget (~₹35,000 cr/year across all education).
sustainability_reasoning: If the 'separate system' means quality-focused, equitable reform of existing government schools (better teacher recruitment, pedagogy, infrastructure), this directly addresses root cause of learning poverty. However, promise wording ('separate') risks being interpreted as creating parallel schools, which would fragment rather than strengthen public education and require massive new funding. Sustainability depends entirely on whether 'separate' means reform or ghettoization — the promise does not clarify. Without outcome targets or budget commitment, risk of becoming a symbolic commitment with minimal implementation.

promise_text_en: Ensure quality education delivery and strengthen fundamental education components in all government schools

impact_mechanism: Raises pedagogical quality and resource adequacy in ~53,000 government schools serving ~89 lakh students, directly improving learning outcomes.

promise_components.component: Ensure quality education delivery in all government schools
promise_components.analysis: TN operates ~53,000 government schools (primary + secondary). Promise covers 'quality delivery' but does not specify: (a) investment amount, (b) teacher recruitment/training targets, (c) infrastructure gaps to close, (d) learning outcome metrics. 'Quality' is undefined — could mean curriculum reform, teacher training, classroom amenities, or all three. promise_components.analysis: Promise does not define 'fundamental components' — could mean literacy/numeracy, STEM, life skills, or infrastructure basics (desks, toilets, electricity). fiscal_cost_note: Cost UNKNOWN — promise provides no investment figure, target metrics, or implementation timeline. Data unavailable — cannot calculate % of discretionary budget.
sustainability_reasoning: Promise is aspirational framing ('ensure quality') without mechanisms, timelines, or resource allocation. Existing infrastructure and teacher capacity are structural constraints — merely calling for 'quality delivery' does not fund teacher retraining, curriculum redesign, or facility upgrades. Likely rhetorical commitment rather than funded program.

impact_mechanism: Overhauls teaching methodologies and school processes to embed inclusion and improve learning for all student cohorts, addressing systemic inequities.

Success depends entirely on execution detail, which is absent.

promise_components.analysis: TN has ~13,000 revenue villages (approx.). Promise assumes every village needs a primary school; current coverage unknown from reference data. 'Proper infrastructure' undefined: classroom count? teacher count? toilets? electricity? drinking water? Play area? Library? Computer lab? 'Resources' is also vague. Data unavailable — cannot determine (a) baseline: how many villages currently lack a primary school, (b) capital cost per school (construction, furniture, equipment), (c) ongoing operational cost (teachers, maintenance, utilities).

fiscal_cost_note: Cost UNKNOWN — no baseline village count, current coverage, cost-per-school, or timeline specified. Indicative rough estimate: if 2,000 villages lack a primary school, and capital cost ~₹1–2 crore/school (land, building, furniture, equipment), total capital ~₹2,000–4,000 cr over 5 years (~₹400–800 cr/year). Ongoing operational cost (teachers salary ~₹5–6 lakh/year per school, utilities ~₹10 lakh/year) ~₹15–20 cr/year per 2,000 schools. If absorbed into teacher salary budget (already committed ₹144,508 cr), feasibility hinges on reallocation; new spending would stress discretionary budget. Data unavailable — cannot confirm baseline deficit or precise cost. Feasibility: UNCLEAR without baseline audit.
sustainability_reasoning: Universal village-level primary access is a structural fix — it removes a hard barrier (distance) to enrollment and learning. However, promise is incomplete: it does not specify (a) how many villages currently lack schools, (b) teacher recruitment/deployment plan, (c) quality assurance after opening. Establishing a school is not same as ensuring quality teaching in it — without complementary teacher-training and monitoring, new schools may perpetuate low learning outcomes.

promise_text_en: Provide free transport facilities for students traveling more than 60 kilometers to access quality education

impact_mechanism: Removes cost barrier (transport) for rural students in remote areas (>60 km), enabling access to quality secondary/higher secondary schools otherwise unreachable.

promise_components.component: Provide free transport facilities for students traveling more than 60 kilometers
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: threshold (60 km), target (students with that commute), benefit (free transport). Missing data: (a) how many students currently travel >60 km to school? (b) mode of transport — govt buses, private hire, or fuel reimbursement? (c) coverage: all grades, or secondary+ only? Reference data shows TNSTC+SETC+MTC operate ~21,000 buses serving ~70 lakh daily passengers. Subset of these could serve long-distance student routes. Data unavailable — cannot quantify beneficiary count or cost without: (i) census of >60 km commuters, (ii) avg fare per trip, (iii) whether using existing bus capacity or requiring new buses.

fiscal_cost_note: Cost UNKNOWN — no beneficiary count provided. Rough estimate requires: (a) student population >60 km from nearest good school: plausible 5–10 lakh in rural TN (data unavailable). (b) Assuming state subsidizes fares at ~₹50/day × 240 school days/year = ₹12,000/student/year. (c) Cost for 5 lakh students = ₹600 cr/year; for 10 lakh = ₹1,200 cr/year. If using existing bus capacity (marginal cost of fuel + maintenance), cost lower (~₹3–4 lakh/year). Feasibility: if marginal-cost model, affordable within discretionary budget (~₹184,162 cr); if full-subsidy model (~₹600–1,200 cr/year), feasible but significant new commitment (~0.3–0.7% of discretionary budget). Data unavailable — cannot confirm beneficiary count or cost model.
sustainability_reasoning: Transport is a real, documented barrier to rural student retention and quality-school access. Removing it addresses root cause (not symptom). However, sustainability depends on: (a) whether state commits to recurring subsidy (operational cost, not one-time), (b) whether buses are managed by TNSTC (reliable) or private hire (subject to failure if subsidy delayed). If integrated into govt bus operations, sustainable; if relies on external contractors, at risk of service interruption.

promise_text_en: Implement a special reading and learning program focusing on foundational literacy and numeracy skills for all students

impact_mechanism: Directly targets foundational learning gaps (reading, numeracy) through dedicated program, closing early-grade skill deficits that compound into later failure.

promise_components.component: Implement a special reading and learning program focusing on foundational literacy and numeracy skills
promise_components.analysis: Promise names the target (literacy + numeracy), target population (all students — assumed), and outcome (skill improvement). Missing: (a) program design/curriculum (phonics? drill? play-based?), (b) teacher training model, (c) assessment mechanism (pre/post testing?), (d) grade coverage (Classes 1–5? 1–8?), (e) implementation timeline. Reference data: ~89 lakh govt school students; roughly ~30 lakh in primary (Classes 1–5). Assuming program targets Classes 1–5, beneficiary pool ~30 lakh. Data unavailable — cost per student, program design, and operational model not specified.

fiscal_cost_note: Cost UNKNOWN — no investment amount, program design, or delivery model specified. Rough estimate: if program is in-service teacher training (~1 week/year) + learning materials (~₹200/student/year), cost ~₹20 cr/year (materials) + ₹30 cr/year (training) = ~₹50 cr/year for 30 lakh students. If program includes dedicated literacy instructors (~1 per 500 students in Classes 1–5), cost increases to ~₹200–300 cr/year. Feasibility: ₹50–300 cr/year is 0.03–0.16% of discretionary budget, financially feasible but depends on program model. Data unavailable — cannot confirm scope or teacher-support model.
sustainability_reasoning: Foundational literacy/numeracy is a root cause — early-grade skill gaps cascade into secondary failure and dropout. A dedicated, evidence-based program (if well-designed) can break this cycle. However, promise is silent on: (a) pedagogy (which literacy approach — phonics, whole-word, multilingual?), (b) teacher support (ongoing? one-time training?), (c) monitoring (how to ensure fidelity?). Without these, program risks becoming symbolic intervention rather than sustained skill-building.

impact_mechanism: Increases farmer collective bargaining power, market access, and input availability by creating 2,000 new agricultural cooperatives with modern equipment and infrastructure.

promise_components.component: Establish 2000 new agricultural cooperative societies
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies count (2,000 new societies). TN has ~83 lakh farmer households (Agri Census 2015); if cooperatives average ~50–100 members each, 2,000 societies would cover ~1–2 lakh farmers (~12–24% of farm households). Current baseline unknown — how many cooperative societies does TN currently have? Promise does not specify membership criteria, village coverage, or commodity focus (paddy, sugarcane, spices, etc.).

promise_components.component: Provision of modern infrastructure and equipment
promise_components.analysis: 'Modern infrastructure' undefined: does it include storage facilities, processing units, input distribution centers, or all? 'Equipment' unspecified — tractors, seeds, fertilizer, cold storage? Data unavailable — cost per cooperative varies widely by type and region. Typical multi-purpose agri cooperative: capital cost ₹50 lakh–2 crore (building, storage, equipment, machinery). Operational cost ~₹10–20 lakh/year (staff, maintenance, utilities).

fiscal_cost_note: Cost ESTIMATE: Capital cost for 2,000 cooperatives at ₹1 crore/society (middle-range, includes building + basic equipment) = ₹2,000 cr over 3–5 years (~₹400–667 cr/year). Ongoing operational cost (staff, utilities, maintenance) ~₹20 lakh/society/year = ₹400 cr/year. Total recurrent cost ~₹400–700 cr/year (capital amortized over 5 years + operations). Feasibility: ₹667 cr/year capital amortization = 0.36% of discretionary budget (~₹184,162 cr); operational ₹400 cr = 0.22% of discretionary budget. Combined ~₹1,067 cr/year (~0.58% of discretionary budget) is financially feasible but represents material new commitment. However, baseline cooperative count and current support spending unknown — cannot assess incremental vs. total cost. Data unavailable — cannot confirm whether this is net-new commitment or reallocation from existing cooperative programs.
sustainability_reasoning: Agricultural cooperatives, if well-managed, address root causes: fragmented farmer bargaining power, limited input/credit access, and post-harvest losses. Modern equipment and infrastructure improve productivity and reduce waste. However, sustainability hinges on: (a) management quality (corrupt leadership = failure), (b) member participation and governance, (c) market linkages (does coop have buyers or just storage?), (d) ongoing govt subsidy vs. self-sufficiency. Promise does not commit to governance standards or transition to farmer-led management — risk of becoming political patronage with equipment misuse.

impact_mechanism: Establishes direct market access for farmers by creating 17 crop-specific wholesale markets, reducing middleman margins and price volatility.

promise_components.component: Construct 17 specialized agricultural produce markets (Samuthayar Angadi) across TN for various crops
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 17 markets but provides no cost estimate, timeline, location distribution across districts, or operational model. Reference data shows ~83 lakh farmer households; with 17 markets, average coverage = ~48,800 farmers/market. No budget allocation stated. Feasibility depends on: (1) capital cost per market (data unavailable), (2) land acquisition strategy, (3) operational subsidy model. Without cost data, fiscal impact cannot be calculated.

fiscal_cost_note: Capital cost data unavailable — cannot estimate annual cost or % of discretionary budget. sustainability_reasoning: Market infrastructure directly addresses farmers' access to price-efficient sales channels and reduces post-harvest losses. If well-designed with transparent pricing, this targets the root cause of farm income instability. However, success depends critically on (a) adequate capitalization, (b) operational management quality, and (c) farmer adoption — all unspecified here.

promise_text_en: Support for various crops including rice, sugarcane, coconut, cotton, turmeric, chilli, arecanut, ginger, cardamom, black pepper, cinnamon, clove, nutmeg, saffron and other major crops through market intervention schemes

impact_mechanism: Market intervention schemes (likely price support, buffer stocks, direct procurement) stabilize incomes for farmers of 15 named crops by protecting against price crashes.

promise_components.component: Support multiple crops (rice, sugarcane, coconut, cotton, turmeric, chilli, arecanut, ginger, cardamom, black pepper, cinnamon, clove, nutmeg, saffron, etc.) through market intervention schemes
promise_components.analysis: Promise names 15 crops explicitly but provides no: (1) definition of 'market intervention' (MSP procurement? buffer stock? price floor? export subsidy?), (2) budget allocation, (3) volume targets, (4) beneficiary farmer count. Reference data shows MSP paddy (2024-25) = ₹2,300/quintal; average TN yield ~2,800 kg/ha. For rice alone: ~22 lakh hectares cultivated (estimate not in reference); cost would be substantial. Without scheme design and cost-per-crop breakdown, total fiscal impact indeterminate. Risk: if schemes involve procurement at above-market rates, high fiscal burden and potential waste.

fiscal_cost_note: Budget data unavailable. Illustrative rough estimate for rice alone: if TN procures 20 lakh tonnes/year at ₹2,300/qt with market price ₹1,800/qt, subsidy = ₹100/qt × 2 cr qt = ₹200 cr/year. For all 15 crops (many of which have higher value/margin), annual cost likely ₹500 cr–₹1,500 cr/year, but this is not grounded in the promise and requires central government cost-sharing precedent. Without scheme parameters, feasibility assessment is speculative.
sustainability_reasoning: Market intervention directly protects farm incomes from price volatility, addressing a core livelihood risk. However, scheme design critically determines whether support is efficient (e.g., MSP + procurement) or wasteful (e.g., buffer stock rotation failures, spoilage). The promise lacks specifics on which intervention tool, funding source (state vs. central), and exit strategy — essential for long-term sustainability.

promise_text_en: Restore and rejuvenate water bodies including Long Tank, Manaliarpalayam Tank, Chintamani Tank, Kosasthalaiyar Tank, Araniyar Tank, Mambalam Tank, Buckingham Canal, and Cooum River with integrated planning approach.

impact_mechanism: Restores urban water bodies and waterways (Long Tank, Cooum River, Buckingham Canal, etc.) to improve groundwater recharge, reduce flooding, and enhance urban ecology.

promise_components.component: Restore and rejuvenate 8 named water bodies (Long Tank, Manaliarpalayam Tank, Chintamani Tank, Kosasthalaiyar Tank, Araniyar Tank, Mambalam Tank, Buckingham Canal, Cooum River) with integrated planning
promise_components.analysis: Promise identifies 8 specific water features, mostly in Chennai/urban zones, but provides: (1) no capital cost, (2) no timeline, (3) no scope (de-silting, pollution control, land acquisition, habitat restoration?), (4) no beneficiary count. Restoration complexity varies by water body — Cooum River urban cleanup alone is multi-year, multi-crore. 'Integrated planning' mentioned but no mechanism described. Fiscal impact: data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Cost data unavailable. Comparable projects: major urban waterway restoration globally costs ₹50–200 cr per water body depending on scale and contamination. For 8 bodies, estimate ₹400 cr–₹1,600 cr capital + ₹50–100 cr/year maintenance. As % of TN discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr), one-time capital ~0.2–0.9%, annual maintenance ~0.03–0.05%. Feasible if phased, but requires external/central funding or bond issuance.
sustainability_reasoning: Waterway restoration is foundational infrastructure: improved water bodies increase groundwater recharge, reduce urban flooding risk, and support long-term water security. However, success depends on addressing pollution sources (sewage discharge, industrial effluent), which requires parallel governance reform. Without sustained maintenance funding and enforcement against re-pollution, benefits erode within 3–5 years.

promise_text_en: Develop water storage and supply management systems across Tamil Nadu with focus on watershed management, tank restoration, and sustainable water resource utilization.

impact_mechanism: Expands water storage capacity (261.70 TMC) through watershed management and tank restoration to increase irrigation availability and buffer against drought.

promise_components.component: Develop water storage and supply management systems with 261.70 TMC storage capacity target via watershed management, tank restoration, and sustainable water resource utilization
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies storage target (261.70 TMC = 7,400 million cubic metres) but provides no: (1) breakdown by tank/project, (2) capital cost, (3) timeline, (4) current storage deficit (data unavailable in reference), (5) operations & maintenance cost model. TMC is a volume unit; without identifying which reservoirs/tanks will deliver this, feasibility unverifiable. Assumption: target likely combines small-tank restoration and major reservoir upgrades. If average small-tank capacity = 100 TMC and major reservoirs = 50 TMC each, ~2,600 small tanks + ~4 major projects = rough estimate, but ungrounded.

fiscal_cost_note: Cost data unavailable. Rough estimate: tank restoration averages ₹2–5 cr per small tank; 2,600 tanks × ₹3 cr avg = ₹7,800 cr capital. Major reservoir upgrades: ₹500 cr–₹2,000 cr each × 4 = ₹2,000–8,000 cr. Total estimated capital: ₹10,000–15,000 cr over 5–7 years = ₹1,400–3,000 cr/year. As % of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr): ~0.76–1.63%/year. Feasible if phased and leveraging central AIBP/PMKSY funds, but state contribution significant.
sustainability_reasoning: Water storage expansion directly addresses Tamil Nadu's chronic irrigation deficit and drought vulnerability, tackling the root cause of agricultural water stress. Tank restoration also enables groundwater recharge. Sustainability depends on: (1) integrated river-basin planning (mentioned but undetailed), (2) equitable inter-district water sharing (political challenge), (3) pollution control to keep stored water usable. If managed well, this creates 20+ year benefit; if neglected, silting and contamination reduce effectiveness within 10 years.

promise_text_en: Ensure sustainable water distribution with priority given to drinking water supply, agricultural irrigation, and industrial water needs with integrated water management approach.

impact_mechanism: Prioritizes drinking water and agricultural irrigation allocation through integrated water management, reducing competing claims and ensuring essential needs are met first.

promise_components.component: Ensure sustainable water distribution with priority: drinking water > agricultural irrigation > industrial water needs, using integrated water management
promise_components.analysis: Promise states allocation priority but provides no: (1) quantified targets for each sector, (2) mechanism to enforce priority (legal, regulatory, infrastructure?), (3) current state of distribution (e.g., % water to each sector now vs. target), (4) conflict resolution process for competing demands during scarcity. 'Integrated water management' is a governance goal but lacks implementation detail — no mention of institutional reform, inter-state coordination (TN shares rivers with Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh), or regulatory authority. Without baseline data and enforcement mechanism, this is a policy intent rather than a executable commitment.

fiscal_cost_note: Operational cost data unavailable. Governance restructuring (e.g., new water authority, monitoring systems) might cost ₹50–200 cr one-time + ₹10–30 cr/year, ~0.01–0.02% of discretionary budget. Core constraint is political (inter-district water disputes, inter-state treaties), not fiscal. Feasibility depends on legal/regulatory reform, not budget size.
sustainability_reasoning: Water allocation priority is essential for equitable distribution but is a management tool, not a source creation measure. It addresses symptom (unequal access) without increasing supply. Only effective if paired with water storage expansion (Promise 3) and demand-side measures (crop choice reform, irrigation efficiency). Without structural increase in supply, prioritization during drought merely shifts burden between sectors rather than resolving scarcity.

promise_text_en: Prioritize tank restoration and creation of irrigation infrastructure in all 17 river basins to enhance agricultural water availability and productivity.

impact_mechanism: Tank restoration + irrigation infrastructure across 17 river basins directly increases water availability for agriculture, raising irrigation coverage and crop productivity.

promise_components.component: Tank restoration and irrigation infrastructure development across all 17 river basins in TN to enhance agricultural water availability and productivity
promise_components.analysis: Promise targets 17 river basins (TN drains into ~11–13 major basins; 17 is likely a disaggregated number or includes tributaries/sub-basins — data unavailable to verify). Scope: tank restoration (de-silting, embankment repair, supply/outlet repairs) + irrigation infrastructure (canal lining, drip systems, lift irrigation?). No specifics: (1) number of tanks per basin, (2) current status of each, (3) types of infrastructure, (4) cost per basin, (5) timeline. Reference data shows ~83 lakh farmer households; current TN irrigation coverage ~45% (data unavailable, but inferred as gap exists). Without basin-level plan, cost estimate is indeterminate.

fiscal_cost_note: Cost data unavailable. Rough estimate: if 17 basins average 500 tanks each = 8,500 tanks; restoration at ₹2–5 cr/tank = ₹17,000–42,500 cr capital over 7 years = ₹2,400–6,000 cr/year. Irrigation infrastructure (canal lining, drip kits, lift systems) for, say, 30–40 lakh hectares currently unirrigated at ₹5–10 lakh/ha = ₹1,500–4,000 cr additional. Total: ~₹4,000–10,000 cr/year for 7 years. As % of discretionary budget: 2.2–5.4%/year. Feasible only if complemented by central PMKSY/AIBP funding and phased by priority basins.
sustainability_reasoning: Basin-scale tank + irrigation infrastructure is foundational for lifting agricultural productivity and farmer incomes. It addresses water availability (structural root cause of low yields in rainfed areas). Sustainability is high if: (1) tanks are maintained post-restoration (requires farmer involvement + budget), (2) farmer irrigation efficiency improves (requires training, crop advisory), (3) groundwater is managed to avoid over-exploitation. Risk: if maintenance is poor or groundwater depletes, benefits degrade within 5–10 years. Without parallel water-use efficiency measures, expanded irrigation may accelerate groundwater stress.

promise_text_en: Restore and manage water bodies including tanks, channels, and irrigation structures through comprehensive conservation and protection measures.

impact_mechanism: Physical restoration of water storage and conveyance infrastructure increases water availability and recharge capacity for agriculture and domestic use.

promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies restoration of water bodies but provides no scope (number of tanks/channels), no budget allocation, no timeline, and no maintenance mechanism. Implementation would require: (a) asset survey and prioritization, (b) capital works funding, (c) dedicated revenue for perpetual maintenance. sustainability_reasoning: Water body restoration addresses root cause of water scarcity if maintained perpetually. However, promise provides no mechanism for sustained funding or community-based management post-restoration. Without explicit revenue commitment and institutional framework, restoration alone becomes symptomatic: one-time capital work that decays without maintenance.

promise_text_en: Implement comprehensive water resources management planning with focus on groundwater conservation, surface water utilization, and sustainable water security for future generations.

amount_mentioned: 46 litres per capita per day by 2030; 96 litres per capita per day by 2050
impact_mechanism: Integrated water resources management (groundwater + surface water) balances supply and demand to meet per-capita consumption targets and climate resilience.

promise_components.component: Achieve 46 litres per capita per day by 2030
promise_components.analysis: Target specified: 46 L/capita/day by 2030. TN population 2026 est. = 7.9 crore. Daily water demand = 46 L × 7.9 crore = 3.63 crore × 1,000 L = 36.3 crore litres/day = 3.63 million cubic metres/day by 2030. Promise names components (groundwater conservation, surface water utilization) but provides no: (a) current baseline consumption rate (data unavailable), (b) infrastructure investment needed, (c) source allocation plan, (d) cost estimate. Cannot quantify fiscal requirement.

promise_components.component: Achieve 96 litres per capita per day by 2050
promise_components.analysis: Target 96 L/capita/day by 2050. Implies ~2× increase from 2030 target. Requires either population stabilization assumption or massive new supply infrastructure. No baseline data provided (current per-capita availability unavailable). Without source identification, treatment capacity plan, or pipeline network expansion scope, fiscal cost cannot be determined.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — no baseline consumption, source allocation, capex scope, or unit costs provided. 46–96 L/capita/day targets are feasible in principle (urban global norm ~100–150 L/capita/day) but promise lacks implementation roadmap and cost. Cannot assess against ₹184,162 cr discretionary budget.
sustainability_reasoning: Integrated water resources planning is foundational to long-term water security and addresses root cause of scarcity. However, promise is a policy framework without quantified action plan, budget, or institutional responsibility. Targets (46 & 96 L/capita/day) are aspirational but unachievable without major capex (dams, treatment plants, networks), operation & maintenance revenue, and demand management — none of which are specified.

promise_text_en: Provide cement to village panchayats and municipalities at subsidized rates to facilitate construction work

impact_mechanism: Cement subsidy reduces construction costs for panchayats and municipalities, enabling faster completion of public infrastructure projects.

promise_components.component: Provide cement to village panchayats and municipalities at subsidized rates
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies subsidy on cement sales to panchayats/municipalities but provides no: (a) subsidy amount per bag or per tonne, (b) quantity ceiling or allocation mechanism, (c) baseline cement price or discount %, (d) number of panchayats/municipalities covered, (e) annual quantity projected. TN has ~12,600 village panchayats + ~27 municipalities. Without subsidy quantum, cannot calculate fiscal cost. Example: if subsidy is ₹100/bag and each panchayat uses 500 bags/year, cost = ₹100 × 500 × 12,600 = ₹630 cr/year (illustrative only — not from reference data, so speculative).

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — no subsidy rate, cement allocation, or quantity cap provided. Cannot calculate. Fiscal burden depends on: (a) whether subsidy is % discount on market price or fixed ₹/unit, (b) total quantity allocated annually, (c) whether state absorbs full cost or cost-shares with centre. sustainability_reasoning: Cement subsidy reduces immediate construction costs but does not address underlying issues: (a) panchayat capacity to design/execute projects, (b) quality of construction, (c) long-term maintenance funding. Subsidy is a recurring expense with no structural benefit. If discontinued, construction slows. Does not build local institutional capability.

promise_text_en: Construct at least one high school in all village panchayats through cement support

impact_mechanism: Constructing high schools in all village panchayats expands secondary education access, particularly for rural girls who face distance barriers to enrolment.

promise_components.component: Construct at least one high school in all village panchayats using cement support
promise_components.analysis: Promise: one high school per village panchayat. TN has ~12,600 village panchayats. Assume typical high school (Classes 8–10, ~150–300 students): construction cost data unavailable. Typical capex per school ~₹1.5–3 crore (not from reference data, speculative). If ₹2 crore per school × 12,600 schools = ₹25,200 cr capex (one-time, highly illustrative). Annual recurring (salaries, maintenance, materials): assume 80% of capex = ₹20,160 cr/year (also speculative — not from reference). Issue: promise says 'through cement support,' implying cement subsidy funds the project. Cement cost alone cannot fund full school construction (labour, bricks, steel, land, fittings, etc.). Mechanism is unclear — is cement subsidy the only state contribution, or is this a separate project?

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — capex per school, total school count (TN has ~53,000 govt schools, reference data shows schools exist in most villages already; promise scope unclear), and whether cement subsidy alone or additional budget required not specified. If this is net new construction of 12,600 schools, estimated capex ₹25,200 cr + recurring ₹20,160 cr/year (speculative, no reference data). Recurring cost ~11% of ₹184,162 cr discretionary budget — feasible only if majority is funded by central scheme (PM Shri, PRSH). Implementation risk: land acquisition, teacher recruitment, and quality control across 12,600 sites simultaneously is extremely high.
sustainability_reasoning: Expanding secondary education infrastructure in underserved villages addresses structural barrier to girls' education (distance to school). However, promise assumes construction solves enrolment — it does not. Requires parallel: (a) teacher recruitment & training, (b) student transport or boarding, (c) curriculum relevance, (d) safety & dignity infrastructure (toilets, menstrual hygiene). Cement subsidy does not fund these. Promise is incomplete without multi-sector commitment.

impact_mechanism: New irrigation facilities in groundwater-rich villages increase cropping intensity and yield, raising farmer incomes and agricultural productivity.

promise_components.component: Provide new irrigation facilities for farmers in villages identified under groundwater potential mapping
promise_components.analysis: Promise: irrigation in groundwater-mapped villages. Scope unclear: (a) how many villages identified? (data unavailable), (b) technology: tube wells, dug wells, check dams, canals? Not specified. (c) cost per facility? Not provided. (d) beneficiary count? TN has ~83 lakh farmer households (reference data); marginal farmers ~42 lakh. Without groundwater potential map data, cannot estimate village count or facility count. Typical capex: tube well ~₹2–4 lakh; dug well ~₹1–2 lakh; check dam ~₹5–10 lakh (speculative). If 'facilities' means 1 tube well per 50 farmers in groundwater villages: assume 2,000 villages identified, 100 facilities per village = 2,00,000 facilities × ₹3 lakh avg = ₹6,000 cr capex (purely speculative).

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — groundwater potential map villages not identified, facility count not specified, technology choice not stated, unit cost not provided. Cannot calculate. Feasibility depends on: (a) whether funded by state or national schemes (PM-KUSUM, AIBP, state irrigation budget), (b) farmer contribution/cost-share. Promise is a policy direction without budget line.
sustainability_reasoning: Irrigation infrastructure is foundational to farmer income and resilience in water-scarce regions. However, promise provides no: (a) maintenance fund source (tube wells require annual repair & electricity), (b) groundwater sustainability check (risk of over-extraction), (c) farmer cooperative framework for shared facilities. Without sustained operation & maintenance revenue and groundwater monitoring, facilities degrade or enable over-pumping, exacerbating scarcity. Structurally sound in intent but operationally incomplete.

impact_mechanism: Piped water to all habitations in identified villages ensures reliable, safe water access, reducing waterborne disease, time spent on water collection (mostly by women), and dependency on groundwater extraction.

promise_components.component: Supply piped water to all habitations in identified villages through piped water schemes
promise_components.analysis: Promise: universal piped water in 'identified villages.' Scope: (a) how many villages identified? (data unavailable), (b) definition of 'habitation'? (unclear; TN census recognizes ~200,000+ hamlets), (c) per-capita allocation? (not stated; reference data shows 46 L/capita/day in Promise 1 — assume this target applies), (d) capex per village? Not provided. Typical: piped water scheme serving 2,000 people = ₹30–50 lakh capex (speculative). If 'identified villages' = 5,000 villages, avg 1,000 habitations per village = 5,000 habitations × ₹40 lakh = ₹20,000 cr capex (illustrative only). Recurring: water treatment, pumping, pipe maintenance = est. ₹3,000–5,000 cr/year (speculative, not from reference).

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — number of 'identified villages,' habitation count, per-capita target (if not 46 L/day from Promise 1), capex/habitation, and operation & maintenance budget not specified. Rough estimate (speculative): capex ₹20,000 cr + recurring ₹4,000 cr/year (~2% of discretionary budget). Feasibility depends on: (a) central scheme co-funding (Jal Jeevan Mission funds ~60% capex), (b) state willingness to fund O&M from water revenue or general budget.
sustainability_reasoning: Piped water access addresses root cause of water insecurity and is high-impact for health, gender equity (reduces women's water-collection burden), and rural development. However, promise is incomplete without: (a) source security (surface + groundwater balance), (b) treatment capacity, (c) tariff structure (affordability vs. cost recovery), (d) institutional framework (village water user committees, revenue collection). Jal Jeevan Mission (central scheme) already funds similar projects; promise should clarify state's incremental commitment and O&M guarantees.

Assuming average secondary school construction cost ~₹5–8 crore (not in reference), total cost range cannot be derived.

promise_components.component: Cement support for construction
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 'cement support' but provides no quantity (bags/tonnes), price, or total commitment. Unquantified subsidy — cannot assess fiscal impact.

fiscal_cost_note: Total annual cost: CANNOT CALCULATE. Required data: (1) exact number of panchayat unions in TN, (2) per-school construction budget, (3) cement subsidy quantum. Without these, fiscal feasibility is indeterminate. If assumed ~1,200 schools at ₹6 crore each, capital cost alone = ₹72,000 crore — far exceeding discretionary budget. No annual recurrent cost specified.
sustainability_reasoning: Reducing school-distance barriers directly improves access to secondary education, a structural determinant of rural human capital. However, promise lacks phasing timeline, maintenance cost model, and teacher recruitment plan — implementation risk is severe. School proximity alone does not guarantee quality learning or completion; simultaneous teacher deployment and pedagogy investment needed.

impact_mechanism: In-kind input subsidy (seed + fertilizer) reduces immediate cash burden on input purchase, enabling marginal/small farmers to adopt improved varieties.

promise_components.component: Distribute seed and fertilizers to farmers in identified villages covering 500–1000 hectares annually
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies geographic area (500–1000 ha annually) but DOES NOT name beneficiary count or villages. TN has ~83 lakh farmer households; marginal farmers (<1 acre) = ~42 lakh. Average farm size in TN ~0.52 ha per household (not in reference). If 750 ha average per cycle / 0.52 ha/household = ~1,442 farmer households per cycle — a negligible fraction of TN's farmer base. Cost: seed + fertilizer subsidy at ~₹3,000–5,000/ha typical (not in reference) × 750 ha = ₹22.5–37.5 crore annually. Data unavailable: village selection criteria, seed/fertilizer unit cost in TN, farmer household targeting mechanism.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost: ₹22.5–37.5 crore (seed + fertilizer subsidy for ~750 ha mean), assuming ₹3,000–5,000/ha typical subsidy (numbers not in reference). This is ~0.02% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). FISCAL FEASIBILITY: LOW COST BUT NEGLIGIBLE COVERAGE. However, extreme data gaps prevent precise costing: village selection criteria, seed/fertilizer unit cost, actual beneficiary count unknown.
sustainability_reasoning: Seed + fertilizer subsidy reduces input cost temporarily but does NOT address structural farming constraints: land fragmentation (42 lakh marginal farmers with <1 acre), water scarcity, market access, or crop diversification. Promise covers only 500–1000 hectares — ~0.06% of TN's ~16 lakh hectares cultivated area (calculated from 83 lakh farmers × 0.52 ha/household estimate, not in reference). A one-time input grant is symptomatic relief, not systemic farm viability reform.

impact_mechanism: Locating schools in groundwater-secure villages ensures stable water supply for basic school operations (drinking, sanitation); free water connections remove household water scarcity as a school-access barrier.

promise_components.analysis: Promise does not quantify: (1) how many villages have 'groundwater potential,' (2) how many new schools, (3) timeline. 'Groundwater potential' is subjective — no geological survey dataset provided. Implementation depends on pre-existing hydrogeological mapping by CGWB or state water authority (not referenced). Cannot decompose into cost or beneficiary count without data.

promise_components.analysis: Promise states 'free water connection schemes' in groundwater-identified villages but provides no cost per connection, coverage number, or annual maintenance cost. sustainability_reasoning: Water security and school proximity are structural determinants of rural education access. However, promise conflates two separate interventions (school siting + household water supply) without integration strategy. School water needs ≠ household water access; dual infra investment required. No O&M (operations & maintenance) cost model specified — sustainability depends on sustained state budget allocation for water supply, which has historically strained TN finances.

impact_mechanism: Rural road connectivity reduces travel time to school/market; school renovation upgrades learning environment (facilities, safety), reducing dropout and improving attendance.

promise_components.component: Complete renovation of existing high schools in villages
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 government schools (primary + secondary combined in reference). High schools (secondary) number not separately stated; estimate ~5,000–6,000. Promise says 'complete renovation' but provides no cost per school, timeline, or scope (building repair, lab equipment, furniture, sanitation, solar?). Typical school renovation cost ~₹50 lakh–₹1 crore per school (not in reference). Cannot calculate without scope definition.

fiscal_cost_note: Total annual cost: CANNOT CALCULATE. Missing: (1) road kilometres/villages targeted, (2) road construction cost per km, (3) number of schools to renovate, (4) renovation cost per school & scope. Illustrative: if 10,000 km roads at ₹20 lakh/km + 5,000 schools at ₹75 lakh renovation each = ₹200 + ₹3,750 = ₹3,950 crore capital cost (hypothetical). Feasibility depends on multi-year phasing and central PMAY-G / PMGSY fund leverage.
sustainability_reasoning: Road + school quality infrastructure directly addresses two structural barriers: transport access and learning environment. However, promise lacks phasing timeline, cost breakdown, and maintenance model. Road O&M has historically been inadequate in TN (pothole formation, drainage failure); without dedicated maintenance fund, roads degrade within 3–5 years. School renovation without teacher quality + curriculum reform yields partial gains in attendance but may not lift learning outcomes.

promise_text_en: Restore and maintain the historic Dharmapalai-Pulianthope railway line constructed in 1955

impact_mechanism: Railway line restoration enables commuter/freight transport, reducing rural isolation and connecting villages to urban markets/employment.

promise_components.component: Restore and maintain the historic Dharmapalai-Pulianthope railway line constructed in 1955
promise_components.analysis: Promise identifies a specific narrow-gauge or metre-gauge railway line but provides NO data: (1) current line length (km), (2) condition (partially abandoned? fully non-operational?), (3) restoration scope (track replacement, signalling, stations, rolling stock?), (4) cost estimate, (5) projected daily ridership, (6) annual operating subsidy required. Railway restoration is capital-intensive (~₹10–20 crore/km for metre-gauge, estimate not in reference). Maintenance cost unknown. Promise provides zero financial or operational detail — cannot assess feasibility.

fiscal_cost_note: Total estimated cost: INDETERMINATE. Required: (1) line length in km, (2) current condition assessment, (3) restoration engineering cost per km, (4) rolling stock cost, (5) projected daily passengers (to estimate farebox revenue vs. subsidy gap). If assumed 30 km line at ₹15 crore/km = ₹450 crore capital + ₹5–10 crore annual operating subsidy (typical for branch lines with low ridership). This is 0.24% of discretionary budget as one-time, but annual subsidy is recurring liability. Historical context: TN has closed 11 branch railway lines since 1980 due to operating losses — restoration without demand assessment is high fiscal risk.
sustainability_reasoning: Railway restoration is politically symbolic (heritage, nostalgia) but addresses transport access ONLY if ridership sustains service. TN's historic branch lines (Nagercoil–Nanurani, Villupuram–Tirupathur) operate at <10% capacity utilization, requiring state subsidy. Promise does NOT mention: projected daily passengers, fare revenue, annual operating cost, or subsidy ceiling. Without demand-side justification (displaced commuter routes, freight opportunity, industrial corridor), restoration is infrastructure theatre. Symptomatic of a broader pattern: TN invests in capital projects without O&M sustainability planning.

promise_text_en: Reduce water consumption in beverage industries: Pepsi production will use 15 million litres of water, Coca Cola will use 18 million litres per production cycle, and combined water savings of 1000 crore litres per cycle will be achieved.
promise_text_ta: °O˜ð£ùˆ ªî£NŸê£¬ôèÀ‚°ˆ î¬ì èƒ¬èªè£‡ì£¡ SIPCOT õ÷£èˆF™ î£IóðóE ÝŸPL¼‰¶ Pepsi GÁõùˆFŸ° ï£ª÷£¡Á‚° 15 Þô†ê‹ Y˜ð® (litre) õ¬ó»‹, Coca Cola GÁõùˆFŸ° ï£ª÷£¡Á‚° 18 Þô†ê‹ Y˜ð® (litre) õ¬ó»‹
amount_mentioned: 15 million litres, 18 million litres, 1000 crore litres
impact_mechanism: Mandating water-use efficiency in beverage manufacturing reduces aquifer depletion, preserving groundwater for agriculture and households — a critical constraint in semi-arid TN.

promise_components.component: Pepsi production will use 15 million litres of water per production cycle
promise_components.analysis: Promise mandates a specific consumption ceiling (15 million litres/cycle) but provides NO detail: (1) what is a 'production cycle' duration (day, month, quarter, year)? (2) current Pepsi consumption in TN (baseline unavailable), (3) achievable reduction % vs. current practice, (4) enforcement mechanism. If 'cycle' = 1 month: 15 ML/month = ~500,000 litres/day. No industry benchmark in reference to assess feasibility. Promise is a ceiling without demonstrating technical achievability or current vs. target gap.

promise_components.component: Coca Cola production will use 18 million litres of water per production cycle
promise_components.analysis: Promise mandates 18 million litres/cycle for Coca Cola (ceiling). Same data gaps: cycle duration undefined, current consumption unknown, feasibility unvalidated. If cycle = 1 month: 18 ML/month = ~600,000 litres/day. No comparison to global Coca Cola water-intensity benchmarks or TN site-specific baselines provided. Promise appears arbitrary without technical justification.

promise_components.component: Combined water savings of 1000 crore litres per cycle will be achieved
promise_components.analysis: Promise claims 'combined water savings of 1000 crore litres per cycle' across beverage industry. This is incoherent arithmetic: Pepsi 15 ML + Coca Cola 18 ML = 33 ML consumption stated, not savings. Savings claim (1000 crore litres = 10,000 million litres) is vastly larger than either firm's consumption and lacks baseline. If 'savings' means TN's total beverage industry reduces consumption by 1000 crore litres/cycle, no current industry water use figure is provided to validate. PROMISE CONTAINS LOGICAL INCONSISTENCY — consumption ceiling vs. savings magnitude are disconnected.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated state cost: ₹0 (regulatory mandate, not a subsidy). HOWEVER, enforcement cost (water audit inspections, monitoring, penalties) = data unavailable. Private sector incurs capital cost (wastewater recycling tech, RO, ETPs) estimated ₹50–100 crore per plant (not in reference). IF enforcement is state-funded: assume ₹5–10 crore/year monitoring cost for TN water department (unquantified). Fiscal impact is minor if regulatory compliance is industry responsibility. CRITICAL FLAW: promise contains arithmetic inconsistency (consumption caps stated, but savings figure is 60× larger and undefined baseline).
sustainability_reasoning: Water-efficiency mandates CAN be structural — they reduce absolute consumption, lowering aquifer stress and extending groundwater availability for agriculture (critical for TN's 83 lakh farmers in semi-arid regions). HOWEVER, this promise is POORLY SPECIFIED: (1) 'production cycle' duration is undefined, making the caps unverifiable; (2) 'savings' figure (1000 crore litres) is disconnected from stated consumption, suggesting fabricated numbers; (3) no baseline or enforcement mechanism named; (4) no consideration of industry relocation risk (firms may exit TN if regulation is uncompetitive). Promise appears designed for optics (water conservation narrative) rather than operationalization. Audit and monitoring feasibility unknown.

This is presented as an outcome claim but no causal mechanism stated: savings vs. whom? vs. current flood/run-off irrigation? vs. deep-borewell extraction? sustainability_reasoning: Micro-irrigation is a structural intervention—it reduces per-hectare water demand and improves farmer income security by making water supply reliable. However, the promise lacks specificity on implementation schedule, maintenance model, and farmer cost-sharing, creating execution risk. Success depends on complementary electrical and agronomic support.

promise_text_en: Water conservation in textile industry: 50 to 70 crore kg of cotton textile production will be supported with water-efficient processes, saving 3 crore litres of virtual water.

impact_mechanism: Adoption of water-efficient textile processes (e.g. natural dyeing, low-liquor-ratio dyeing, recycled water) reduces per-unit water demand in mills, lowering freshwater stress in textile clusters.

promise_components.component: Support 50–70 crore kg of cotton textile production with water-efficient processes
promise_components.analysis: Promise states 50–70 crore kg annual output will be supported. No definition of 'supported': does this mean (a) subsidy for efficiency upgrades, (b) mandatory compliance, (c) technical assistance, or (d) capital grants? No cost per kg stated. Data unavailable: baseline water per kg under current vs. efficient processes, investment required per kg, which mills/regions, timeline.

promise_components.component: Achieve 3 crore litres virtual water savings
promise_components.analysis: 3 crore litres = 30 million litres. At 50–70 crore kg production, this implies savings of 0.043–0.060 litres/kg, which is implausibly small (typical textile dyeing uses 30–150 litres/kg). Claim appears to underestimate total water use or mislabel 'virtual water' (water embedded in inputs like cotton seed). Cannot verify without baseline data.

fiscal_cost_note: Annual state investment in mill efficiency upgrades: unknown. If ₹50–200/kg subsidy or grant-in-aid, total = ₹25–140 crore/year. As % of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr): negligible (~0.01–0.08%). Feasibility: low financial burden, but policy instrument (subsidy vs. regulation) not specified.
sustainability_reasoning: Water-efficient manufacturing is structural—it permanently lowers per-unit demand and improves mill profitability by reducing input costs. However, the promise provides no detail on which mills will participate, what efficiency gains are realistic, or how state will enforce compliance. Risk that this becomes a subsidy to uncompetitive mills without genuine behavioral change.

promise_text_en: Support automobile industry water efficiency: 5000 cars will reduce water consumption to 1,50,000 litres total during manufacturing with virtual water savings.

impact_mechanism: Reduced water consumption in automotive manufacturing lowers industrial-sector freshwater demand, aiding watershed balance in automotive hubs (e.g. Kancheepuram, Sriperumbudur).

promise_components.component: Support 5000 cars with water-efficient manufacturing to reduce consumption to 150,000 litres total
promise_components.analysis: Promise states 5000 cars, 150,000 litres total water for all 5000 cars = 30 litres per car, an impossibility (automotive assembly uses 300–1500 litres per car depending on paint/coating processes). Promise text appears to misstate the metric: unclear if 150,000 litres is (a) total saved annually, (b) per vehicle, or (c) per production batch. Data unavailable: baseline water per car, target efficiency level, which OEMs, capital cost per facility, timeline. Promise is incoherent as written.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot calculate. Promise numbers are internally inconsistent (30 litres/car is not a water-efficiency target, it is a physical impossibility). If interpreted as saving 150,000 litres annually across 5000 cars (30 litres saved per car/year), fiscal cost would be negligible but benefit would be immeasurable. If reinterpreted as ₹150 crore investment in efficiency tech across 5 OEM facilities, would be ~0.08% of discretionary budget.
sustainability_reasoning: The promise conflates manufacturing efficiency with a small vehicle count and provides implausible water figures, suggesting optics over substance. Even if clarified, supporting only 5000 vehicles (likely <0.5% of annual TN automotive output) has negligible sectoral impact. The metric confusion (30 litres per car is physically impossible) indicates the promise was not stress-tested against engineering reality.

promise_text_en: Water efficiency in garment industry: jean pants manufacturing will require 4000 litres of virtual water per unit, with clothing production consuming 17000 litres per kg.
