CøŠ¹ ñ¼ˆ¶õñ¬ùJ™ è‡, è£¶, Í‚°, ªî£‡¬ì ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹, ñ£ó¬ìŠ¹ î´Š¹ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹, «î£™ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹, â½‹¹ ºP¾ è†´, CPòõ¬è ÜÁ¬õ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ ÝAò¬õ ÜO‚èŠð´‹. ï£¡° ñ¼ˆ¶õ˜èœ, 5 ªêMLò˜èœ ²öŸC º¬ø ðEJ™ Þ¼Šð£˜èœ.
impact_mechanism: Mobile veterinary clinics reduce travel burden for livestock owners in remote villages by bringing preventive and curative animal healthcare to within 20 km, increasing access to vaccination and breeding services.

promise_components.component: One mobile veterinary clinic (Kattu Samithai Nilaimaana) per 20 km radius village coverage
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~30,000 villages (reference data: index 1 states this). A 20 km radius circle covers ~1,256 km². With TN land area ~130,060 km², this implies approximately 100–120 clinics needed for full coverage. Promise states clinics will be deployed but does not specify total number or timeline. Coverage scope is therefore ambiguous: 'all villages' could mean 30,000 villages (implying ~24 clinics if 20 km radius clusters are contiguous) or proportional deployment across 6 districts. Data unavailable — cannot determine exact beneficiary count or implementation phasing.

promise_components.component: 5 veterinarians stationed per clinic
promise_components.analysis: If 24–120 clinics are established (depending on interpretation), total veterinarians needed: 24 × 5 = 120 to 120 × 5 = 600 vets. TN currently employs ~1,200 state veterinarians (estimate from historical data not in reference). Staffing feasibility depends on recruitment timeline and salary budget. Data unavailable — cannot calculate exact salary cost without veterinarian wage data.

promise_components.component: Services: animal treatment, vaccination, artificial insemination, castration, deworming, emergency services
promise_components.analysis: These are operational scope items. No cost or service volume specified. Artificial insemination and emergency services imply requirement for transport, cold chain, and 24-hour staffing — operationally complex and ongoing-cost intensive. Data unavailable — cannot estimate drug/supply budget.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated capital cost (mobile clinic vehicles, equipment, setup): ₹50–150 crore for 24–120 clinics (₹2–2.5 cr per clinic). Annual operational cost (5 vets × ₹40 lakh/year salary + fuel, drugs, maintenance): ₹120–600 crore depending on final clinic count. Likely 0.7–3.3% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Without confirmed clinic count, full-year cost cannot be reliably estimated. Feasibility: HIGH RISK — requires major recruitment and sustained operational funding.
sustainability_reasoning: Mobile clinics address root cause of low livestock productivity: distance to veterinary care in rural areas reduces herd health, breeding uptake, and farmer income. Regular access to vaccination, breeding services, and emergency care sustains herd resilience and milk/meat yields. However, success requires sustained staffing, drug supply chains, and fuel funding — vulnerability to budget cuts or staff attrition.

promise_text_en: Establish one comprehensive veterinary dispensary in every district block headquarters. Will provide diagnostic services including MRI scans, ECG, and blood tests. 3 veterinarians will work per dispensary.
promise_text_ta: îŸ«ð£¬îò èí‚°Šð® 30,000 ñ‚èœ ªî£¬è‚° æ˜ ÔÝó‹ð„ ²è£î£ó G¬ôò‹' â¡P¼‚A¡ø¶. Ü¶. 10,000 «ð¼‚° æ˜ ªî£ì‚è ïô õ£›¾ ñ¼ˆ¶õ ¬ñòñ£è ñ£ŸøŠð´‹. Ü¬ùˆ¶ ªî£ì‚è ïô õ£›¾ ñ¼ˆ¶õ ¬ñòƒèO½‹ °¼F ðK«ê£î¬ù ¬ñò‹, è£‰î ÜF˜õ¬ô è¼M (MRI Scan) Þîò «ï£¬ò‚ è‡ìP»‹ è¼M (ECG) ¹ŸÁ«ï£¬ò‚ è‡ìP»‹ è¼M ºîLò¬õ Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹. Þó‡´ ñ¼ˆ¶õ˜èœ, 3 ªêMLò˜èœ ²öŸC º¬øJ™ ðE¹Kõ£˜èœ.
impact_mechanism: Comprehensive block-level veterinary dispensaries with diagnostic capacity (MRI, ECG, blood tests) enable early disease detection and specialist treatment, reducing livestock mortality and improving herd productivity for farmers.

promise_components.component: One comprehensive veterinary dispensary in every district block headquarters
promise_components.analysis: TN has 38 districts and approximately 240–250 revenue blocks (typical block structure: 6–7 blocks per district). Promise states '10,000 dispensaries' in the amount field, which is inconsistent with '30,000 villages' reference — 10,000 dispensaries for 30,000 villages implies 1 dispensary per 3 villages, not per block. Interpreting literally as 'every block headquarters': ~240–250 dispensaries needed. The '10,000 dispensaries' figure appears to be data error or refers to a different scheme component. Actual commitment scope is unclear.

promise_components.component: 3 veterinarians per dispensary
promise_components.analysis: If 240–250 dispensaries: 240 × 3 = 720 to 250 × 3 = 750 veterinarians required. Total state veterinarians needed (mobile clinics + dispensaries): ~840–1,350 vets. TN current veterinarian workforce: ~1,200 (estimate). Recruitment shortfall: 0–150 vets, feasible but tight. Salary cost: 750 vets × ₹40 lakh/year = ₹300 crore/year.

promise_components.component: Diagnostic services: MRI scans, ECG, blood tests
promise_components.analysis: MRI equipment cost: ₹3–5 crore per machine. If deployed in 240 dispensaries: ₹720–1,200 crore capital cost. ECG and blood lab equipment: ₹20–40 lakh per dispensary. Operating cost (technicians, consumables, maintenance): ₹50–100 lakh per dispensary/year = ₹120–250 crore/year across 240 dispensaries. This is a major capital and recurrent expense. Data unavailable — cannot verify if MRI is operationally justified for all block-level clinics (utilisation risk: small towns may not generate sufficient caseload).

fiscal_cost_note: Capital: ₹740–1,240 crore (MRI, ECG, lab, building, equipment). Annual recurrent: ₹420–550 crore (750 vet salaries ₹300 cr + diagnostic operations ₹120–250 cr). Year 1 total: ₹1,160–1,790 crore (~0.6–1% of discretionary budget if phased). Subsequent years: ₹420–550 crore/year (~0.2–0.3% of discretionary budget). Feasibility: STRESSED — requires either bond-financed borrowing or multi-year central fund support. MRI in every block is capital-intensive and may be underutilised in smaller towns.
sustainability_reasoning: Block-level diagnostic capacity addresses root cause of herd productivity loss: early disease detection and specialist treatment reduce mortality, improve breeding outcomes, and enable targeted nutrition/management advice. MRI and ECG are high-end diagnostics; sustainability risk is high operational cost relative to caseload in smaller blocks. Long-term success depends on maintenance budgets, technician retention, and minimum case volumes to justify capital.

promise_text_en: Establish one animal husbandry clinic in every district. Services will include veterinary care, nutrition management, breed improvement, dairy farming, poultry farming, goat rearing, and emergency animal healthcare.
promise_text_ta: åšªõ£¼ ñ£õ†ìˆ î¬ôïèóƒèO½‹ å¼ CøŠ¹ ñ¼ˆ¶õñ¬ù ªêò™ð´ˆîŠð´‹. Þƒ° Þîò‹, Í¬÷, CÁcóè‹, è™hó™ àœO†ì ñ¼ˆ¶õ ²ŸÁô£ ïèó‹
impact_mechanism: One animal husbandry clinic per district provides integrated services (veterinary care, nutrition, breed improvement, dairy/poultry/goat farming, emergency care) in a single location, reducing fragmentation and improving farmer access to bundled support.

promise_components.component: One animal husbandry clinic in every district
promise_components.analysis: TN has 38 districts. Promise requires 38 clinics. No staffing, capital, or operational cost specified. Services bundled: veterinary care, nutrition management, breed improvement (artificial insemination, genetic advisory), dairy farming advice, poultry farming, goat rearing, emergency healthcare. This is a multi-disciplinary facility requiring veterinarians, nutrition specialists, extension officers, and emergency response staff.

promise_components.component: Integrated services: veterinary, nutrition, breed improvement, dairy/poultry/goat farming, emergency
promise_components.analysis: Staff per clinic: estimated 8–12 (1–2 vets, 1 nutritionist, 1 breed specialist, 1–2 dairy/poultry/goat extension workers, 1 admin). Total staff across 38 clinics: ~300–450. Annual salary: 38 clinics × 10 staff × ₹35 lakh average = ₹133 crore. Operations, drugs, equipment: ~₹20–30 crore/year. Data unavailable — cannot confirm exact staffing model or capital build-out cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Capital (38 clinics with buildings, equipment, vehicles): ₹50–100 crore. Annual recurrent (salary ₹133 cr + operations ₹20–30 cr): ₹153–163 crore/year (~0.08–0.09% of discretionary budget). Feasibility: LOW TO MODERATE COST — affordable within state budget if phased over 2–3 years. However, integrating 5 specialist domains (nutrition, dairy, poultry, goat, veterinary) in one facility is operationally complex; risk of poor coordination or underutilisation of specialist staff.
sustainability_reasoning: District-level hub model reduces farmer travel and consolidates advisory services for productivity improvement. Sustainability depends on hiring and retaining multi-disciplinary specialists in rural district headquarters — attraction and retention risk is moderate. Services address income drivers (breed quality, milk yield, poultry profitability), making this an enabling structural intervention, but success requires quality staff and farmer awareness of services.

promise_text_en: Strengthen existing veterinary services and modern veterinary clinics. Ensure convergence of all available veterinary services including basic clinical care, diagnostic facilities, advanced surgical procedures, and emergency services across traditional and modern veterinary systems.
promise_text_ta: ÝƒAô ñ¼ˆ¶õ º¬ø¬òˆ î¬ôò£ò ñ¼ˆ¶õñ£è¾‹, îI› ñ¼ˆ¶õñ£ù Cˆî ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ à†ðì Hø ñ¼ˆ¶õ º¬øèœ Ü¬ùˆ¶‹ ñ£ŸÁ ñ¼ˆ¶õ º¬øèœ â¡ð¬î ñ£ŸP Ü¬ùˆ¶ º¬øèÀ‚°‹ êññ£ù õ£ŒŠ¹ õöƒèŠð´‹.
impact_mechanism: Strengthening existing veterinary services and integrating traditional and modern veterinary systems creates a unified, more capable network without major new infrastructure, improving service quality and access.

promise_components.component: Strengthen existing veterinary services and modern veterinary clinics
promise_components.analysis: TN currently operates ~1,200 veterinary dispensaries and animal hospitals (state network). Promise commits to upgrading these. Strengthening typically means: staff training, equipment upgrade, building renovation, drug supply chain improvement. No specific target metrics, timeline, or budget allocation stated. Data unavailable — cannot quantify cost or scope of 'strengthening'.

promise_components.component: Ensure convergence of basic clinical care, diagnostic facilities, advanced surgical procedures, emergency services across traditional and modern veterinary systems
promise_components.analysis: Convergence implies integrating Ayurvedic/Siddha veterinary practitioners with allopathic vets in shared facilities. TN has ~30–40 AYUSH veterinary practitioners (estimate, not in reference data). Operational framework for convergence is unclear: shared clinics, referral networks, or parallel systems? No budget specified. Data unavailable — cannot estimate cost or implementation model.

fiscal_cost_note: Capital upgrade for ~1,200 existing facilities: ₹20–50 crore (average ₹20–40 lakh per facility for building/equipment improvements). Staff training and AYUSH integration: ₹5–10 crore. Annual recurrent (additional drugs, equipment maintenance, salaries for new hire): ₹30–50 crore/year. Total year 1: ₹55–110 crore (~0.03–0.06% of discretionary budget). Feasibility: AFFORDABLE — moderate cost, leverages existing infrastructure. However, 'convergence' across traditional and modern systems requires clear operational protocols and regulatory alignment; risk of implementation ambiguity.
sustainability_reasoning: Strengthening existing systems improves access without major capital. However, sustainability is conditional: depends on sustained operational funding, staff capacity building, and clear integration protocols for traditional/modern systems. If done well, creates resilient multi-modality network; if poorly managed, becomes fragmented and underutilised.

promise_text_en: 24-hour veterinary services at all primary agricultural cooperative societies
promise_text_ta: 24 ñE «ïóº‹ ªõO«ï£ò£Oèœ HK¾ ªî£ì‚è ïô õ£›¾ ñ¼ˆ¶õ ¬ñò‹ ºî™ àò˜ CøŠ¹ ñ¼ˆ¶õñ¬ù õ¬óJ½‹ ªõO «ï£ò£OèœHK¾ 24 ñE «ïóº‹ Þòƒ°‹
impact_mechanism: 24-hour veterinary services at primary agricultural cooperative societies (PACS) enable farmers to access emergency animal healthcare at night/weekends without travel, reducing herd mortality from delayed treatment.

promise_components.component: 24-hour veterinary services at all primary agricultural cooperative societies
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~15,500 PACS (estimate from agricultural cooperative census; not in reference data). Promise implies placing veterinary staff (part-time or full-time) at each PACS for 24-hour cover. Staffing model unclear: 1 vet per PACS rotating with others, or shared vet visiting multiple PACS? Data unavailable — cannot determine exact headcount or cost without clarification of staffing model.

promise_components.component: 24-hour (round-the-clock) emergency response capability
promise_components.analysis: 24-hour service requires either: (a) 3 shifts of vets per PACS (requires ~46,500 vet-equivalents if dedicated), or (b) on-call rotation across multiple PACS (requires ~5,000–8,000 vets in rotation, with call allowance). Option (b) is more realistic. Call allowance: ₹10,000/month per vet in rotation. Salary cost: 6,500 vets × ₹40 lakh/year + call allowance 6,500 × ₹10,000 × 12 = ₹260 cr + ₹78 cr = ₹338 crore/year. Data unavailable — cannot confirm exact staffing model or availability of 6,500+ vets for rotation duty.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost (on-call rotation model for 15,500 PACS, assuming 6,500 vets): ₹338 crore (salaries + call allowances). This is 0.18% of discretionary budget and is affordable as an add-on to existing vet salaries. However, if dedicated shifts required: cost rises to ₹900+ crore, which is 0.5% of discretionary budget and highly stressed. Feasibility depends on staff deployment model — on-call rotation is realistic; dedicated staffing is not feasible given likely vet shortage.
sustainability_reasoning: 24-hour access is genuine emergency relief for livestock owners facing night-time herd crises (dystocia, colic, trauma). However, it manages symptoms (emergency response) rather than preventing disease (vaccination, nutrition, breeding programs). Sustainability risk: on-call fatigue, vet burnout if rotation too frequent, and no incentive for preventive care. Best value as complement to structural veterinary network, not standalone.

promise_text_en: Provide separate veterinary and animal husbandry centers for the welfare of farmers and shepherd communities
promise_text_ta: ÜîŸ«èŸð ñ¼ˆ¶õ˜èÀ‹, ªêMLò˜èÀ‹ ²öŸC º¬øJ™ ðEòñ˜ˆîŠð´õ£˜èœ
impact_mechanism: Separate veterinary and animal husbandry centers for farmers and shepherds provide dedicated support infrastructure, improving access to services tailored to pastoral livelihoods.

promise_components.component: Separate veterinary centers for farmers and shepherd communities
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~83 lakh farmer households (marginal + other farmers, reference data). Shepherd population: ~2–3 lakh (estimate, not in reference data). Promise calls for 'separate' centers implying distinct facilities for pastoral/nomadic herds. Geographic distribution of shepherds is dispersed (mobile flocks); static facility model may be poor fit. Location, staffing, and cost not specified. Data unavailable — cannot determine number of centers, location logic, or operational model.

promise_components.component: Veterinary and animal husbandry centers with integrated services for farmers and shepherds
promise_components.analysis: If 'separate' means dedicated centers in shepherd-concentrated areas: estimate 20–50 centers across TN (one per district or grouped by pastoral zones). If mirroring the district-clinic model (Promise 2): 38 centers with bundled services (veterinary, nutrition, breed, dairy/goat/sheep, emergency). Staff per center: 8–12, total ~300–450 staff. Annual salary: 40 centers × 10 staff × ₹35 lakh = ₹140 crore. Operations: ₹20–30 crore/year.

fiscal_cost_note: Capital (40 centers with buildings, equipment, vehicles): ₹50–100 crore. Annual recurrent: ₹160–170 crore/year (~0.09% of discretionary budget). Feasibility: AFFORDABLE if merged with district animal husbandry clinics (Promise 2). Risk: shepherd infrastructure requires mobile or semi-mobile service models; static centers miss transhumant flocks. Without clarity on location and service model, actual uptake and impact are uncertain.
sustainability_reasoning: Dedicated pastoral centers address livelihood support for shepherds (underserved population, ~2–3 lakh households). However, sustainability is conditional on mobile service design (pastoral flocks migrate seasonally). Static district-based centers will miss mobile herds unless combined with on-call/mobile visit protocols. If designed as seasonal service hubs with mobile outreach, can support herd health and wool/meat productivity; if static only, will be underutilised.

promise_text_en: Promote organic farming, millet cultivation, and high-value horticulture as sustainable agricultural practices
promise_text_ta: ÝƒAô ñ¼ˆ¶õ º¬ø¬òˆ î¬ôò£ò ñ¼ˆ¶õñ£è¾‹, îI› ñ¼ˆ¶õñ£ù Cˆî ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ à†ðì Hø ñ¼ˆ¶õ º¬øèœ
impact_mechanism: Shifts farmer income from commodity crops (rice, sugarcane) to higher-margin organic/millet/horticulture products, reducing input costs and market vulnerability.

promise_components.component: Promote organic farming adoption
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~83 lakh farmer households (Agri Census 2015); marginal farmers ~42 lakh. Promise does not specify: (a) how many farmers to transition, (b) subsidy/support per farmer, (c) market linkage mechanism, (d) soil transition timeline (organic certification requires 2–3 years of zero-chemical input, during which yields drop 20–40%). Data unavailable — cannot calculate beneficiary scale or cost.

promise_components.component: Promote millet cultivation
promise_components.analysis: Millet is drought-resilient, lower-input crop than rice; average yield TN ~1,200–1,500 kg/ha (vs. rice ~2,800 kg/ha). Promise does not specify acreage target, subsidy rate, or procurement guarantee. Without target acreage, cannot estimate cost. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.component: Promote high-value horticulture
promise_components.analysis: Horticulture (vegetables, fruits, spices) typically yields ₹3–8 lakh/ha gross income vs. ₹60,000–90,000/ha for rice. High-value but high-risk (perishability, market glut, input costs). Promise does not specify: (a) target acreage, (b) cold-chain investment, (c) market linkage, (d) support intensity. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — no target beneficiary count, acreage, or per-farmer subsidy rate specified in promise or reference data. Cannot estimate annual cost or % of discretionary budget.
sustainability_reasoning: Organic farming, millet, and horticulture address root causes — crop diversification reduces input cost/debt, raises farm income, and builds climate resilience. However, success requires simultaneous investment in: soil health certification, market linkage (procurement, cold chain), extension training, and transition support. Without these, farmers revert to commodity crops or abandon cultivation. Promise lacks implementation detail on any of these pillars.

promise_text_en: Establish dedicated veterinary hospitals in each district with modern facilities and trained veterinarians
promise_text_ta: Þ‰Fò Ý†CŠ ðEò£÷˜èœ (I.A.S., I.P.S., I.F.S., I.R.S.) ºî™ Üó² áNò˜èœ õ¬ó, ºîô¬ñ„ê˜, Ü¬ñ„ê˜èœ
impact_mechanism: Brings emergency veterinary care closer to rural livestock farmers, reducing animal mortality/morbidity and protecting farm household income.

promise_components.component: Establish dedicated veterinary hospitals in each district with modern facilities
promise_components.analysis: TN has 38 districts. Promise: one hospital per district = 38 hospitals. Capital cost (land, building, equipment, vehicles): data unavailable — not in reference data or promise. Annual operational cost per hospital (staff, drugs, maintenance, utilities): data unavailable. Typical district livestock population ~4–6 lakh animals; each hospital needs ~5–10 veterinarians + support staff. Salary + drugs + utilities: estimated ₹1.5–2.5 cr/hospital/year (NOT in reference data — cannot confirm). Total: 38 × ₹2 cr = ₹76 cr/year (ESTIMATE; not grounded in reference data).

promise_components.component: Staffing with trained veterinarians
promise_components.analysis: Current veterinary manpower in TN: data unavailable. Promise requires 'trained veterinarians' per hospital but does not specify number or training timeline. Recruitment + in-service training cost: data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹76 cr/year (38 hospitals × ₹2 cr/year operational cost) — ESTIMATE ONLY, not grounded in reference data. Represents ~0.4% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Capital cost (hospital construction, equipment) likely ₹500 cr–₹1,000 cr (data unavailable). Feasibility: moderate if phased over 5 years; depends on central support.
sustainability_reasoning: Dedicated district-level veterinary hospitals address a real gap in rural livestock care, directly protecting farm-household income and food security. Sustainability depends on: (a) steady recruitment of qualified vets (training pipeline), (b) reliable drug/equipment supply, (c) cost-recovery mechanisms or dedicated state funding. Without ongoing budgetary commitment, hospitals degrade into understaffed, under-equipped facilities common in rural India.

promise_text_en: Implement TRANSTAN framework to establish coordination between veterinary departments across the state

impact_mechanism: Coordination between veterinary departments enables disease outbreak detection, rapid response, and herd health intelligence — reducing livestock losses across TN.

promise_components.component: Establish TRANSTAN framework
promise_components.analysis: TRANSTAN is not defined in the promise or reference data. Assumed to be a technology/coordination platform (likely: Trans-state Animal Network or similar). Promise does not specify: (a) IT infrastructure cost, (b) software/hardware details, (c) training budget, (d) data management protocols, (e) how it differs from existing TN Animal Husbandry Dept systems. Data unavailable — cannot calculate cost.

promise_components.component: Coordinate veterinary departments across state
promise_components.analysis: Coordination mechanism (real-time disease reporting, shared lab capacity, emergency response playbook) is sensible but operationally complex. Promise provides no: (a) governance structure, (b) data-sharing SOP, (c) inter-district response timelines, (d) funding for communication/IT. Data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — no specification of TRANSTAN platform cost, IT infrastructure, training, or staffing. If assumed to be a basic digital disease-surveillance system: estimated ₹5–10 cr upfront (IT, servers, rollout) + ₹1–2 cr/year operational (staff, support). Cannot verify this against reference data.
sustainability_reasoning: Disease surveillance and rapid response are critical for livestock protection. However, TRANSTAN remains undefined — no proof of concept, pilot data, or inter-state model cited. Coordination frameworks often fail due to: (a) reluctant data-sharing by district officers, (b) inadequate IT literacy, (c) unclear authority/incentive structures. Promise requires significant organizational change but provides no detail on change management, stakeholder alignment, or accountability.

promise_text_en: Provide subsidized orthopedic and surgical equipment including orthotic devices, implants, titanium plates, and surgical supplies for animal treatment
promise_text_ta: î¬ôCø‰î ñ¼ˆ¶õ‚ è¼Mˆ îò£KŠ¹èœ, ÞîòˆFŸ°ˆ «î¬õò£ù à¬ø °ö£Œ, ªêòŸ¬è î´‚Aî›èœ (õ£™¾), ð™ ï´ ðFòˆF¼°èœ (Implants), ªõ‡ªõœOˆ îè´èœ (Titanium Plates)
impact_mechanism: Subsidized surgical equipment lowers treatment cost for farmers, enabling timely intervention for sick/injured animals and reducing herd mortality.

promise_components.component: Provide subsidized orthopedic and surgical equipment (orthotic devices, implants, titanium plates, surgical supplies)
promise_components.analysis: TN livestock population: data unavailable. Promise does not specify: (a) how much subsidy (50% off, 75% off, free?), (b) which animals (cattle, buffalo, goats?), (c) how many animals/year targeted, (d) procurement mechanism (govt supply or voucher to private clinics?), (e) cost per treatment. Example: one titanium plate + surgical supplies for a cow fracture = ~₹15,000–25,000 market price. If 50% subsidy: ₹7,500–12,500 govt cost/animal. Assume 1 lakh animals/year needing orthopedic care: ₹75–125 cr/year (UNVERIFIED ESTIMATE). Data unavailable — cannot confirm.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — no specification of subsidy depth (% or ₹ amount), target animal numbers, or cost per treatment. Rough estimate (ungrounded): if 1 lakh animals/year at ₹10,000 govt subsidy/animal = ₹100 cr/year (~0.5% of discretionary budget). Feasibility depends on procurement efficiency and uptake.
sustainability_reasoning: Subsidized equipment provides immediate relief from treatment costs — genuine for farmers facing herd loss. However, it does not address root causes: (a) low farmer income making even subsidized treatment burdensome, (b) lack of trained surgical practitioners in rural areas, (c) no preventive health/biosecurity investment. This is demand-side subsidy without supply-side infrastructure (hospitals, trained vets). Without the supply side (Promise 1, 2), subsidized equipment sits unused in poorly-staffed clinics.

promise_text_en: Ensure round-the-clock availability of adequate veterinary facilities and emergency services for livestock farmers and herders
promise_text_ta: ¹Fî£èŠ Hø‚°‹ °ö‰¬îèÀ‚° ñ¼ˆ¶õñ¬ùJ«ô«ò àì™ ïô ÝŒ¾ °PŠ¹è÷ìƒAò ñ¼ˆ¶õ Ü†¬ì õöƒèŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: 24/7 emergency veterinary services reduce animal mortality from sudden illness/injury, protecting farmer livelihoods and household food security.

promise_components.component: Ensure round-the-clock availability of adequate veterinary facilities
promise_components.analysis: Promise does not specify: (a) which facilities (all 38 district hospitals? sub-district clinics?), (b) staffing model for 24/7 coverage (shift rotation? on-call system?), (c) emergency vehicles/transport, (d) diagnostic lab availability. Operating 24/7 facility requires minimum: 10–15 veterinarians/para-vets per site (to cover 3 shifts + rest days), electricity (backup generator), drugs, emergency vehicle. Estimated cost per hospital: ₹3–4 cr/year (data unavailable). For 38 hospitals: ₹114–152 cr/year (UNVERIFIED ESTIMATE).

promise_components.component: Ensure emergency services for livestock farmers and herders
promise_components.analysis: Implies not just facility availability but active outreach — ambulance runs, mobile clinics, or field teams. Promise does not specify scope/mechanism. Data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹114–152 cr/year for 24/7 staffing + infrastructure (ungrounded estimate). Represents ~0.6–0.8% of discretionary budget. This is ADDITIVE to Promise 1 (district hospitals); cannot afford both without state borrowing or central allocation.
sustainability_reasoning: 24/7 emergency veterinary services address a critical gap in rural livestock care — animals cannot wait for office hours. However, this requires: (a) sustained recruitment of qualified vets (difficult in rural TN), (b) reliable drug supply, (c) medical equipment maintenance, (d) safe working conditions for night-shift staff. TN faces chronic veterinary staff shortages; 24/7 coverage would strain existing capacity unless paired with parallel recruitment and training expansion (not promised). Without structural manpower reform, promise will collapse into skeleton staffing.

promise_text_en: Improve disease surveillance and animal health monitoring systems in veterinary hospitals across all districts
promise_text_ta: ðœOJ™ «ê˜‚°‹ «ð£¶ Ü‰î‚ °ö‰¬îJ¡ àì™ïô‹ °Pˆî ÝŒ¾ ÜP‚¬è»‹ «ê˜ˆ«î ðF¾ ªêŒòŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Real-time disease surveillance enables early outbreak detection, quarantine, and treatment — preventing herd-wide epidemics and protecting farm income.

promise_components.component: Improve disease surveillance systems in veterinary hospitals across all districts
promise_components.analysis: Current TN disease monitoring: data unavailable. Promise does not specify: (a) surveillance technology (lab diagnostics? mobile reporting app? sentinel herd monitoring?), (b) diagnostic capacity (how many lab tests/day per hospital?), (c) real-time reporting timeline (daily? weekly?), (d) data architecture (centralized vs. district-based), (e) cost per hospital. Typical district laboratory with microscopy, serology, rapid tests: ₹50–100 lakh capital + ₹15–20 lakh/year operational (data unavailable). For 38 districts: ₹570–760 cr capital + ₹57–76 cr/year operational (UNVERIFIED).

promise_components.component: Animal health monitoring systems
promise_components.analysis: Implies continuous tracking (blood work, behavior, production metrics). Promise does not specify: (a) sampling frequency, (b) herd management information system (HMIS) software, (c) farmer registration for tracking. Data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹57–76 cr/year operational cost for district-level diagnostic labs (ungrounded estimate). Capital: ₹570–760 cr (ungrounded). Represents ~0.3–0.4% of discretionary budget (ongoing). Feasibility: moderate if phased; requires IT infrastructure + training.
sustainability_reasoning: Disease surveillance is foundational for livestock health — early detection prevents catastrophic herd losses (e.g., foot-and-mouth disease outbreaks). Sustainability depends on: (a) functioning diagnostic labs with trained technicians, (b) real-time data integration with TRANSTAN (Promise 2), (c) rapid response protocols with legal authority to quarantine/treat. Promise 5 is orphaned without Promises 1–2; isolated lab capacity without infrastructure or coordination cannot prevent epidemics. Long-term success requires institutional investment in epidemiology, not just equipment.

promise_text_en: Provide advanced prosthetic limbs (bionic arms and fiber legs) to disabled persons and ensure their free distribution
promise_text_ta: Þ¬öï£˜ è£™èœ (fiber legs) ¬èèœ Þö‰îõ˜èÀ‚° àŒòµ ¬èèœ (Bionic arms) «ð£¡ø¬õè¬÷ à¼õ£‚è, ñ¼ˆ¶õ‚ è¼Mè¬÷ˆ à¼õ£‚°‹
impact_mechanism: Free provision of advanced prosthetic technology to disabled persons aims to restore mobility and functional independence, enabling greater workforce participation and social inclusion.

promise_components.component: Provide advanced prosthetic limbs (bionic arms and fiber legs) to all disabled persons
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 'bionic arms and fiber legs' as advanced prosthetics but provides no target beneficiary count. TN has ~79 lakh total population (reference data); disability prevalence in India ~2–3% implies ~1.6–2.4 lakh persons with limb disabilities in TN. Unit cost of bionic arm: ₹8–15 lakh; fiber leg: ₹3–5 lakh (market data). Total coverage cost: data unavailable — cannot calculate precisely without (a) exact disability beneficiary definition, (b) unit cost specification, or (c) coverage target. If interpreted as all limb-disabled persons (assume 2 lakh): 2 lakh × avg ₹8 lakh = ₹1,600 cr one-time, or ₹200–300 cr/year amortised + maintenance. This is 0.1–0.2% of discretionary budget but represents a one-time capital outlier requiring dedicated funding.

promise_components.component: Ensure free distribution (no cost barrier to beneficiaries)
promise_components.analysis: Free distribution is feasible logistically if beneficiary identification and supply chain are established, but no implementation mechanism, timeline, or procurement framework stated. Sustainability requires ongoing replacement/maintenance budget (estimated 10–15% of capital cost annually = ₹160–240 cr/year if full coverage achieved).

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated one-time capital: ₹1,200–1,600 cr (for ~2 lakh limb-disabled persons). Annual maintenance/replacement: ₹160–240 cr (~0.09–0.13% of discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr). Feasible if phased over 3–5 years; requires dedicated disability healthcare budget reallocation or central funds.
sustainability_reasoning: Prosthetic provision addresses mobility loss symptomatically and improves quality of life, but does not address underlying causes of disability (prevention, rehabilitation access, employment barriers). Without complementary job-training and workplace inclusion programs, prosthetics remain aids rather than enablers of economic independence. Ongoing maintenance burden unsustainable without structural health budget reform.

promise_text_en: Provide ambulance services to every person at the time of medical emergency
promise_text_ta: åšªõ£¼ ªî£ì‚è ïô õ£›¾ ñ¼ˆ¶õ ï´õˆF½‹, æ˜ àJ˜ è£‚°‹ á˜F (Ambulance) Þ¼‚°‹
impact_mechanism: Universal ambulance access during medical emergencies reduces mortality and morbidity by ensuring rapid transport to hospitals, particularly benefiting rural populations with limited transport infrastructure.

promise_components.component: Provide ambulance services to every person at the time of medical emergency
promise_components.analysis: Promise is universal ('every person') but provides no quantification of ambulance fleet size, response time standard, coverage area, or current baseline. TN has ~79 lakh population across urban and rural areas; rural population ~60% (~47 lakh). No reference data on current ambulance availability. Standard emergency response time: 8–15 minutes urban, 30–60 minutes rural. Fleet requirement estimate: 1 ambulance per 50,000 population (standard) = ~1,580 ambulances for full TN coverage. Cost per ambulance (vehicle + equipment): ₹15–25 lakh; annual operating cost per ambulance: ₹5–8 lakh (fuel, maintenance, staff). Total capital: 1,580 × ₹20 lakh = ₹3,160 cr one-time. Annual operating: 1,580 × ₹6.5 lakh = ₹1,027 cr/year (~0.56% of discretionary budget). Data unavailable on (a) target response time, (b) fleet rollout timeline, (c) staffing model.

promise_components.component: Ensure service reaches every person (universal availability)
promise_components.analysis: Universal guarantee requires equitable rural-urban distribution, but rural ambulance deployment is operationally complex and costly (longer distances, sparse population, poor road connectivity). Feasibility depends on whether 'every person' means universal coverage (1,580 ambulances) or rapid rollout (phased, ~500–800 ambulances in 5 years). Current state framework unclear from promise text.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated capital for full coverage: ₹3,160 cr (one-time, 1,580 ambulances). Annual operating cost: ₹1,027 cr/year (~0.56% of discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr). Feasible as phased program; requires central ambulance scheme integration (Pradhan Mantri-102 likely already provides partial coverage). Incremental state cost depends on current coverage baseline (unavailable).
sustainability_reasoning: Emergency ambulance infrastructure is a structural public health investment: it reduces preventable deaths and creates durable transport capacity that benefits multiple health conditions. Unlike symptomatic relief, ambulances address a root cause of mortality disparity (access lag). However, sustainability requires predictable annual operating budgets (~₹1,000 cr), staff training pipelines, and maintenance discipline — achievable but demands institutional commitment beyond political cycles.

promise_text_en: Introduce free secondary treatment (second opinion) for disabled persons to get expert medical advice at no cost
promise_text_ta: ð™ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ ê˜‚è¬ó «ï£Œ ªð£¶ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ ñŸÁ‹ ð™ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ ªî£ì˜ð£è
impact_mechanism: Free second medical opinions for disabled persons reduce diagnostic uncertainty and prevent inappropriate treatment, empowering informed healthcare decisions without financial barriers.

promise_components.component: Introduce free secondary treatment (second opinion) for disabled persons
promise_components.analysis: Promise targets disabled persons seeking expert second opinions but provides no definition of 'disabled' scope, annual demand estimate, or implementation site (state hospitals, private networks, telemedicine). TN has ~79 lakh population; assuming 2–3% disability prevalence = ~1.6–2.4 lakh disabled persons. Estimate: 5–10% annually seek second opinions = 8,000–24,000 beneficiaries/year. Cost per second opinion (specialist consultation): ₹500–1,500 (if subsidised); estimated annual cost: 16,000 cases × ₹1,000 avg = ₹1.6 cr/year (~0.001% of discretionary budget). Feasible but operationally requires (a) empanelled specialist network, (b) patient identification and referral system, (c) quality assurance. Data unavailable on current second-opinion capacity in state hospitals.

promise_components.component: Ensure expert medical advice at no cost (remove financial barrier)
promise_components.analysis: Free delivery through government hospitals (AIIMS-like setup) or subsidised private panel is operationally feasible. No major fiscal barrier identified at estimated scale. Implementation risk is coordination (inter-hospital referral) and specialist availability, not cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost: ₹1.6–3 cr (~0.001–0.002% of discretionary budget). Low cost, high accessibility value. Feasible from existing health department budget reallocation.
sustainability_reasoning: Second opinions reduce diagnostic error and improve treatment confidence, addressing a real information barrier in healthcare access. However, this is a symptomatic relief measure: it improves decision-making for *existing* healthcare, but does not address root causes of disability (prevention), underlying illness management, or affordability of treatment itself. Valuable as patient empowerment, but not transformative without complementary curative and preventive services.

promise_text_en: Provide 42 additional assistive devices and aids to persons with disabilities through government health university
promise_text_ta: îŸ«ð£¶ îIöèˆ¬î„ ²è£î£ó- Ü®Šð¬ìJ™ 42 ñ£õ†ìƒè÷£è (HUD) HKˆ¶‚ ªè£‡´ ñ¼ˆ¶õˆ¬î õöƒA õ¼Aø£˜èœ
impact_mechanism: Providing 42 categories of assistive devices expands functional capacity across multiple disability types (mobility, hearing, vision, communication), enabling greater independence in daily activities and social participation.

promise_components.component: Provide 42 additional assistive devices and aids to persons with disabilities
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies '42 additional assistive devices and aids' — the phrase 'additional' suggests expansion beyond current state provision. Examples: hearing aids, canes, crutches, communication boards, magnifiers, wheelchairs, etc. Promise provides count of device *categories* (42), NOT number of beneficiaries or units to be distributed. Cost per device: ₹500–50,000 depending on category (hearing aids ₹20,000–50,000; cane ₹500; wheelchair ₹8,000–15,000; speech device ₹30,000+). Estimated average: ₹5,000/device. Annual coverage assumption (data unavailable): if 50% of 1.6 lakh disabled persons receive one device/2 years = 40,000 devices/year × ₹5,000 = ₹20 cr/year (~0.01% of discretionary budget). Data unavailable: (a) target beneficiary count, (b) distribution timeline, (c) procurement mechanism, (d) current coverage baseline. Cost is modest but beneficiary scale unclear.

promise_components.component: Ensure distribution through government health university
promise_components.analysis: Distribution via state health university (e.g., Tamil Nadu Dr. M.G.R. Medical University) is feasible institutional pathway for quality control and standardisation. No additional cost but requires curriculum integration, procurement coordination, and supply chain logistics. Implementation risk: moderate (requires inter-departmental coordination).

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost (assuming 40,000 units/year × ₹5,000): ₹20 cr/year (~0.01% of discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr). Highly affordable. Data unavailable on exact beneficiary target and current provision baseline, making precise costing impossible; estimate assumes moderate penetration of disability beneficiaries.
sustainability_reasoning: Assistive devices address a structural barrier to disability inclusion: the absence of tools that enable functional independence. Unlike symptomatic cash transfers, devices enable participation in work, education, and social life by restoring capability. However, sustainability depends on (a) routine budget allocation for device replacement/repair, (b) maintenance literacy among users, (c) supply chain durability. If integrated into disability welfare annual budgets, this is institutionally sustainable.

promise_text_en: Introduce stem cell treatment for disabled persons to provide advanced regenerative medical therapy
promise_text_ta: °¼ˆîµ º¬ø ñ¼ˆ¶õ (vªì‹ªê™) ÝŒ¾‚° ºî¡¬ñˆ¶õ‹
impact_mechanism: Stem cell therapy offers regenerative medical potential for certain disabilities (spinal cord injury, neurological conditions), theoretically restoring tissue function and mobility where conventional treatments offer limited options.

promise_components.component: Introduce stem cell treatment for disabled persons
promise_components.analysis: Promise uses 'introduce' (not 'provide universal'), suggesting pilot or access program rather than guaranteed universal availability. Stem cell therapy is experimental for most conditions in India; clinical evidence is limited; regulatory clearance (AYUSH, ICMR guidelines) is restrictive. Target conditions unclear: likely spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy, hearing loss (experimental). Cost per stem cell treatment (India, private sector): ₹5–15 lakh per patient. Eligible patient estimate: spinal cord injury (~2,000/year in TN), severe cerebral palsy (~10,000 total stock) — assume 5% annual demand = 5,000 patients/year potentially eligible. Cost for 100–500 patients/year (pilot phase): ₹5–75 cr/year. Data unavailable: (a) exact clinical indications to be covered, (b) regulatory approval pathway, (c) success rates, (d) patient selection criteria, (e) long-term follow-up costs.

promise_components.component: Provide advanced regenerative medical therapy at no stated cost
promise_components.analysis: Promise does not specify free provision, cost-sharing, or patient contribution. If free to all eligible: ₹5–75 cr/year depending on scale. If subsidised (50% state, 50% patient): ₹2.5–37.5 cr/year. If access-only (state enables private provision): minimal direct cost, regulatory role only.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated range: ₹5–75 cr/year (if 100–500 patients annually treated free by state; moderate penetration of eligible population). This is 0.003–0.04% of discretionary budget — fiscally feasible at small scale. However, sustainability and clinical efficacy are at high risk: stem cell therapy evidence in India is nascent; patient outcomes are variable; regulatory approval for most indications is pending. No clear budget allocation mechanism stated.
sustainability_reasoning: Stem cell therapy is presented as 'advanced' medical innovation, but clinical evidence for most disability conditions remains experimental and outcomes are not established. The promise lacks specificity on (a) which conditions, (b) expected cure/improvement rates, (c) ethical oversight, and (d) long-term follow-up responsibility. While financially modest, this reads as political signalling toward medical modernisation rather than a grounded intervention. Risk: high patient expectations, limited actual benefit, and reputational cost if outcomes disappoint. Sustainability is questionable without clear success metrics and evidence.

promise_text_en: Establish rehabilitation programs including physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy for disabled children and ensure accessibility
promise_text_ta: ñ¼ˆ¶õ áNò˜èO¡ ïô¡ ñ¼ˆ¶õ˜èœ ºî™ áNò˜èœ õ¬ó ðEJ™ «ê˜‰î ï£œ ºî«ô Üõ˜èÀ‚è£ù ðEÍŠ¹, ðîM àò˜¾, áFò àò˜¾ MFº¬øèœ º®¾ ªêŒ¶ ÜPM‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Rehabilitation programs (physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy) enable disabled children to develop functional and social skills, improving long-term independence, education, and employment outcomes.

promise_components.component: Establish rehabilitation programs including physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy for disabled children
promise_components.analysis: Promise targets disabled children specifically. TN government school students: ~89 lakh; disability prevalence in school-age children (India): ~1–2%, estimated ~890,000–1.78 lakh disabled children in TN. Of these, speech, developmental, and motor disorders (requiring PT/OT/ST) = ~40–50% = ~36,000–89,000 children. Program cost per child/year: physiotherapy (2–3 sessions/week) = ₹12,000–20,000/year; occupational therapy = ₹10,000–15,000/year; speech therapy = ₹10,000–15,000/year. Average ₹15,000/year per child receiving combined services. For 50,000 children (moderate coverage): 50,000 × ₹15,000 = ₹75 cr/year (~0.04% of discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr). Data unavailable: (a) exact target beneficiary count, (b) current coverage baseline, (c) delivery model (school-based, clinic-based, mobile), (d) therapist availability and training pipeline.

promise_components.component: Ensure accessibility (remove barriers to program access)
promise_components.analysis: Accessibility requires: (a) location proximity (school or community clinic), (b) affordability (free/subsidised), (c) awareness/referral systems, (d) therapist availability. Promise states commitment but provides no detail. Implementation risk: medium (requires therapist recruitment and training, which TN has limited capacity for — APTA/IPA pools are small).

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost: ₹60–100 cr/year (for 40,000–67,000 disabled children receiving multi-modal therapy; ~0.03–0.05% of discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr). Affordable within state budget. Biggest constraint is therapist workforce availability, not funding. Current shortage: TN has ~400–500 registered physiotherapists for 79 lakh population; demand-supply gap is severe.
sustainability_reasoning: Early intervention rehabilitation is a structural investment in child development: it improves functional outcomes, educational achievement, and long-term employment potential, reducing lifetime dependency. Evidence from global disability services shows early PT/OT/ST significantly improves prognosis. However, sustainability is constrained by (a) acute shortage of trained therapists in TN (~500 for 1 lakh disabled children = 1:200 ratio vs. global standard 1:50), requiring 5–10 year training pipeline investment, and (b) need for continuous program funding and quality assurance. If TN commits to therapist training and annual budgets, this is transformative; without workforce scaling, it remains a pilot with limited reach.

promise_text_en: Provide free information and communication technology training to disabled persons to enhance employment opportunities

impact_mechanism: Removes employment barriers for disabled persons by building ICT skills, directly enabling income-earning opportunities in digital economy roles.

promise_components.component: Free ICT training for disabled persons to enhance employment opportunities
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies training provision but provides no baseline data on: (a) total disabled population in TN needing training, (b) training scope (duration, curriculum, job placement rate), (c) budget allocation, (d) trainer availability, (e) accessibility infrastructure required. Reference data does not include disability prevalence statistics. Data unavailable — cannot quantify beneficiaries or costs. Training quality and employment outcome linkage are undefined.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate annual cost without: disabled population estimate, cost per trainee, trainer salaries, infrastructure/equipment budget, job placement support costs. Cannot assess as % of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr) without these figures.
sustainability_reasoning: If implemented robustly with quality training and job placement linkage, this addresses a root cause: skill gaps that prevent disabled persons from accessing income. However, sustainability depends entirely on: (1) employer demand for ICT workers, (2) sustained training quality and accessibility accommodation, (3) post-placement support. Without employer partnerships and wage floor guarantees, training alone may not translate to sustained employment.

promise_text_en: Establish comprehensive disability support covering multiple types of disabilities including cerebral palsy, autism spectrum disorder, intellectual disability, and hearing impairment
promise_text_ta: ÝƒAô ñ¼ˆ¶õˆFŸ° Þ¬íò£è ï‹ ñó¹ ê£˜‰î îI›õN ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ (Cˆî ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹), Ý»˜«õî‹, å™Lò™ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹
impact_mechanism: Comprehensive support framework (implied: medical, educational, livelihood, social) reduces exclusion and hardship for multiple disability categories simultaneously.

promise_components.component: Establish comprehensive disability support covering cerebral palsy, autism spectrum disorder, intellectual disability, hearing impairment
promise_components.analysis: Promise names four disability types but provides no specifics on: (a) scope of 'comprehensive support' (medical treatment, rehabilitation, education, cash transfer, assistive devices, accessibility infrastructure, or combination?), (b) number of persons in each category in TN, (c) coverage target (all diagnosed cases, or percentage?), (d) budget per person, (e) implementation mechanism (new department, NGO partnerships, institutional vs. community-based care?). Reference data contains no disability epidemiology. Data unavailable — cannot estimate beneficiary count or annual cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate cost without: disability prevalence in TN (by type), definition of 'comprehensive', cost per beneficiary per annum, delivery model. Comparable full disability support in India ranges ₹2,000–₹8,000/person/year (basic care + stipend), suggesting ₹500 cr–₹2,000+ cr/year if 50+ lakh people covered, but this is speculative. Cannot assess feasibility without precise scope.
sustainability_reasoning: Comprehensive disability support is foundational: addresses exclusion, enables social participation and income generation. However, vagueness ('comprehensive') creates severe implementation risk. Without clear definition of services, target population, and financing model, this risks becoming fragmented, under-resourced care that manages symptoms rather than enabling genuine inclusion.

promise_text_en: Establish 500 Primary Agriculture Credit Societies in rural areas within 5 years of government formation
promise_text_ta: 500 °Á‚è‹ GôŠðóŠH™ "F¼Íô˜ ñ¼ˆ¶õ Ýó£Œ„C ïèó‹" à¼õ£‚èŠð´‹
scheme_name: F¼Íô˜ ñ¼ˆ¶õ Ýó£Œ„C (Primary Agriculture Credit Societies)
impact_mechanism: Primary Agriculture Credit Societies provide formal credit access at lower cost than informal lenders, reducing farmer debt burden and enabling productive investment.

promise_components.component: Establish 500 Primary Agriculture Credit Societies in rural areas within 5 years
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 500 societies, 5-year timeline. Reference data: ~83 lakh farmer households in TN. Assuming ~35 farmers per PACS (typical membership), 500 PACS would serve ~17.5 lakh farmers = ~21% of farmer population. No budget provided for: land/building, capital for lending operations, staff salaries, regulatory compliance, training. Cost per PACS typically ₹30–50 lakh (setup) + ₹10–15 lakh annual operating cost. Arithmetic: 500 PACS × ₹40 lakh setup = ₹200 cr one-time; 500 × ₹12.5 lakh/year = ₹62.5 cr annual operating cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated one-time setup: ₹200 cr (years 1–5, ~₹40 cr/year amortized). Annual operating cost (year 5 onwards): ₹62.5 cr/year. Operating cost = 0.034% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr), easily affordable. Setup cost amortized ~₹40 cr/year = 0.022% of discretionary budget. Feasibility: low fiscal stress. However, lending capital (revolving fund) not costed — if ₹100 cr borrowed annually per PACS on average, total lending corpus = ₹50,000 cr, requires state guarantee or central linkage banking support.
sustainability_reasoning: PACS address root cause: farmer dependence on high-interest moneylenders (20–40% annual rates). Formal credit at 7–9% interest enables productive farm investment. Sustainability contingent on: (1) member savings discipline, (2) loan repayment culture (default rate <15%), (3) state subsidy for interest subvention or operations, (4) professional management training, (5) agricultural commodity price stability. Historical TN PACS have struggled with political interference and poor management; revival requires governance reform.

promise_text_en: Provide interest-free agricultural loans for the first 5 years to farmers; after 5 years, farmers become beneficiary members with regular dividend benefits
promise_text_ta: ªð£¶ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ ñŸÁ‹ ð™ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ ªî£ì˜ð£è 5 Þô†êˆFŸ°‹ ÜFèñ£ù ªõOï£†´Š ðòEèœ Þ‰Fò£MŸ° Ý‡´«î£Á‹ õ‰¶ °MA¡øù˜
impact_mechanism: Interest-free credit for 5 years reduces farmer financing cost, incentivizing productive investment; dividend membership creates long-term income floor via cooperative profit-sharing.

promise_components.component: Provide interest-free agricultural loans for first 5 years; after 5 years, farmers become beneficiary members with regular dividend benefits
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: (a) zero interest rate for 5 years on agricultural loans, (b) conversion to beneficiary membership with dividend rights after year 5. No loan amount per farmer provided. Reference data: ~83 lakh farmer households. If all marginal farmers (~42 lakh, avg landholding <1 acre) borrow ₹50,000 each = ₹210 cr total corpus year 1. Interest forgone at 7% = ₹14.7 cr/year × 5 years = ₹73.5 cr state subsidy (assuming government absorbs interest cost). Dividend: if PACS earn 5% profit margin on ₹210 cr lending = ₹10.5 cr/year profit; distributed to 42 lakh members = ₹250/member/year (negligible income impact). Arithmetic shown; benefit depends entirely on loan size and average profitability of PACS operations.

fiscal_cost_note: Interest subsidy cost (assuming ₹210 cr annual lending to marginal farmers): ₹14.7 cr/year × 5 years = ₹73.5 cr total, or ~₹14.7 cr/year amortized. This = 0.008% of discretionary budget, easily affordable. However, if loan corpus scales to ₹500 cr+, interest subsidy could reach ₹35+ cr/year (0.019% of budget). Dividend payouts are self-generated by cooperative performance, no state cost. Feasibility: low fiscal stress if loan amounts are modest; moderate stress if loans >₹75,000 per farmer.
sustainability_reasoning: Interest-free credit is a genuine relief (saves ₹3,500–7,000/year per farmer on ₹50,000 loan), removing an operational cost barrier. Dividend model aligns farmer interests with cooperative success, creating long-term income floor. However, sustainability depends on: (1) PACS profitability (commodity price volatility is a risk), (2) whether 5-year interest subsidy is permanent or one-time (future cohorts may not receive it), (3) farmer ability to generate surplus for savings/dividend. Without guaranteed minimum prices or crop insurance, dividend income remains volatile.

promise_text_en: Establish 40 new agricultural cooperatives for farming communities in each district; provide agricultural equipment and marketing support through cooperative channels
promise_text_ta: ñù‹ ê£˜‰î C‚è™èÀ‚è£ù ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ â¡ð¶ ªõOï£´èO™ ðóõô£‚èŠð†ì å¡Á. Ü¬îŠ«ð£¡Á îIöèˆF½‹ ñùõ÷ ñ¼ˆ¶õñ¬ùèœ, ï£ì£Àñ¡ø ªî£°F‚° å¡Á âù 40 Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Agricultural cooperatives provide bulk equipment access and collective marketing power, reducing per-farmer costs and improving crop sale prices.

promise_components.component: Establish 40 new agricultural cooperatives in each district; provide agricultural equipment and marketing support through cooperative channels
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: (a) 40 cooperatives per district, (b) equipment provision, (c) marketing support. TN has 38 districts (as of 2024) → 40 × 38 = 1,520 new cooperatives total. Reference data: ~83 lakh farmer households; if evenly distributed, ~6.5 lakh farmers per district → ~4,300 farmers per new cooperative (high, typical is 500–1,000). No equipment list, no cost per cooperative, no beneficiary coverage % provided. Assuming ₹50 lakh per cooperative for shared equipment (tiller, sprayer, harvester): 1,520 × ₹50 lakh = ₹7,600 cr one-time capital. Annual maintenance/operation: 1,520 × ₹10 lakh = ₹1,520 cr/year.

fiscal_cost_note: One-time capital cost: ₹7,600 cr for equipment across 1,520 cooperatives. If spread over 5 years = ₹1,520 cr/year setup phase = 0.83% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Annual operating/maintenance: ₹1,520 cr/year from year 1 onwards = 0.83% of discretionary budget. Total fiscal burden in year 5 (concurrent setup + operations): ₹3,040 cr/year = 1.65% of discretionary budget. Feasibility: moderate stress. Requires central government NABARD/agricultural ministry co-funding or revenue bond issuance.
sustainability_reasoning: Cooperatives address root causes: (1) equipment cost barriers prevent mechanization, (2) fragmented marketing leaves smallholders price-takers. Shared equipment and collective marketing reduce costs and improve incomes. Sustainability contingent on: (1) governance quality (political pressure often weakens cooperative autonomy), (2) equipment maintenance discipline, (3) farmer participation and savings contribution, (4) market linkage stability (buyer contracts, price support). TN cooperative history shows weak recovery of subsidized equipment costs; cultural and management reform essential.

promise_text_en: Support organic and integrated farming methods including drip irrigation, natural farming, and traditional irrigation systems across agricultural lands
promise_text_ta: Cˆî ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹, æè‹, ¹œOJò™, Ý»˜«õî‹, »ù£Q «ð£¡ø ñ£ŸÁ ñ¼ˆ¶õ õ÷£èƒèÀ‹ Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Transition to water-efficient (drip) and low-input (organic/natural) farming reduces production costs and improves resilience to water stress and price volatility.

promise_components.component: Support organic and integrated farming methods including drip irrigation, natural farming, traditional irrigation systems across agricultural lands
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies four farming methods but provides no specifics on: (a) target area (hectares, % of TN's ~52 lakh ha cultivated area), (b) subsidy per hectare, (c) training provision, (d) certification support, (e) market linkage for organic premium. Reference data: TN ~52 lakh ha cultivated (not in reference, sourced from fact: typical state knowledge), average yield ~2,800 kg/ha (paddy). No budget, no timeline, no coverage metric. Data unavailable — cannot estimate beneficiary count or cost without target acreage.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate without: (a) target hectarage for conversion, (b) subsidy per hectare, (c) training cost. Indicative from national PMKSY-DI (drip irrigation): ₹40,000–60,000/ha subsidy. If 10% of cultivated area (5.2 lakh ha) converts: 5.2 lakh ha × ₹50,000/ha = ₹2,600 cr one-time capital, or ₹260 cr/year over 10 years (0.14% of budget). Organic certification: ₹500–1,000 per farm. Training: ₹5,000–10,000 per farmer. These are estimates only, not grounded in promise data.
sustainability_reasoning: Organic/natural farming and drip irrigation address root causes: (1) groundwater depletion (drip cuts water use by 40–60%), (2) chemical input cost burden (natural farming reduces fertilizer/pesticide spend by 30–50%), (3) soil degradation (organic builds soil carbon and microbial health). Sustainability contingent on: (1) farmer access to organic premium prices (requires buyer contracts, certification), (2) yield stability during transition period (3-year organic certification requires yield sacrifice), (3) knowledge transfer and extension support, (4) market demand sustainability. Risk: if organic premiums collapse or yield drops persistently, farmers revert to conventional methods.

promise_text_en: Provide 5-year agricultural loan benefits including soil testing, improved seeds, and livestock support to farmers; establish veterinary care facilities with government support

impact_mechanism: Subsidized agricultural credit combined with productivity inputs (soil testing, seeds, livestock) reduces farmer borrowing costs and improves per-hectare yields through better agronomic practices.

promise_components.component: 5-year agricultural loan benefits at unspecified interest rate or subsidy level
promise_components.analysis: Promise states '5-year' (likely ₹5 crore budget allocation based on 'amount: 5') but does NOT specify: (a) interest rate subsidy %, (b) loan size per farmer, (c) eligibility criteria, or (d) total beneficiaries. TN has ~83 lakh farmer households; marginal farmers (42 lakh) are typically loan-constrained. WITHOUT loan size and subsidy %, cost cannot be calculated. Data unavailable — cannot estimate total cost.

promise_components.component: Soil testing for all loan-recipient farmers
promise_components.analysis: Soil testing cost is typically ₹50–200/hectare. If targeting 83 lakh farmers with average 1.5 hectare holding = 124.5 lakh hectares: 124.5 lakh ha × ₹100/ha = ₹12.45 crore one-time (or ₹2.5 crore/year if amortized over 5 years). Promise does NOT specify coverage scope; unclear if all farmers or only loan recipients. Data partially unavailable — rough order of magnitude only.

promise_components.component: Improved seeds and livestock support (scope, subsidy % unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: No budget or subsidy percentage stated. Cost depends on: (a) # farmers receiving seeds, (b) subsidy rate, (c) livestock type and # animals per farmer. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.component: Veterinary care facilities with government support
promise_components.analysis: Promise does NOT specify: (a) # of new facilities, (b) construction + operating cost per facility, (c) staffing model, (d) geographic scope. TN has ~3.9 crore livestock (cattle, buffalo, goats, poultry). One government-run veterinary clinic typically serves 10,000–50,000 head of livestock and costs ₹10–20 lakh/year to operate. Without target facility count, cost is data unavailable — cannot calculate.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹5 crore baseline (from 'amount: 5') but INADEQUATE for stated scope. Soil testing alone for all farmers: ₹12.45 crore one-time. Veterinary facilities: if 50 new clinics at ₹15 lakh/year each = ₹7.5 crore/year. TOTAL rough estimate: ₹20–30 crore/year, or ~0.01–0.02% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). HOWEVER, loan subsidy cost (largest component) is NOT quantified in the promise — actual cost could be ₹100+ crore/year depending on interest rate concession. FEASIBILITY: Data-constrained; if ₹5 crore is the only allocation, SEVERELY UNDERFUNDED.
sustainability_reasoning: Improved seeds, soil health, livestock productivity, and credit access are structural inputs that increase farm income and resilience long-term. However, promise lacks detail on subsidy levels, beneficiary targeting, and veterinary network scale — implementation quality and reach are uncertain. Veterinary care is particularly underdeveloped in TN and could be transformative IF adequately funded.

promise_text_en: Establish periodic natural disaster and flood relief schemes for farmers; provide crop insurance at subsidized rates and compensation mechanisms

impact_mechanism: Crop insurance at subsidized rates and disaster relief reduce income volatility and post-shock recovery time for weather-affected farmers.

promise_components.component: Periodic natural disaster and flood relief schemes (scope, beneficiary #, payout amount unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Promise does NOT state: (a) relief amount per farmer, (b) trigger criteria for payouts, (c) # eligible farmers, (d) frequency/schedule of 'periodic' relief. TN Disaster Management Authority exists but scheme design, cost, and coverage targets are not specified in the promise. Data unavailable — cannot calculate cost.

promise_components.component: Crop insurance at subsidized rates (subsidy %, coverage % unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) already operates in TN with ~35–40 lakh farmer enrollments and Centre+State subsidy. Promise does NOT specify: (a) subsidy level (current PMFBY Centre subsidy is ~70% of premium), (b) state's additional subsidy rate, (c) whether promise is to EXPAND existing PMFBY or launch state-specific scheme, (d) # of additional farmers covered. Average PMFBY premium is ~₹500–1,200/season/farmer. If state adds 10% premium subsidy to 40 lakh farmers across 2 seasons: 40 lakh × ₹750 avg premium × 10% × 2 = ₹60 crore/year. But promise target unclear. Data partially unavailable — rough estimate only.

promise_components.component: Compensation mechanisms (payout levels, disbursement timeline unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Promise uses generic language 'compensation mechanisms' WITHOUT specifying: (a) payout as % of crop loss, (b) minimum/maximum claim amount, (c) claim settlement SLA, (d) whether separate from insurance claims or complementary. This is optics-heavy and operationally vague. Data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Budget marker 'amount: 10, 15' suggests ₹10–15 crore allocation, but this is GROSSLY INADEQUATE. Crop insurance subsidy alone for 40 lakh farmers (PMFBY baseline): ₹60 crore/year minimum. If state doubles coverage to 83 lakh farmers: ₹120–150 crore/year. Disaster relief for flood/drought years could add ₹50–100 crore/year in bad years. TOTAL realistic cost: ₹150–250 crore/year, or ~0.08–0.14% of discretionary budget. FEASIBILITY: Promised allocation (₹10–15 cr) is SEVERELY UNDERFUNDED; actual scheme would require ₹150+ crore/year to be credible.
sustainability_reasoning: Crop insurance and disaster relief are genuine income protection tools but DO NOT address root causes: agricultural fragmentation, water scarcity, low soil health, or market volatility. They manage risk after-the-fact rather than building resilience. Promise lacks operational detail (premium subsidy %, payout triggers, claim timelines) making implementation and farmer trust problematic.

promise_text_en: Establish forestry and agroforestry cooperatives with government support; provide one new agricultural cooperative per taluk in forestry-based farming regions

impact_mechanism: Forestry and agroforestry cooperatives create alternative income streams (timber, fruit, forest products) diversifying farm revenue beyond cereal crops.

promise_components.component: Establish forestry and agroforestry cooperatives with government support (# of cooperatives, investment per cooperative unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Promise does NOT state: (a) total # of cooperatives to be formed, (b) government capital/subsidy per cooperative, (c) membership size, (d) geographic focus. TN has ~4,200 agricultural cooperatives already; forestry cooperatives are fewer. If target is to establish 200 new forestry cooperatives at ₹50 lakh capital cost each = ₹100 crore one-time investment. Operating cost per cooperative ~₹10–15 lakh/year; 200 × ₹12 lakh = ₹24 crore/year. Data partially unavailable — rough estimate only.

promise_components.component: One new agricultural cooperative per taluk in forestry-based farming regions (taluk count ~222 in TN, but 'forestry-based regions' scope unclear)
promise_components.analysis: If 'forestry-based regions' means 50 taluks in Western Ghats + forest-adjacent zones: 50 cooperatives × ₹50 lakh = ₹25 crore capital, ₹6 crore/year operating. If interpreted as 222 taluks: ₹111 crore capital, ₹26.6 crore/year operating. Promise is ambiguous on geographic scope. Budget allocation 'amount: 1' (likely ₹1 crore) is INSUFFICIENT for either interpretation.

fiscal_cost_note: Promised ₹1 crore is symbolic. Realistic capital cost for 50–100 forestry cooperatives: ₹25–50 crore one-time. Annual operating cost: ₹6–12 crore. TOTAL: ₹30–60 crore one-time + ₹6–12 crore/year, or ~0.003–0.007% of discretionary budget if annualized. FEASIBILITY: Affordable in budget terms, but ₹1 crore allocation suggests token implementation only.
sustainability_reasoning: Agroforestry diversifies income, improves soil carbon, and creates long-term asset value (timber/fruit trees mature in 5–15 years). This is structural if cooperatives provide genuine market linkage, training, and input credit. However, implementation track record of TN cooperatives is weak (debt-ridden, low member engagement); success depends on governance and member commitment, not just creation.

impact_mechanism: Rural farming communities scheme with modern equipment and technology transfer increases farm productivity and knowledge sharing among peer farmers.

promise_components.component: Create rural farming communities scheme (# of communities, membership model, subsidy level unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Promise uses vague phrase 'rural farming communities' WITHOUT specifying: (a) organizational model (self-help group, cluster, forum?), (b) target # of communities, (c) membership size, (d) government investment per community. This could range from informal farmer clubs (minimal cost) to formally registered cooperatives (₹10–20 lakh per group). Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.component: Support farmer groups with modern agricultural equipment (equipment list, subsidy %, # of beneficiary groups unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Promise does NOT specify: (a) equipment type (tractors, harvesters, planters, pumps?), (b) cost per equipment, (c) ownership model (community pooling vs. individual), (d) subsidy % of cost, (e) # of farmer groups to be supported. Modern equipment cost ranges ₹5 lakh (manually-operated implements) to ₹25+ lakh (tractor). If targeting 10,000 farmer groups with one equipment per group at 50% subsidy: 10,000 × ₹8 lakh × 50% = ₹40 crore one-time. Data unavailable for target scope.

promise_components.component: Technology transfer programs (curriculum, extension staff, beneficiary target unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Likely refers to agronomic training (soil testing, precision irrigation, integrated pest management, etc.). TN has 600+ agricultural extension offices already. Incremental training cost: ₹5,000–10,000 per farmer per year for 2–3 year program. If targeting 5 lakh farmers: 5 lakh × ₹7,500/year × 3 years = ₹112.5 crore total, or ₹37.5 crore/year. Data partially unavailable — rough estimate only.

fiscal_cost_note: NO BUDGET ALLOCATION STATED ('amount: null'). Realistic annual cost estimate: Equipment subsidy (if 10,000 groups, one item per 3 years): ₹13 crore/year. Technology transfer for 5 lakh farmers: ₹37.5 crore/year. TOTAL rough estimate: ₹50 crore/year, or ~0.03% of discretionary budget. FEASIBILITY: Affordable if targeted to 10,000–20,000 farmer groups, but without specified beneficiary target and equipment list, cost is speculative.
sustainability_reasoning: Technology transfer and equipment access are structural if farmer communities sustain operation and maintenance practices post-subsidy. Peer learning reduces extension service dependence. HOWEVER, equipment schemes often result in idle asset use and farmer debt if maintenance/repair costs exceed benefit. Sustainability hinges on strong community governance and viable farm sizes (marginal farmers <1 acre may not benefit from machinery).

promise_text_en: Establish farmer producer organizations (FPOs); support marketing initiatives and ensure fair pricing mechanisms through collective bargaining
promise_text_ta: ê˜‚è¬ó «ï£J¡ î¬ôïèó£è M÷ƒ°Aø¶. Þ‰î ê˜‚è¬ó «ï£Œ ñ†´‹ å¼õ¼‚° õ‰¶M†ì£™ Üî¡ ªî£ì˜„Cò£è Þóˆî Ü¿ˆî‹
impact_mechanism: Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) and collective bargaining reduce post-harvest losses, middleman margins, and price volatility by aggregating supply and accessing better markets.

promise_components.component: Establish Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) at unspecified scale (# of FPOs, membership per FPO, government capital unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Promise does NOT state: (a) target # of FPOs, (b) membership per FPO (typically 200–500 farmers), (c) government capital support per FPO, (d) commodity focus (cereals, vegetables, spices?). India has ~7,000 active FPOs nationally (SFAC data); TN has ~150–200 registered FPOs. Cost per FPO formation: ₹10–15 lakh (registration, office, initial working capital). If TN targets 500 new FPOs at ₹12 lakh each = ₹60 crore one-time. Annual operating cost per FPO: ₹15–20 lakh (staff, certification, marketing); 500 × ₹17.5 lakh = ₹87.5 crore/year. Data partially unavailable.

promise_components.component: Support marketing initiatives (e.g., aggregation centers, direct buyer linkage, branding) — investment per FPO unspecified
promise_components.analysis: Marketing infrastructure (collection center, cold storage, processing unit) costs ₹50–150 lakh per FPO depending on commodity. If 500 FPOs with 200 having storage/processing: 200 × ₹100 lakh = ₹200 crore one-time. OR modest support (branding, buyer directory, digital platform): ₹5–10 lakh/FPO = ₹25–50 crore one-time. Promise is vague on scope. Data unavailable.

promise_components.component: Ensure fair pricing mechanisms through collective bargaining (price floor guarantee, direct buyer contracts — modality unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Fair pricing typically implemented via: (a) MSP-linked procurement (already exists for cereals via civil supplies), (b) direct buyer contracts (private aggregators, exporters, processors), (c) regulated market linkage. Promise does NOT specify mechanism. If it implies state-guaranteed price floor above MSP for non-cereal crops (vegetables, spices), cost depends on: (i) commodity list, (ii) price floor above market, (iii) volume handled. TN vegetable production: ~75 lakh MT/year at wholesale price ₹2,000–3,000/MT. If state guarantees 5% price uplift to farmers: 75 lakh MT × ₹100 (avg 5% buffer) = ₹75 crore/year subsidy. Data partially unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: NO BUDGET ALLOCATION STATED ('amount: null'). Realistic estimate: FPO formation (500 FPOs): ₹60 crore capital, ₹87.5 crore/year operating. Marketing infrastructure (200 centers): ₹200 crore one-time OR ₹25 crore if modest. Fair price guarantee (vegetables): ₹75 crore/year if 5% buffer. TOTAL rough estimate: ₹400+ crore capital one-time, ₹150–250 crore/year operating, or ~0.08–0.14% of discretionary budget. FEASIBILITY: Expensive; would require ₹150–250 crore/year sustained allocation — ABSENT from stated budget.
sustainability_reasoning: FPOs are a structural solution: they reduce post-harvest losses (12–15% nationally), lower farmer marketing costs (save ₹500–1,000/MT by eliminating middlemen), and improve price realization. BUT success depends on: (1) farmer participation discipline (not free-riding), (2) professional management, (3) stable market linkage. TN's FPO survival rate is ~40–50% — many fail within 3–5 years due to poor governance. Promise does NOT address these structural weaknesses; scaling without fixing governance model risks repeated failure.

promise_text_en: Implement C. N. Annadurai Agricultural Research Fund to support innovative farming practices and agricultural mechanization; provide research-backed extension services

scheme_name: C.ð£.ÝFˆîù£˜ ªðòK™ (C. N. Annadurai Agricultural Research Fund)
impact_mechanism: Research fund and extension services embed scientific practices into farming, raising productivity and resilience through evidence-based agronomic innovation.

promise_components.component: Implement C. N. Annadurai Agricultural Research Fund (fund size, research areas, annual allocation unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: Promise names a fund but does NOT specify: (a) total corpus/annual budget, (b) research focus areas (crop breeding, soil health, climate adaptation, farm mechanization?), (c) how funds are disbursed (grants to universities, research institutes?), (d) expected output (varieties, practices, publications). This is a branding exercise with no cost detail. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.component: Support innovative farming practices (conservation agriculture, zero-budget farming, precision irrigation, organic farming — scope unclear)
promise_components.analysis: If targeting 1 lakh farmers with 2-year transition to conservation agriculture at ₹5,000/ha support per farmer: 1 lakh farmers × 1.5 ha avg × ₹5,000 = ₹75 crore one-time. If broader (5 lakh farmers): ₹375 crore one-time. Ongoing monitoring + incentive cost: ₹10–15 crore/year. Promise does NOT specify coverage or subsidy levels. Data unavailable.

promise_components.component: Provide research-backed extension services (# of new extension staff, budget per district, farmer contact modality unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~1 Agricultural Extension Officer per 5,000–10,000 farmers (650+ extension officers exist). If promise means hiring 500 new extension workers at ₹6 lakh salary + ₹2 lakh operating cost each: 500 × ₹8 lakh = ₹40 crore/year. If modest (digital/mobile extension, reducing in-person visits): ₹5–10 crore/year. Data partially unavailable.

promise_components.component: Agricultural mechanization support (machinery subsidy, hire-purchase schemes, custom hiring centers — investment level unspecified)
promise_components.analysis: If bundled with mechanization: machinery subsidy for marginal farmers (<1 acre) is HIGH cost relative to holding size. Typical subsidy: 40–50% of machinery cost. If targeting 42 lakh marginal farmers with one implement per farmer at 50% subsidy: 42 lakh × ₹3 lakh cost × 50% = ₹630 crore one-time. More realistic (10% of marginal farmers): ₹63 crore one-time. Data partially unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: NO BUDGET ALLOCATION STATED ('amount: null'). Realistic estimate: Research fund corpus (if ₹10 crore/year): ₹10 crore/year. Innovative farming support (1 lakh farmers, 2-year cycle): ₹75 crore one-time, ₹15 crore/year ongoing. Extension services (500 staff): ₹40 crore/year. Mechanization (10% of marginal farmers): ₹63 crore one-time. TOTAL rough estimate: ₹200 crore capital one-time, ₹65–75 crore/year operating, or ~0.04–0.04% of discretionary budget. FEASIBILITY: Affordable if modestly scoped, but promise lacks cost specificity; risk of underfunding implementation.
sustainability_reasoning: Research-backed extension and innovation adoption ARE structural: they permanently raise farmer knowledge, enabling adoption of high-efficiency practices (soil testing, precision irrigation, mechanization) that increase net income. TN has strong ICAR institute network (TNAU, Central institutes) to partner with. HOWEVER, extension service delivery in TN is fragmented (state, ICAR, private inputs agents competing); promise does NOT clarify coordination model. Scaling without institutional integration will fragment efforts.

promise_text_en: Increase agricultural universities from 3 to 5 in our government's tenure
