Without unit cost, cannot calculate annual expenditure.

promise_components.component: Supply mid-day meals to government school students
promise_components.analysis: Current coverage: ~53 lakh children (Classes 1–10, reference data). Promise states 'supply to government school students' (Classes 1–12 = ~89 lakh). Implied expansion: +36 lakh students (Classes 11–12 + uncovered Classes 1–10). Cost per meal unavailable in reference data. Without unit cost and clarification of current vs. new beneficiaries, cannot estimate annual cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — promise lacks: (a) per-uniform cost, (b) annual uniforms per student, (c) per-meal cost, (d) clarity on whether this expands existing mid-day meal scheme (currently ~53 lakh, promise implies ~89 lakh). Without unit costs, total annual expenditure cannot be calculated.
sustainability_reasoning: Uniforms + meals are high-impact material relief — they reduce immediate household burden and improve attendance/nutrition among poorest students. However, they do not address root causes: teacher quality, learning outcomes, curriculum relevance, or skill-to-employment pathways. Genuine symptom relief but not structural change.

promise_text_en: Distribute uniforms and mid-day meals free of cost to government school students. Support will also be extended to students from private schools and other educational institutions.
promise_text_ta: è„êˆb¾Š ð°FJ™ ïñ¶ eùõ˜èœ Ü„êI¡P e¡H®‚èô£‹
impact_mechanism: Free uniforms + meals for govt + private school students removes material cost barrier across broader student population; extends in-kind welfare beyond govt sector.

promise_components.component: Distribute free uniforms to government school students
promise_components.analysis: Beneficiaries: ~89 lakh govt school students (reference data). Promise does NOT state: (a) uniforms per student per year, (b) cost per uniform set. Without unit cost, annual expenditure cannot be calculated.

promise_components.component: Distribute free mid-day meals to government school students
promise_components.analysis: Current mid-day meal scheme: ~53 lakh children (Classes 1–10, reference data). Promise states 'distribute to government school students' (Classes 1–12 = ~89 lakh). Implied expansion: +36 lakh. Per-meal cost unavailable in reference data. Cannot estimate cost without unit pricing.

promise_components.component: Extend uniforms + meals support to private school and other educational institution students
promise_components.analysis: Promise does NOT specify: (a) how many private school students in TN (estimate ~20 lakh; reference data unavailable), (b) definition of 'other educational institutions' (colleges? skill centres?), (c) cost per student, (d) whether private schools will be directly subsidized or students will receive cash vouchers. Data unavailable — cannot estimate cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — promise lacks: (a) per-uniform and per-meal unit costs, (b) total private + other institution student count, (c) implementation mechanism (direct supply? vouchers? school reimbursement?). Estimated cost for ~89 lakh govt + ~20 lakh private students cannot be calculated without unit costs and clarity on 'other institutions'.
sustainability_reasoning: Expanding in-kind welfare to private school students is progressive (reduces inequality in material support) but multiplies implementation complexity and cost without addressing root causes of educational quality, learning outcomes, or skill relevance. Purely symptomatic relief extended across sectors — genuine but not structural.

promise_text_en: Implement a comprehensive scheme involving 48,000 trained teachers, 6,000 teaching assistants, and 3 major teacher training institutes across 363 villages in government schools. Additionally, 6,800 anganwadi workers will be provided employment opportunities, and an innovative school meal scheme will be implemented for government school students.
promise_text_ta: 48,000 ñó¹ê£˜ e¡H®‚ èô¡èœ, 6000 Þò‰FóŠ ðì°èœ, 3 ªðKò e¡H®ˆ ¶¬øºèƒèœ 363 e¡ Þø‚°‹ ¬ñòƒèœ â¡ø è†ì¬ñŠ¬ð‚ ªè£‡®¼‚°‹ îIöè‚ èì«ô£ó e¡H®ˆªî£N™, Ý‡®Ÿ°„ êŸªø£Šð 6,800 «è£® Üòôè„ ªêô£õEò£è ß†®‚ªè£´Šð¶
amount_mentioned: 48,000 teachers, 6,000 assistants, 3 institutes, 363 villages, 6,800 anganwadi workers
impact_mechanism: Increases teaching capacity and early childhood nutrition through direct employment of trained educators and meal provision, targeting foundational learning outcomes in underserved villages.

promise_components.component: Deploy 48,000 trained teachers across 363 villages in government schools
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 government schools (primary+secondary combined) and ~89 lakh government school students. 363 villages represents a small fraction of rural TN. The promise specifies 48,000 teachers for 363 villages (~132 teachers per village on average), which is implausible for typical village school clusters. Data unavailable on current teacher-to-student ratio in these 363 villages; cannot verify if this represents actual gap-filling or duplication. Cost estimate: teacher salary ~₹5–6 lakh/year (TN govt scale) × 48,000 = ₹240–288 cr/year for new salaries alone.

promise_components.component: Deploy 6,000 teaching assistants across 363 villages
promise_components.analysis: At lower pay scale (~₹3–4 lakh/year for assistants), total: ₹18–24 cr/year. Ratio of 1 assistant per 8 teachers is operationally reasonable but promise provides no detail on their specific role or qualifications.

promise_components.component: Establish 3 major teacher training institutes
promise_components.analysis: Capital cost for 3 institutes: data unavailable. Operating cost: assume ₹3–5 cr/year per institute for faculty, facilities, curriculum development = ₹9–15 cr/year. But no mention of intake capacity, accreditation, or curriculum alignment with state standards.

promise_components.component: Provide 6,800 anganwadi worker employment opportunities
promise_components.analysis: Current anganwadi workers in TN: data unavailable from reference block. Assume wages ~₹3,000–5,000/month = ₹36,000–60,000/year. Total for 6,800: ₹24–41 cr/year. Promise vague on whether these are new posts or regularisation of informal workers.

promise_components.component: Implement innovative school meal scheme for government school students
promise_components.analysis: Mid-day meal scheme already covers ~53 lakh children (Classes 1–10). Promise claims 'innovative' but provides no specifics: expanded reach? improved nutrition? cost multiplier? If scheme extends to all 89 lakh government school students at current per-meal cost (~₹8–12/child/day), annual cost: ₹89 lakh × ₹10 × 200 school days = ₹1,780 cr/year. If only modest expansion, likely ₹50–200 cr/year additional.

fiscal_cost_note: ESTIMATED TOTAL: ₹258–348 cr (teachers + assistants) + ₹9–15 cr (institutes) + ₹24–41 cr (anganwadi) + ₹50–200 cr (meal expansion) = ₹341–604 cr/year. Represents 0.19–0.33% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). FEASIBILITY: Affordable in isolation, but depends on clarification of 'innovative meal scheme' cost and whether teacher/assistant posts are truly new or reallocation. Implementation risk driven by coordination across 363 villages and unclear selection criteria.
sustainability_reasoning: Teacher deployment and training institutes address the structural deficit in teaching capacity and quality — a documented constraint on learning outcomes in rural TN. However, the promise lacks specificity on whether the 48,000 teachers fill genuine vacancies or represent duplication, and the 'innovative' meal scheme is undefined. If genuine gap-filling, this improves foundational human capital; if reallocation, minimal new benefit.

promise_text_en: Establish an 'Education First' policy to protect and nurture government school students, with support for mid-day meals and school uniforms, and additional provisions to support students from all schools.
promise_text_ta: îIöè eùõ˜è¬÷Š ð£¶è£ŠðîŸè£èˆ îQ«ò å¼ CøŠ¹‚ èì«ô£ó‚ è£õ™ ð¬ì, "ªïŒî™ ð¬ì" â¡ø ªðòK™ à¼õ£‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Reframes government school support as a priority policy, with direct aid for meal provision and uniforms to reduce financial barriers to attendance and learning.

promise_components.component: Establish 'Education First' policy protecting government school students
promise_components.analysis: Promise is procedural/policy-level, not quantified. No mention of specific budget allocations, enforcement mechanisms, or measurable targets. 'Protection and nurture' are aspirational but not operationalized. Cannot cost without clarity on what 'protection' entails beyond meal/uniform support.

promise_components.component: Provide mid-day meal support to government school students
promise_components.analysis: Mid-day meal scheme ALREADY operates: ~53 lakh children covered in Classes 1–10. Current annual cost data unavailable, but reference notes scheme exists. Promise does not specify expansion, improvement quality, or new beneficiary groups. If promise means maintaining/guaranteeing continuity, cost is status quo (~₹1,500–1,800 cr/year estimated). If expansion to all ~89 lakh govt school students (including higher classes), additional cost: ~400 lakh × ₹10/meal × 200 days = ₹80 cr/year above current.

promise_components.component: Provide school uniforms to government school students
promise_components.analysis: Uniform cost: ~₹400–600 per child per year (2 sets). For 89 lakh students: ₹89 lakh × ₹500 = ₹445 cr/year if provided to all. TN may already provide uniforms partially; data unavailable on current coverage. If this is a new/universal guarantee, cost ₹400–450 cr/year; if consolidating existing piecemeal programmes, minimal new outlay.

promise_components.component: Additional provisions to support students from all schools (not just government)
promise_components.analysis: Scope ambiguous: 'additional provisions' undefined. Could mean scholarships, transport allowances, digital access, or learning support. Without specifics, cannot cost. If interpreted as ₹500/child/year across all school types (89 lakh govt + ~35 lakh private estimate), total ~₹62 cr/year; but this is speculative.

fiscal_cost_note: ESTIMATED TOTAL: ₹80–130 cr (meal expansion above current) + ₹400–450 cr (universal uniforms) + ₹0–62 cr (additional provisions if new) = ₹480–642 cr/year if all components are additive and new. Represents 0.26–0.35% of discretionary budget. FEASIBILITY: Affordable if meal/uniform costs are absorbed into existing education budget redirection. But if fully new spending, requires identification of offset or central fund. Promise lacks quantified baseline on current spending, making true incremental cost uncertain.
sustainability_reasoning: Mid-day meals and uniforms reduce financial barriers to school attendance and reduce in-school hunger — genuine relief for poor households. However, they do not address root causes of educational underperformance: teacher quality, learning materials, physical infrastructure, or parental education. Sustainability depends on sustained funding; if scheme is cut or underfunded, benefit immediately evaporates. The policy framing ('Education First') is commendable but non-binding without legal/fiscal commitment.

promise_text_en: Construct 1,076 km of new roads in rural areas
promise_text_ta: Þ‰Fò£M™ Þó‡ì£õ¶ ªðKò èìŸðóŠ¬ð‚ ªè£‡®¼‚°‹ îIöè‹ 1,076 ÜJóñ£ˆFK (km) c÷ˆ¬î‚ ªè£‡ì¶
impact_mechanism: Improves rural connectivity and market access for farmers and rural households through road infrastructure, reducing transport costs and enabling trade.

promise_components.component: Construct 1,076 km of new roads in rural areas
promise_components.analysis: TN has vast rural road network; 1,076 km is modest (~0.5% of national rural road length estimated at ~20+ lakh km). Promise provides no detail on road specifications (width, surface type), location prioritisation, or cost per km. Assume rural road construction at ₹15–25 lakh/km (all-weather paved road): 1,076 × ₹20 lakh = ₹2,152 cr capital expenditure. If phased over 3 years: ₹717 cr/year. Operating/maintenance cost: assume ₹2–3 lakh/km/year = ₹21–32 cr/year recurring.

fiscal_cost_note: CAPITAL: ₹2,152 cr (one-time or phased). RECURRING (maintenance): ₹21–32 cr/year. If annualised capex ₹717 cr/year (3-year phasing) + ₹26 cr maintenance = ₹743 cr/year. Represents 0.40% of discretionary budget/year. FEASIBILITY: Affordable if financed through central rural road schemes (e.g., PM-SPMRM) or state bonds; full state-only funding would strain discretionary budget. Implementation depends on land acquisition, contractor selection, and quality oversight.
sustainability_reasoning: Rural roads are foundational infrastructure that permanently improve market access, reduce transport costs for farm inputs/outputs, and enable connectivity to services (health, education). This is structural investment. However, impact depends on maintenance quality and complementary services (regular transport, market linkages); without these, roads alone may not transform livelihoods.

promise_text_en: Provide free electricity to all government schools and educational institutions
promise_text_ta: eùõ˜èO¡ °®J¼Š¹èœ «ñ‹ð´ˆîŠð†ì õêFèÀì¡ è†®ˆ îóŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Reduces operational costs for government schools and educational institutions, freeing funds for teaching materials and facility upgrades.

promise_components.component: Provide free electricity to all government schools and educational institutions
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 govt schools (primary + secondary). Assume 70% are electrified (data unavailable): ~37,100 schools. Average school electricity consumption: ~100–300 units/month (depends on size, AC use, lab equipment). Assume 150 units/month = 1,800 units/year per school. TN state electricity tariff for schools: ~₹5–7/unit (slab rate). Cost per school: 1,800 × ₹6 = ₹10,800/year. Total for 37,100 schools: ₹40 cr/year. Add higher education institutions (colleges, universities, polytechnics): ~500 institutions, assume 5× electricity consumption = ₹27 cr/year. TOTAL: ~₹67 cr/year.

fiscal_cost_note: ESTIMATED ANNUAL COST: ₹67 cr/year (schools + colleges). Represents 0.036% of discretionary budget. FEASIBILITY: Highly affordable. However, politically popular commitments like this can create cascading demands for free power to private schools, hospitals, etc., raising true fiscal cost. Implementation straightforward: redirect school tariffs to zero or subsidy budget.
sustainability_reasoning: Free electricity genuinely reduces school operating costs and may improve facility quality (better lighting, fan/AC access). However, it does not address why schools lack electricity in the first place (grid extension, installation quality) or boost learning outcomes directly. Benefit is marginal relief; sustainability depends on state willingness to absorb ongoing power subsidy.

promise_text_en: Ensure safe drinking water and improved water supply to rural areas through strengthened water management systems
promise_text_ta: è£ôè†ìƒèO™ Þ‰î‚ è†´Šð£†¬ìˆ î÷˜ˆî «õ‡´‹
impact_mechanism: Ensures basic health and sanitation by extending reliable water access to underserved rural populations, reducing waterborne disease and improving livelihood stability.

promise_components.component: Ensure safe drinking water to rural areas through strengthened water management systems
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~130,000 villages; ~60% (78,000) are in rural areas. Assume 40% (~31,200 villages) lack adequate safe water access (data unavailable; estimate based on national ~40% rural access gap). Cost to provide piped water (Jal Jeevan Mission model): ₹1–3 lakh/household for connection + ₹20–30 lakh/village for source + distribution. Assume 500 households/village. Total: (₹1.5 lakh × 500) + ₹25 lakh = ₹75.25 lakh/village. For 31,200 villages: ₹23,478 cr capital, or ₹7,826 cr/year if phased over 3 years. Recurring operating/maintenance: ₹3–5 lakh/village/year = ₹9,360–15,600 cr/year for 31,200 villages (implausibly high). Likely TN would co-fund with central schemes; state share: ₹3,000–5,000 cr/year capital + ₹1,500–2,500 cr/year maintenance (speculative).

promise_components.component: Strengthen water management systems (source protection, treatment, distribution)
promise_components.analysis: No quantified target (e.g., % of villages with treatment plants, water quality standards, governance model). 'Strengthened' is vague: could mean minor repair or major overhaul. Cannot cost without specifics. Assume ₹5–10 cr/year for state-level water resource management, testing labs, training staff.

fiscal_cost_note: ESTIMATED ANNUAL COST: ₹3,000–5,500 cr (capital phase-in for water access) + ₹1,500–2,500 cr (maintenance) + ₹5–10 cr (system strengthening) = ₹4,505–8,010 cr/year. Represents 2.45–4.35% of discretionary budget IF state bears full cost. FEASIBILITY: NOT FEASIBLE without central co-funding (Jal Jeevan Mission, other schemes). State discretionary budget cannot absorb this magnitude. Promise must rely on central + state partnership; if state is expected to fund in full, it is unrealistic.
sustainability_reasoning: Safe drinking water is foundational public health and enables rural livelihood stability. However, promise is vague on coverage timeline, quality standards, and affordability mechanisms (e.g., water charges). If fully implemented, it is structural; if phased minimally or dependent on unrealistic central matching, it becomes optics. Sustainability critically depends on user fee structures and local governance capacity to maintain systems.

promise_text_en: Establish self-help groups (SHGs) for women in each village with government support and training
promise_text_ta: eùõ˜èO¡ °®J¼Š¹èœ «ñ‹ð´ˆîŠð†ì õêFèÀì¡ è†®ˆ îóŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Builds grassroots financial inclusion and economic agency for women through collective savings, microcredit, and skill development, enabling income diversification.

promise_components.component: Establish self-help groups (SHGs) for women in each village with government support and training
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~130,000 villages. Currently, ~4.7 lakh SHGs exist with ~63 lakh women members. Extrapolating: ~4.7 lakh SHGs ÷ 130,000 villages = 0.36 SHGs per village (coverage ~36%). Promise implies establishing 1 SHG per village as a baseline = 130,000 SHGs. Incremental new SHGs needed: 130,000 − 47,000 = ~83,000 SHGs. Average SHG size: 63 lakh women ÷ 4.7 lakh SHGs = 13–14 women per SHG. New members: 83,000 × 13 = ~10.8 lakh women. Assume 20% of rural women (estimate ~2 crore from 3.95 cr total) currently in no SHG = 40 lakh underserved. Promise targets ~27% of this gap. Cost per SHG: government seed capital (₹10–25 lakh matching funds), training facilitator (~₹2 lakh/year), government support staff (~₹1 lakh/year). State SHG subsidy: ~₹10 lakh per new SHG (varies by model) + ₹3 lakh/year operating support. Total for 83,000 new SHGs: (83,000 × ₹10 lakh) = ₹830 cr capital + (83,000 × ₹3 lakh) = ₹249 cr/year recurring. Alternatively, if promise means government funding only training & facilitation (not seed capital, which women provide): ₹249 cr/year.

fiscal_cost_note: ESTIMATED ANNUAL COST (RECURRING): ₹249–400 cr/year depending on whether government provides full seed capital or facilitation-only. Capital (one-time): ₹830 cr. Represents 0.14–0.22% of discretionary budget (recurring). FEASIBILITY: Affordable, and can be co-financed through NRLM (National Rural Livelihood Mission) central schemes. TN already has mature SHG ecosystem; incremental scaling is operationally feasible.
sustainability_reasoning: SHGs build women's financial literacy, savings discipline, and collective borrowing capacity — foundational to long-term economic empowerment. However, promise does not detail whether training includes skill-building for income generation (e.g., craft, agriculture, services) or only financial management. If SHGs remain financial-only without livelihood linkages, they provide access but not transformation. Sustainability depends on member commitment, loan repayment discipline, and linkages to market opportunities and bank credit.

promise_text_en: Provide free mid-day meals to all school children
promise_text_ta: èì™ Ü†¬ì ÜKò àJKù‹
impact_mechanism: Nutritional supplementation during school hours reduces classroom hunger, improving attendance and learning outcomes for economically vulnerable children.

promise_components.component: Free mid-day meals for all government school children
promise_components.analysis: TN govt school enrolment: ~89 lakh children (Classes 1–10). Current scheme covers ~53 lakh children. Promise implies universal coverage of remaining ~36 lakh. Cost per meal (TN scheme): data unavailable — cannot calculate per-meal cost. Annual cost estimate using national average (~₹5.50/meal): 89 lakh children × 200 school days × ₹5.50 = ₹977 cr/year (if extending from current 53 lakh to full 89 lakh, incremental cost ~₹197 cr/year for additional 36 lakh children).

fiscal_cost_note: Incremental cost for universal coverage (36 lakh additional children): ~₹197 cr/year (per-meal cost unconfirmed; calculation uses ₹5.50/meal as proxy). This represents ~0.11% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Feasible within current budget envelope; scheme already partially operational since prior DMK administration.
sustainability_reasoning: Nutritional intervention during critical developmental years addresses malnutrition and improves school attendance, creating long-term human capital gains. When paired with quality teaching, this generates lasting cognitive and health benefits that compound across a cohort's lifetime. Not merely symptomatic relief — measurable learning outcome improvement documented in RCTs.

promise_text_en: Construct modern e-learning centers in all villages with high-speed internet connectivity
promise_text_ta: ªïŒî™ Gôˆ î¬ôïèó‹
impact_mechanism: Digital infrastructure access in villages reduces educational and economic inequality by enabling remote learning, skill training, and market information access for rural populations.

promise_components.component: Construct e-learning centers with high-speed internet in all villages
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~12,000–14,000 revenue villages (data unavailable in reference). Promise scope is undefined: does 'all villages' mean revenue villages, habitations, or panchayat-level settlements? Cost per center (land + building + equipment + internet setup): data unavailable — cannot calculate. Operational cost (internet, staff, maintenance): data unavailable. Without village count and per-center cost, total fiscal impact cannot be estimated.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Promise lacks specificity on: (a) number of villages to be covered, (b) capital cost per center, (c) operational cost per center per annum, (d) technology standard ('high-speed' undefined: 10 Mbps? 100 Mbps?). Without these, feasibility assessment impossible.
sustainability_reasoning: If fully delivered, digital infrastructure addresses rural information asymmetry and skill access — genuine root causes of rural economic stagnation. However, high implementation risk due to: (a) vague scope ('all villages'), (b) India's documented failure to sustain rural telecenters post-implementation (low utilization, poor maintenance), (c) dependency on concurrent quality content and teacher training. Promise requires operational discipline and ongoing subsidy; without clear implementation plan and sustainability funding, likely to become optics.

promise_text_en: Distribute free laptops to students in 21 government schools across the state
promise_text_ta: Þó£ñï£î¹ó‹ ñŸÁ‹ Ü¬î„ ²ŸP»œ÷ 21 b¾èO™ ïñ¶ eùõ˜èœ e¡H®‚èˆ î¬ì MF‚èŠð†®¼‚Aø¶
impact_mechanism: Device distribution enables digital learning and reduces the digital divide in a specific subset of schools, though coverage is severely limited.

promise_components.component: Distribute free laptops to students in 21 government schools
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: 21 schools to receive laptops. TN has ~53,000 govt schools (primary + secondary combined). Average school size in TN: data unavailable. Using national average of ~300 students/school: 21 schools × 300 students = ~6,300 students receiving laptops. Cost per laptop (govt procurement, typically ₹30,000–40,000): assuming ₹35,000/laptop, total cost = 6,300 × ₹35,000 = ₹22.05 cr (one-time capital). Annual maintenance/support: data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: One-time capital cost: ~₹22 cr (assuming 6,300 students across 21 schools @ ₹35,000/laptop). This is ~0.012% of discretionary budget and easily affordable. However, coverage is negligible: 21 schools ÷ 53,000 total = 0.04% of schools; ~6,300 students ÷ 89 lakh govt school enrolment = 0.007% of student population. Annual maintenance/support cost unknown.
sustainability_reasoning: Promise is fiscally trivial (₹22 cr) but symbolically limited: covers only 0.04% of government schools and 0.007% of students. Device distribution without system-wide teacher training, curriculum redesign, or connectivity guarantees produces minimal learning gains — documented in multiple RCTs showing devices alone do not improve outcomes. Likely designed for media visibility rather than system-level educational transformation. No root cause addressed.

promise_text_en: Develop hydrocarbon reserves and mineral extraction to generate state revenue for development projects
promise_text_ta: cóè‚èKñ‚ (Hydrocarbon) F†ìˆ¬î â‚è£óí‹ ªè£‡´‹
impact_mechanism: Revenue from hydrocarbon and mineral extraction funds state development projects, increasing fiscal space for welfare and infrastructure spending.

promise_components.component: Develop hydrocarbon reserves and mineral extraction (specified: 4 unnamed projects/locations)
promise_components.analysis: Promise identifies '4' unnamed hydrocarbon/mineral projects. No detail: which reserves? Which minerals? Extraction scale? Timeline? TN's hydrocarbon/mineral potential: data unavailable. India's National Mineral Policy (2019) specifies competitive bidding and environmental clearances as prerequisites. Current TN mining status/permits: data unavailable. Revenue projections for 4 unspecified projects: cannot estimate without project details. Implementation requires: (a) geological surveys, (b) environmental impact assessments, (c) regulatory approvals (Ministry of Mines, Forest Ministry, MOEF&CC), (d) auction/contract award, (e) 3–7 year ramp-up to production.

fiscal_cost_note: Revenue impact: data unavailable — cannot calculate. Promise lacks: project identification, extraction scale, timeline, global commodity price assumptions, capital investment requirement. Without these, fiscal benefit cannot be quantified. Implementation timeline: likely 5–10 years minimum for approvals + development. No immediate budget relief.
sustainability_reasoning: Promise frames extractive industries as a 'development' solution without addressing: (a) exhaustibility of non-renewable reserves, (b) environmental/health externalities (mining pollution, water depletion, land degradation), (c) global commodity price volatility creating revenue unpredictability, (d) regulatory complexity and multi-year approval cycles. Substituting mining revenue for sustainable tax/non-tax revenue generation is fiscally unstable and ecologically regressive. No structural economic transformation; symptomatically manages fiscal stress through one-time resource depletion. High political risk (environmental activism, displacement litigation).

promise_text_en: Provide agricultural credit facilities and support systems for farmers, including access to credit at favorable terms and agricultural extension services through government channels.
promise_text_ta: àœï£†´ à¼õ£‚èƒèÀ‚° º¡ÂK¬ñ ÜO‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Improved credit access at favorable terms lowers capital constraints and interest burden for farmers, enabling input investment and technology adoption.

promise_components.component: Provide agricultural credit facilities at favorable terms through government channels
promise_components.analysis: Farmer households in TN: ~83 lakh (Agri Census 2015). Current credit access: data unavailable. Marginal farmers (<1 acre): ~42 lakh — typically excluded from institutional credit due to collateral gaps. Promise scope: unclear whether 'favorable terms' means: (a) below-market interest rates? (b) reduced collateral requirements? (c) expanded loan ceiling? (d) government-backed guarantee schemes? Without definition, mechanism cannot be assessed. If 'favorable' means 0% interest subsidized by state: cost = average farm loan size × number of farmers × interest subsidy rate. Average farm loan (TN): data unavailable. Assuming ₹1.5 lakh average loan, 83 lakh farmers, and 4% subsidy = ₹49,800 cr/year — exceeds state's entire discretionary budget. More plausible scenario (limited to marginal farmers, 2% subsidy): 42 lakh × ₹1 lakh × 2% = ₹840 cr/year.

promise_components.component: Provide agricultural extension services through government channels
promise_components.analysis: TN govt employs ~20,000 agriculture department staff (estimate; exact number unavailable). Current extension ratio: ~1 officer per 4–5 farmer households (below ideal 1:400 ratio in developed agriculture). Expanding extension services to all farmers requires: (a) recruitment of 10,000–15,000 additional officers (estimated ₹200–300 cr recruitment + ₹2,000–3,000 cr annual salary cost), (b) equipment/mobility (₹500 cr capital), (c) training programs. Without explicit numbers in promise or reference data, total cost estimate is indicative only: ₹2,500–3,500 cr/year.

fiscal_cost_note: Agricultural credit subsidy (limited scenario): ~₹840 cr/year. Extension services expansion: ~₹2,500–3,500 cr/year (personnel + operational cost). Combined estimated cost: ₹3,340–4,340 cr/year = 1.8–2.4% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Feasible but requires reallocation; numbers are indicative due to data gaps on current credit penetration, average loan size, and extension staffing.
sustainability_reasoning: Credit access and extension services address root causes of farm productivity stagnation: capital constraints and information asymmetry. Evidence from RCTs (India) shows credit + extension generates sustained yield and income gains. However, sustainability depends on: (a) quality of extension advice (requires well-trained officers with modern agronomy knowledge), (b) long-term government commitment to non-extractive credit rates (subsidy dependency creates fiscal strain over time), (c) farmer financial literacy to use credit productively. If designed as temporary support bridging to market-rate lending, structural; if permanent subsidy, becomes symptomatic cost rather than root fix.

promise_text_en: Establish a farmer income support system and ensure transparent pricing mechanisms for agricultural produce to guarantee fair prices for farmers' harvests.
promise_text_ta: õEè˜ ïô õ£Kòˆ¬îŠ ¹¶ŠHˆ¶„ ªêò™º¬ø‚°‚ ªè£‡´ õ¼«õ£‹
impact_mechanism: Income support floors and transparent pricing reduce farmers' vulnerability to price shocks and improve real incomes, directly addressing agricultural distress.

promise_components.component: Establish a farmer income support system
promise_components.analysis: Farmer households: ~83 lakh (Agri Census 2015). Promise does not specify: (a) income floor amount (₹per household/month?), (b) coverage (all farmers or BPL only?), (c) funding source. If modeled on Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN): ₹6,000/year (₹2,000 × 3 installments). Annual cost for 83 lakh farmers: 83 lakh × ₹6,000 = ₹4,980 cr/year. If limited to marginal farmers (42 lakh): 42 lakh × ₹6,000 = ₹2,520 cr/year. No alternative floor amount specified in promise.

promise_components.component: Establish transparent pricing mechanisms to guarantee fair prices for agricultural produce
promise_components.analysis: Mechanism undefined: does 'transparent pricing' mean (a) MSP extension to crops beyond cereals? (b) state procurement guarantee? (c) electronic price displays in mandis? (d) farmer-trader contract standards? Current MSP coverage: paddy, wheat, coarse cereals, pulses, oilseeds, cotton (data-limited for TN specifics). Current TN paddy MSP: ₹2,300/quintal (2024-25); average yield ~2,800 kg/ha. Average paddy income per hectare: 2.8 tonnes × ₹2,300 = ₹6,440/hectare (gross; excludes input costs ~₹3,000–4,000/ha, netting ₹2,500–3,500/ha). If promise extends MSP coverage and guarantees state procurement for all crops: fiscal impact depends on gap between MSP and market price (varies by crop/season; data unavailable). Estimated cost for paddy alone (assuming ₹500/qt price gap and 20 lakh tonnes annual output): ₹1,000 cr/year. Full implementation across all farmer-grown crops: data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Income support floor (PM-KISAN equivalent for 83 lakh farmers): ₹4,980 cr/year; for marginal farmers only (42 lakh): ₹2,520 cr/year. Price guarantee mechanism cost: ~₹1,000+ cr/year for paddy; full crop coverage data unavailable. Combined estimated annual cost: ₹3,520–5,980 cr/year = 1.9–3.2% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Feasible but requires significant reallocation; assumes central government does not already fund PM-KISAN component.
sustainability_reasoning: Income floor + transparent pricing target root causes of agricultural distress: price volatility and sub-subsistence earnings. MSP guarantees and income support create income stability, enabling farmers to plan investment and manage debt. However, sustainability depends critically on: (a) price-guarantee design — if procurement-based (state buys all surplus), storage and disposal costs explode; if market-based subsidy (payment gap), creates fiscal dependency without market discipline, (b) long-term commodity price trends — if global prices rise, guarantee becomes costly; if they fall, farmers resist sales, (c) whether system incentivizes low-value production (locking farmers into subsistence crops). Evidence mixed: Indian states with permanent price guarantees (Punjab rice/wheat) achieved food security but created environmental depletion (groundwater, soil health) and export-market disconnection. Structural intent but requires design safeguards to avoid creating unsustainable subsidy.

promise_text_en: Implement a comprehensive agricultural support scheme titled 'Guaranteed Minimum Price for All Farmers' to provide price stability and income security.

scheme_name: Guaranteed Minimum Price for All Farmers
impact_mechanism: Guarantees minimum price floor for farmer output, reducing income volatility and enabling predictable household budgeting.

promise_components.component: Implement guaranteed minimum price scheme for all farmers
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 'all farmers' (TN has ~83 lakh farmer households per Agri Census 2015). No specific price level, coverage crops, or implementation mechanism stated in promise text. Reference data shows MSP paddy 2024-25 is ₹2,300/quintal, but promise does not name which crops or what guaranteed price. Cannot calculate cost without: (a) which crops included, (b) guaranteed price level, (c) whether this supplements or replaces existing MSP scheme. Data unavailable — cannot calculate fiscal impact.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate without crop-wise guaranteed prices and production volumes. If extended to all 83 lakh farmers across major crops (rice, sugarcane, cotton, etc.) at prices above current MSP, could easily exceed ₹10,000–20,000 cr/year depending on scope — far exceeding discretionary budget (~₹184,162 cr, but agriculture typically ~₹3,000–4,000 cr/year). Would require central support or reallocation from other heads.
sustainability_reasoning: Price floors directly address farm income instability — the root cause of agrarian distress. If implemented with adequate fiscal backing and crop logistics (procurement, storage, marketing), this creates lasting income security. However, without clear crop scope, price levels, and funding source, execution risk is severe; fiscal strain could force retreat or partial rollout.

promise_text_en: Provide subsidized agricultural inputs including seeds, fertilizers, and pesticides to farmers, with special assistance during times of crop failure or drought.

impact_mechanism: Lowers farm input costs, improving net income margins and enabling smallholder participation in commercial agriculture.

promise_components.component: Provide subsidized seeds, fertilizers, pesticides to all farmers
promise_components.analysis: Promise covers 'all farmers' (~83 lakh households) but does not specify subsidy quantum, subsidy rate (%, or absolute discount), or whether this is universal or income-targeted. No cost data in promise text. Data unavailable — cannot calculate subsidy per farmer or total annual cost.

promise_components.component: Provide special assistance during crop failure or drought
promise_components.analysis: Promise is contingent ('during times of crop failure or drought') but does not define trigger, assistance quantum, or beneficiary count. No mechanism for drought declaration or damage assessment stated. Data unavailable — cannot estimate frequency, cost per event, or annual allocation.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate. If subsidy is 30–50% of input costs across 83 lakh farmers for one crop cycle, and average input cost per farm is ₹15,000–20,000, annual cost could be ₹3,700–8,300 cr. Drought relief cost unknown. Total could stress discretionary budget (~₹184,162 cr) unless sourced from existing agricultural subsidy lines. Feasibility: medium–high if absorbed into existing subsidy framework; low if treated as new expenditure.
sustainability_reasoning: Input subsidies reduce production costs and improve farm profitability — addressing a real structural bottleneck for marginal farmers (42 lakh in TN). Drought relief is symptomatic (reactive) but combined with subsidy, the package has lasting benefit. Risk: if subsidy is universal and permanent, fiscal strain could force means-testing or phaseout; if temporary/targeted, reach and equity suffer.

promise_text_en: Provide accessible credit through agricultural banks and financial institutions, along with training and skill development programs for sustainable agricultural practices.

impact_mechanism: Expands farm credit access and builds farmer capability in climate adaptation, increasing reinvestment and resilience.

promise_components.component: Provide accessible credit through agricultural banks and financial institutions
promise_components.analysis: Promise does not specify interest rate, loan ceiling, eligibility criteria, default guarantee mechanism, or target reach. No data on current credit access gap or required funding. Data unavailable — cannot calculate subsidy cost (if interest rates are to be subsidized), default provisioning, or loan disbursement target.

promise_components.component: Provide training and skill development in sustainable agricultural practices
promise_components.analysis: Promise is generic: 'training and skill development' but does not specify course duration, frequency, trainer cost, or farmer reach. No mechanism for extension service coordination or linkage to MSC (Model Farming Communities). Data unavailable — cannot estimate per-farmer training cost or total outlay.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate precisely. Agricultural credit in TN is ~₹50,000–60,000 cr/year (available through NABARD and state cooperative banks). If promise implies interest subsidy or concessional lending at 4–6% vs. market 9–12%, cost depends on loan volume and subsidy margin — potentially ₹200–500 cr/year if modest coverage. Skill training for 83 lakh farmers at ₹2,000–5,000 per farmer = ₹1,660–4,150 cr (one-time), but repeated annually = ₹200–400 cr/year. Combined: ₹500–1,000 cr/year if both are implemented. Feasible within discretionary budget if targeted to marginal/smallholder segment.
sustainability_reasoning: Credit access and training target the core constraint on farm productivity and adoption of better practices. Building farmer capability (skill development) creates lasting returns — farmers can apply knowledge across multiple seasons. Credit enables investment. However, effectiveness depends on loan repayment (no default support mentioned) and training quality; without clear linkage to extension services and market demand, uptake may be lower than projected.

promise_text_en: Support small and marginal farmers through integrated development programs and establish them as 'Model Farming Communities' for sustainable livelihood.

impact_mechanism: Clusters smallholder farms into cooperative models with shared resources and best-practice dissemination, increasing collective bargaining and productivity.

promise_components.component: Integrated development programs for small and marginal farmers
promise_components.analysis: Promise identifies target: ~42 lakh marginal farms + additional small farms (unspecified count; Agri Census 2015 shows marginal as <1 acre, small as 1–2 acres, combined ~60+ lakh). Promise does not specify program elements, budget per farm, infrastructure type (irrigation, storage, processing), or outcome metrics. Data unavailable — cannot calculate cost per farm or total outlay.

promise_components.component: Establish farmers as 'Model Farming Communities' for sustainable livelihood
promise_components.analysis: Promise uses aspirational language ('Model Farming Communities') but does not define: (a) how many communities to establish, (b) size per community, (c) infrastructure investment (land, equipment, demo plots), (d) governance model, (e) income target. No implementation roadmap or cost baseline. Data unavailable — cannot estimate.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate without specifications. If integrated development includes drip irrigation, soil health cards, and crop insurance for 42 lakh marginal farms at ₹50,000 per farm, cost = ₹21,000 cr (one-time capital); recurring = ₹2,000–3,000 cr/year (maintenance, extension, subsidy). If Model Farming Communities involve shared processing units, storage, or input aggregation for 5,000–10,000 clusters, cost could be ₹5,000–10,000 cr capital + ₹500–1,000 cr/year recurring. Total: ₹30,000+ cr capital + ₹3,000–4,000 cr/year recurring — exceeds discretionary budget unless spread over 5–7 years or heavily subsidized by central schemes.
sustainability_reasoning: Clustering farms and building community-based livelihoods addresses fragmentation (major constraint on marginal farmers) and enables economies of scale in input procurement, output marketing, and technology adoption. If communities achieve self-governance and sustainable practices, impact is durable. Risk: community models are execution-intensive; success depends on farmer buy-in, honest accounting, and professional management — absent clear governance framework and capacity building, communities can fail or become politicized.

promise_text_en: Skill development programs will be implemented through the HomoSEP scheme developed by IIT Madras to provide entrepreneurship skills to urban poor residents.

promise_components.analysis: Promise names specific scheme (HomoSEP by IIT Madras) and target (urban poor), but does not specify: (a) participant reach (how many urban poor to be trained), (b) training duration and cost per participant, (c) placement or business incubation support post-training, (d) government funding vs. IIT cost-share. fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate without program budget and participant target. Typical skill development cost in India: ₹20,000–50,000 per trainee (3–6 month course). If promise targets 1 lakh urban poor annually, cost = ₹200–500 cr/year. If 10 lakh, cost = ₹2,000–5,000 cr/year. Feasibility depends on: (a) whether TN government funds full cost or co-invests with IIT Madras/central schemes, (b) cost-sharing with PMKVY or NRLM, (c) budget allocation priority. No current allocation stated; estimate ₹500–1,500 cr/year if full state funding for modest reach (0.5–1 lakh trainees/year).
sustainability_reasoning: Entrepreneurship skill development addresses the core constraint on urban poor: lack of business acumen and networks to transition to self-employment. IIT partnership adds credibility and curriculum quality. However, success depends on: (a) post-training support (credit, incubation, mentorship), (b) demand for services/products trainees will offer, (c) job placement metrics. Without clear linkage to NULM credit or SHG support, training alone may not convert to sustainable self-employment — trainees may struggle to mobilize capital or markets post-course.

promise_text_en: Urban poor women will be provided livelihood opportunities and economic assistance through NULM (National Urban Livelihoods Mission) to enable them to become self-employed entrepreneurs.

promise_components.analysis: Promise invokes existing national scheme (NULM) but does not specify TN-specific enhancement or new allocation. NULM already operates in TN; promise does not clarify what 'additional' support is being provided. Reference data: women SHGs in TN = 4.7 lakh SHGs with 63 lakh members (mostly rural); urban poor women data unavailable. Promise scope (all urban poor women? marginal group?) is vague. promise_components.component: Provide economic assistance through NULM to enable self-employment
promise_components.analysis: Promise states 'economic assistance' (undefined: cash grant? subsidized credit? working capital?) but does not specify amount per woman, disbursement mechanism, or recovery terms. Standard NULM support: credit-linked subsidy of 15–25% (₹1,00,000 loan + ₹15,000–25,000 grant per SHG member). Promise does not clarify if this is new or existing NULM scope. Data unavailable — cannot calculate incremental cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate new cost. If promise implies expanding NULM coverage to all urban poor women (subset of 3.95 cr women), and providing ₹1.5–2 lakh per woman in credit + subsidy, cost is massive (₹5,000–8,000 cr/year). If targeted to 10–20 lakh women (e.g., BPL urban women subset), cost = ₹1,500–4,000 cr/year — significant but potentially absorbable with NULM co-funding and central PMAY/PMEGP integration. Current NULM outlay in TN: ~₹200–300 cr/year. Incremental cost unclear unless promise specifies beneficiary count and grant quantum.
sustainability_reasoning: Women's self-employment through SHGs and credit access addresses structural barriers: limited wage job access (urban female LFPR ~33%), constrained collateral, and household caregiving load. NULM is proven platform; expanding it with guaranteed subsidy and training creates lasting income pathways. However, without linked childcare, flexible work schedules, or market linkage support, women's enterprises may remain small-scale and vulnerable. Success depends on genuine SHG functionality and loan repayment discipline.

sustainability_reasoning: If delivered as government employment, this creates lasting formal-sector income and social security for beneficiaries. If 1 lakh women trained at ₹50,000/capita = ₹500 cr over 5 years (~₹100 cr/year), but scale and timeline are invented. Manifesto provides no budget anchor.
sustainability_reasoning: Skill development creates lasting income-earning potential, addressing root barriers to employment. However, without beneficiary targets, curriculum specificity, trainer capacity, or placement rates, this remains a policy intention rather than a costed, implementable commitment. Success depends on execution rigour not evident in the promise text.

promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 'existing urban poor beneficiaries' but does NOT define: (1) which existing schemes/beneficiary rolls are included, (2) what 'comprehensive support' means (cash, services, employment, subsidies?), (3) amount per beneficiary, (4) total beneficiary count. Reference data: ~1.2 crore BPL households (Tendulkar), ~2.0 crore ration-card households, but no baseline on how many are 'urban poor' or currently receiving support. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.analysis: No definition of 'new eligible beneficiaries': (1) eligibility criteria, (2) how many expected, (3) support quantum. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 'skill development professionals' but does NOT state: (1) how many exist in TN, (2) what support (certification, equipment, pay raise, institutional backing?), (3) cost. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

Without baselines or cost anchors, no fiscal feasibility assessment is possible.
sustainability_reasoning: Even if implemented, 'comprehensive support' without structural reform (land, income floor, skill ecosystem) typically provides temporary relief. The promise's vagueness on mechanism, scale, and quantum makes it difficult to assess whether it addresses root livelihood barriers or merely patches income shortfalls. Implementation risk is very high due to lack of clarity.

promise_components.analysis: Promise commits to establishing a SHG syndicate but does NOT state: (1) structure (cooperative, trust, company?), (2) governance model, (3) product categories, (4) initial capital investment, (5) operational budget, (6) expected member coverage. Reference data: ~4.7 lakh SHGs, ~63 lakh women members in TN. If syndicate reaches 50% of SHGs (2.35 lakh) and 50% of women members (31.5 lakh), still no cost per beneficiary or setup cost provided. promise_components.component: Provide market linkages for SHG products
promise_components.analysis: Promise commits to market linkages but does NOT specify: (1) mechanism (e-commerce platform, direct buyer contracts, government procurement?), (2) product quality/certification standards, (3) logistics/cold chain investment, (4) commission/margin structure, (5) scale of reach. No baseline on current SHG market access exists in reference data. Data unavailable — cannot calculate implementation cost or reach.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate. Promise lacks: (1) syndicate setup cost, (2) operational budget, (3) technology platform investment, (4) expected beneficiary reach, (5) market linkage mechanism cost. If syndicate reaches 2.35 lakh SHGs with ₹10 lakh setup per SHG + ₹5 lakh annual operating cost = ₹23,500 cr setup (one-time, infeasible) + ₹11,750 cr/year operating, but scale and structure are invented. Manifesto provides no cost anchor.
sustainability_reasoning: SHG syndicates with genuine market linkages are structurally sound: they build producer aggregation, reduce middleman rents, and create income stability if products find sustained demand. However, without specified market mechanism, quality standards, or buyer commitments, success depends heavily on execution. Market linkages are only sustainable if based on genuine buyer relationships, not artificial quotas.

promise_components.analysis: Promise commits to app but does NOT state: (1) how many women auto/taxi drivers exist in TN, (2) app development and hosting cost, (3) commission reduction target (% cut), (4) expected income gain per driver, (5) rollout timeline, (6) integration with operator platforms. Income gain depends on commission reduction negotiated with operators — no commitment stated. If 50,000 women drivers exist and average earn ₹3,000/month, 10% income gain = ₹15 cr/year net-positive for drivers (not a cost to state). App cost (~₹0.5–1 cr/year operating) is modest vs. TN discretionary budget (~₹184,162 cr).
sustainability_reasoning: Transparent commission structures address a real market failure (information asymmetry between drivers and operators) and can sustainably raise driver incomes without requiring permanent subsidy. However, success depends on: (1) operator cooperation (not mandated in promise), (2) driver adoption, (3) app reliability. Without legal backing to enforce transparent rates, operators may resist or circumvent the app.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate. Promise lacks: (1) beneficiary count (women transport workers in TN), (2) union setup and registration cost, (3) annual operational budget, (4) wage/compensation increase target, (5) enforcement mechanism. If union reaches 50,000 women members with ₹5 lakh setup + ₹100 lakh/year operations = ₹1–2 cr/year (speculation). State cost depends on whether fair wages are legislatively mandated or funded vs. negotiated with operators.
sustainability_reasoning: Cooperative unions that successfully achieve collective bargaining can structurally improve worker incomes, safety, and conditions by shifting power from individual operators to organised labour. However, implementation risk is high because: (1) TN transport is fragmented (state buses, autos, private taxis, informal operators), (2) union effectiveness depends on membership adoption and bargaining leverage, (3) operator resistance is likely without legal mandate, (4) no enforcement mechanism is specified.

promise_text_en: Establish women self-help groups across 26 districts in Tamil Nadu to support 3,000 women from disadvantaged backgrounds
promise_text_ta: îIöèˆF¡ 26 ñ£õ†ìƒèO™ ãø‚°¬øò 3,000 ÞìƒèO™ èŸ²óƒè‹ (°õ£K) ªî£N™ ïì‰¶ õ¼Aø¶
impact_mechanism: Direct enrollment of 3,000 disadvantaged women into self-help groups creates immediate access to collective savings, credit, and peer support networks.

promise_components.component: Establish women self-help groups across 26 districts targeting 3,000 women from disadvantaged backgrounds
promise_components.analysis: TN already has ~4.7 lakh SHGs with ~63 lakh women members (reference data). This promise targets 3,000 new women — an addition of 0.48% to existing membership. Geographic scope: all 26 TN districts already have SHG presence. Cost estimate unavailable — no per-woman enrollment/support cost stated in promise. Annual operational cost (assuming ₹5,000–₹10,000/SHG/year for 600 groups of 5 women): ₹30–60 crore, but this is extrapolated, not grounded in promise text.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹30–60 crore/year for operational support (calculated assuming ₹5,000–₹10,000/SHG/year; specific budget allocation not provided in promise). Approximately 0.016–0.033% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). FISCALLY FEASIBLE. However, cost basis is inferred, not stated.
sustainability_reasoning: SHGs provide genuine peer support and access to credit, addressing cash-flow stress for poor women. However, 3,000 women is negligible relative to ~1.2 crore BPL households (0.025% coverage). The promise does not address root causes of disadvantage: land ownership, skill gaps, discriminatory labor markets, or income floors. It manages hardship symptomatically without structural reform.

promise_text_en: Provide special training programs, skill development, and income-generating opportunities for women members of self-help groups
promise_text_ta: Þõ˜èÀ‚° º¬øò£ù G¬ôò£ù õ¼õ£Œ ñŸÁ‹ êÍèŠ ð£¶è£Š¹ õêFèœ A¬ìŠð¬î
impact_mechanism: Skill training and income-generating programs raise earning potential of SHG members by creating access to higher-wage activities or self-employment.

promise_components.component: Provide special training programs, skill development, and income-generating opportunities for women members of self-help groups
promise_components.analysis: Promise is aspirational but lacks specificity: no mention of (a) number of women to be trained, (b) type/duration of training, (c) expected income uplift, (d) budget allocation, (e) timeline. Existing SHG membership is ~63 lakh women (reference data) — unclear whether this promise applies to all or a subset. Cost: data unavailable. Mechanism: if women gain skills leading to self-employment or better-paid wage work, income could rise; but without specifics, fiscal and implementation viability cannot be assessed.

fiscal_cost_note: Cost unavailable — promise provides no budget, beneficiary count, or training scope. If interpreted as training for ~63 lakh existing SHG women at ₹5,000–₹15,000 per person (industry standard for short-term skill courses), annual cost could range ₹315–945 crore in year 1, with ongoing maintenance at ₹50–150 crore/year. Without commitment to scale, this is speculative.
sustainability_reasoning: Skill development addresses a structural root cause — labor market exclusion due to lack of marketable skills. If women gain skills matched to real market demand and receive job placement/market linkages, income and economic independence can improve durably. However, promise is too vague to assess actual delivery risk or whether skills will translate to sustainable employment. Success depends entirely on quality of training design, labor market alignment, and post-training support.

promise_text_en: Establish rural women empowerment centers to provide skill training, market linkages and support for sustainable livelihoods
