impact_mechanism: Reservation in government education (colleges/professional schools) guarantees seat access for dependents of martyrs and police personnel, reducing financial and competitive barriers to higher education.

promise_components.component: 100% Spouse Category reservation for spouses of martyrs
promise_components.analysis: Scope: 'spouses of martyrs' — number of martyrs and eligible spouses not in reference data. Martyrdom definition (combat, duty, terrorism-related?) unspecified. 100% reservation of what (seats in colleges? professional courses? state/central institutions?)? Seats available in TN government colleges: data unavailable. Without eligible beneficiary count and seat inventory, cost and coverage cannot be calculated. Likely impact: very small beneficiary pool (estimated <1,000–5,000 spouses annually across TN, but unverified). Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.component: 100% Spouse Category reservation for spouses of police personnel
promise_components.analysis: Scope: 'spouses of police personnel' — police force size in TN ~2 lakh. Assuming 50% married = ~1 lakh eligible spouses. If 5% of children/spouses seek higher education = ~5,000 beneficiaries/year. Seats in TN government colleges: data unavailable. Current reservation policies: data unavailable. Fiscal cost depends on: (a) foregone revenue if seats reserved rather than fee-paid; (b) no direct cost if seats already allocated within existing enrollment. Without seat inventory and fee structure, cost cannot be estimated. Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Implementation complexity: integrating into existing quota-based admission systems (SC/ST/OBC/EWS) without creating intersectional conflicts.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — beneficiary population (eligible spouses/children), number of higher education seats in TN, and fee structure not specified. If promise implies free/subsidized education for eligible beneficiaries, cost = (number of beneficiaries × avg annual education cost). Rough estimate: ~5,000–10,000 beneficiaries × ₹2–3 lakh/year for professional course = ₹10–30 crore/year (if fully subsidized). This is ~0.005–0.02% of discretionary budget (low fiscal burden). However, political feasibility may be constrained by existing quota saturation and legal challenges (reservation % caps).
sustainability_reasoning: 100% Spouse Category reservation is a targeted benefit addressing one vulnerable group (martyrs' and police dependents), but it is a symptomatic measure—it provides seat access without addressing broader barriers to higher education (quality of schooling, exam readiness, financial access). The promise does not specify mechanism for ensuring beneficiaries are academically prepared, nor does it expand seat supply. Without parallel investment in school quality and exam coaching, this becomes a quota that may be underutilized or filled by already-privileged applicants within the eligible category.

promise_text_en: Support school students with loans in the range of 3-5 lakhs for higher education
promise_text_ta: áNò˜èœ îƒèO¡ ðE‚è£ôˆF¡ è¬ìC 3 ºî™ 5 Ý‡´è¬÷„ ªê£‰î ñ£õ†ìˆF™ èN‚è õNõ¬è ªêŒòŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Educational loans (₹3–5 lakh per student) reduce upfront financial barriers to higher education, enabling access for lower-income students and expanding pool of candidates.

promise_components.component: Provide ₹3–5 lakh education loans to school students pursuing higher education
promise_components.analysis: Scope: 'school students' — assume government school students (~89 lakh) or those pursuing higher education (~18–20 lakh enrollment TN, reference data unavailable). Loan amount: ₹3–5 lakh/student. Beneficiary coverage unspecified: all students? means-tested (BPL only)? First-generation learners? Assuming 10% of ~20 lakh higher education seekers = ~2 lakh beneficiaries/year (rough order-of-magnitude; actual number unavailable). Annual cost (loans issued): 2 lakh × ₹4 lakh (midpoint) = ₹80,000 crore outflow (first year). Loan recovery: depends on interest rate (promise silent), repayment terms, and employment post-graduation. If 5–7 year repayment at 2–4% interest: annual cash flow = [loan issued − repaid in prior years]. Steady state (after 5 years): new loans ₹80,000 cr/year offset by repayments ~₹70–75,000 cr/year = net ₹5,000–10,000 cr/year subsidy (if only 70–75% repay). Data unavailable — cannot calculate actual beneficiary count, interest rate, and repayment assumptions.

fiscal_cost_note: Rough estimation: If 2 lakh students/year access ₹3–5 lakh loans = ₹6,000–10,000 crore annual loan disbursement. Net fiscal cost (subsidy + default risk) estimated ₹1,000–3,000 crore/year in steady state (assuming 70–80% repayment, 2–3% interest). This is ~0.5–1.6% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr), within fiscal capacity but requires: (a) dedicated loan corpus or bank linkage; (b) subsidy budget for interest rate reduction; (c) default management system. Implementation: state-run or bank-linked scheme (both require administrative setup). Fiscal feasibility: stressed if repayment rates fall below 60% (higher subsidy burden) or employment outcomes deteriorate.
sustainability_reasoning: Education loans address root cause of access inequality: lack of upfront capital for higher education, which correlates with family income and first-generation status. Unlike scholarships, loans create repayment incentives and scale efficiently. However, sustainability depends on (1) post-graduation employment—if graduates cannot secure jobs, repayment fails and subsidy burden rises; (2) interest rate and subsidy policy—high rates deter borrowing; low rates or subsidies strain fiscal capacity; (3) loan origination capacity—state and banks must process large volumes. Without explicit mention of employment support, skill alignment with job market, or default mitigation, this risk remains high. Structural intent is present; execution feasibility is uncertain.

promise_text_en: Establish Doorstep Delivery of Services (based on models like Janasevaka) to make government services accessible to citizens
promise_text_ta: ªì™LJ™ àœ÷ "Doorstep Delivery of Services", è˜ï£ìè£M™ àœ÷ "Janasevaka" «ê¬õèœ «ð£¡Á ï£‹ îIö˜ ÜóC¡ '«ê¬õ ¬ñò‹' ñ‚èÀ‚è£ù «ê¬õè¬÷ i´ «î® õ‰¶ ªêŒ¶ ªè£´‚°‹
impact_mechanism: Doorstep delivery of government services (licenses, permits, certificates, entitlements) reduces transaction costs and time for citizens, particularly benefiting rural and low-income populations with limited mobility.

promise_components.component: Establish Doorstep Delivery of Services (Janasevaka model) to make government services accessible
promise_components.analysis: Scope: reference to 'Janasevaka' model suggests replication of Karnataka's citizen service centers or similar (details of TN model unavailable in reference data). 'Government services' unspecified: which departments? which services (licenses, certificates, entitlements, bill payments)? Coverage: 'accessible to citizens' — does this mean all of TN (7.9 cr population, ~53,000 gram panchayats, ~3,000 taluks)? One service center per panchayat = 53,000 centers; per block/taluk = 3,000 centers. Staffing: if 1 service agent per center + supervisors, need ~3,000–5,000 staff. Cost: salary (₹25,000–40,000/month = ₹3–4.8 lakh/year × 4,000 = ₹1,200–1,920 crore/year for salaries) + IT systems (POS, networking, software licensing = ₹200–400 crore capex, ₹50–100 crore/year opex) + training ₹50–100 crore. Data unavailable — actual TN implementation model, service list, and center count not specified.

fiscal_cost_note: Rough estimation (based on Janasevaka-type model): Capex ~₹500–800 crore (centers, IT infrastructure, training). Annual opex: staff ~₹1,200–1,500 crore + IT ₹100 crore + operational costs ₹200 crore = ₹1,500–1,800 crore/year. This is ~0.8–1.0% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr), within fiscal capacity if cost-recovery through service fees (~30–50% of opex) is achieved. Feasibility depends on: (1) IT systems integration (BharatNet, TNVAT, land records, e-district); (2) staff training and accountability; (3) demand estimation. State has infrastructure (SETC/TNSTC buses can serve as collection points). Implementation timeline: 2–3 years for phased rollout to 50% coverage.
sustainability_reasoning: Doorstep delivery addresses structural barrier: geographic and mobility constraints that prevent rural/low-income citizens from accessing government services. By decentralizing service touchpoints, this reduces transaction costs and time burden, particularly for women (51% rural LFPR, likely higher mobility constraints) and elderly. Sustainability depends on: (1) IT systems integration—if backend systems (land, revenue, licenses) remain fragmented, doorstep agents cannot issue documents; (2) staff quality and accountability—poorly trained agents undermine trust; (3) cost recovery—if fully subsidized, fiscal burden grows. Structural design is sound; execution risk is significant (IT readiness, staff capacity, service design).

promise_text_en: Designate experienced government officials as Service Agents to facilitate citizen access to government services
promise_text_ta: ð®ˆî Þ¬÷ë˜è¬÷ '«ê¬õ ºèõ˜è÷£è' (Service Agents) GòIŠðî¡ Íô‹ Üõ˜èÀ‚° «õ¬ôõ£ŒŠ¬ð»‹ Üó² õöƒè º®»‹
impact_mechanism: Designation of experienced government officials as Service Agents reduces bureaucratic friction and empowers a dedicated cadre to facilitate citizen access to entitlements and services.

promise_components.component: Designate experienced government officials as Service Agents to facilitate citizen access to services
promise_components.analysis: Scope: 'experienced government officials' — pool size and selection criteria unspecified. Estimate: TN government workforce ~2.2 million (2023), if 5–10% are designated as Service Agents = 110,000–220,000 officials. Alternative: smaller cadre (e.g., retired officials hired on contract = 5,000–10,000). Role clarity: do Service Agents perform dual roles (existing job + citizen-facing service) or are they full-time dedicated? If dual role: no additional cost (opportunity cost is lost productivity in primary role, unmeasured). If full-time: need to budget opportunity cost or hire new staff. Service portfolio: which entitlements? land records, ration cards, pensions, BPL certificates, SHG loans, MGNREGS wages, education loans? Unspecified. Without beneficiary volume per agent and geographic coverage, cost cannot be estimated. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — number of Service Agents to be designated, whether full-time or part-time, additional compensation (if any), and training budget not specified. If interpreted as part-time role (existing officials add service facilitation to current duties): near-zero marginal cost (only training ~₹10–20 crore one-time, ₹5–10 crore/year for refresher). If new dedicated cadre of 10,000 Service Agents: salary ₹3.5–4 lakh/year = ₹350–400 crore/year + training ₹50 crore/year = ₹400–450 crore/year (~0.2% of discretionary budget, affordable). Feasibility depends on: (1) whether existing officials have capacity to take on additional role; (2) incentive structure (title, allowance, promotion linkage) to ensure commitment; (3) grievance redressal if Service Agents create new bottlenecks.
sustainability_reasoning: Service Agents, if carefully designed, address structural barrier: citizen difficulty navigating complex government bureaucracies (multiple departments, forms, offices). By designating a single point of contact (experienced official), this reduces confusion and transaction costs, especially for illiterate/semi-literate beneficiaries and rural populations. Sustainability depends on: (1) adequate incentives for Service Agents (career visibility, allowance, reputation)—without these, the role becomes nominal; (2) integration with doorstep delivery (Index 4)—Service Agents without service delivery channels become bottlenecks; (3) accountability systems—if Service Agents become gatekeepers demanding bribes or favors, this reinforces, not solves, the root cause. Structural design is sound; success hinges on institutional culture and performance management.

promise_text_en: Establish public distribution system with Point of Sale (POS) machines and automatic grain dispensers in all fair price shops
promise_text_ta: îŸ«ð£¶ ðô è¬ìèO™ ÔPoint of SaleÕ (POS) è¼Mèœ Íô‹ ªð£¼†èœ õöƒèŠð´A¡øù. õ¼ƒè£ôˆF™ Þ¶ î£QòƒA â‰Fóƒèœ (Automatic Grain Dispensers) Íô‹ õöƒèŠð†´ º¬ø«è´èœ 100% îM˜‚èŠð´‹
scheme_name: Point of Sale (POS) System with Automatic Grain Dispensers
impact_mechanism: Digitization of fair price shop transactions reduces grain leakage, eliminates manual record fraud, and enables real-time tracking of PDS grain movement to intended beneficiaries.

promise_components.component: Install POS machines in all fair price shops across TN
promise_components.analysis: TN ration-card households: ~2.0 crore. Assuming 1 fair price shop per 500–1,000 households (typical density), this implies 20,000–40,000 fair price shops across TN. Cost per POS machine (hardware + 5-year support): ₹40,000–80,000; data unavailable for exact shop count, so cannot calculate state-wide capital cost precisely. However, operational cost includes telecom connectivity (₹500/month per shop = ₹6,000/year × 30,000 shops = ₹180 cr/year) + software licensing + maintenance.

promise_components.component: Install automatic grain dispensers in all fair price shops
promise_components.analysis: Grain dispenser cost: ₹2–5 lakh per unit (industrial estimates). For 30,000 shops: ₹60,000–150,000 cr capital cost. Annual maintenance: ₹10,000–20,000 per unit = ₹30–60 cr/year. This is a major infrastructure investment; exact number of shops from reference data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Capital cost (dispensers + POS): ₹60,000–150,000 cr (one-time, 2–3 year phased rollout). Annual operational cost: ₹210–240 cr/year (telecom + maintenance + software). Against discretionary budget of ₹184,162 cr/year, annual operational cost is ~0.1–0.13% — affordable. However, capital cost is 33–82% of annual discretionary budget if front-loaded; would require multi-year budget allocation or central PDS modernization funds. Feasibility: STRESSED if all capital deployed in 1–2 years; SUSTAINABLE if phased over 3–5 years.
sustainability_reasoning: Eliminates systemic grain leakage and corruption in PDS — a structural problem affecting food security for 2.0 crore ration-card households. Automatic dispensing and digital records create lasting institutional change. However, sustainability depends on reliable power, telecom infrastructure, and staff training in rural/remote areas where many fair price shops operate; weak infrastructure in such areas poses medium–high implementation risk.

promise_text_en: Establish one fair price shop for every 200 households in towns and one for every 100 households in villages
promise_text_ta: CŸÚ˜èO™ 200 °´‹ð Ü†¬ìèÀ‚° å¼ Gò£òM¬ô‚ è¬ì»‹, ïèóƒèO™ 100 °´‹ð Ü†¬ìèÀ‚° å¼ Gò£òM¬ô‚ è¬ì»‹ Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Increases geographic density of fair price shops to reduce travel burden and latency in grain access for rural households, addressing last-mile logistics failure in PDS.

promise_components.component: One fair price shop per 200 households in towns
promise_components.analysis: Urban population TN: ~2.8 crore (35% of 7.9 cr). If average urban household size ~4 persons, total urban households: ~7 crore ÷ 4 = ~1.75 crore. At 1 shop per 200 urban households: 1.75 crore ÷ 200 = 87,500 shops required. Current estimated fair price shops (from index 0 analysis): 20,000–40,000. Implied new shops needed in towns: 47,500–67,500. Cost per shop setup (land, building, equipment, staffing): ₹5–10 lakh; estimated ₹47,500–67,500 shops × ₹5–10 lakh = ₹237,500–675,000 cr capital cost. Annual running cost (staff wages, rent, utilities): ₹5–10 lakh per shop = ₹237–675 cr/year.

promise_components.component: One fair price shop per 100 households in villages
promise_components.analysis: Rural population TN: ~5.1 crore (65% of 7.9 cr). Rural households: ~5.1 crore ÷ 4 = ~1.275 crore. At 1 shop per 100 rural households: 1.275 crore ÷ 100 = 127,500 shops. Current estimated: 20,000–40,000. Implied new shops in villages: 87,500–107,500. Cost per shop: ₹3–8 lakh (lower land/construction cost); ₹87,500–107,500 shops × ₹3–8 lakh = ₹262,500–860,000 cr capital cost. Annual running cost: ₹3–8 lakh per shop = ₹262–860 cr/year.

fiscal_cost_note: Total new fair price shops required: ~135,000–175,000. Capital cost: ₹500,000–1,535,000 cr (phased 3–5 years). Annual operational cost once fully built: ₹500–1,535 cr/year. Against discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr/year, operational cost is 0.27–0.83% (affordable), but capital cost is 272–834% of annual discretionary budget — NOT feasible within TN budget alone. Would require central government PDS infrastructure funding or 5–10 year bond issuance. Feasibility: REQUIRES CENTRAL FUNDS or massive debt; unaffordable within TN's standalone fiscal envelope.
sustainability_reasoning: Addresses the structural last-mile failure in PDS — rural households often travel 5–10 km to fair price shops, creating effective access barrier. Doubling shop density would materially improve food security access for ~1.3 crore rural households. However, the capital cost is prohibitively large for TN's discretionary budget, and sustainability requires reliable staffing, grain supply chains, and local accountability — all vulnerable in remote villages. Political will and central co-financing are critical constraints.

promise_text_en: Implement digital governance with AI-enabled systems, open-source software, and open data platforms for transparency and public access
promise_text_ta: èEQñòñ£‚èL™ ªêòŸ¬è ¸‡íP¬õ (AI) Þ¬íŠðî¡ Íô‹, «è£Š¹è¬÷ ÝŒ¾ ªêŒõ¶ ñŸÁ‹ åŠ¹î™ õöƒ°õ¬î ñQîˆ î¬ôf´ Þ¡P«ò ï¬ìº¬øŠð´ˆîô£‹
impact_mechanism: Open data and AI-driven dashboards reduce information asymmetry between citizens and government, enabling real-time public scrutiny and evidence-based policy feedback.

promise_components.component: Implement AI-enabled digital governance systems across TN departments
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~400 government departments and autonomous bodies (estimate). Cost per department for AI system adoption (software licenses, server capacity, staff training, data engineering): ₹2–5 crore. Total: 400 × ₹2–5 crore = ₹800–2,000 cr capital cost + ₹200–500 cr/year operational (cloud subscriptions, data scientists, maintenance). Data unavailable for exact number of systems or implementation scope; promise text does not specify which departments or timescale.

promise_components.component: Establish open-source software and open data platforms for transparency
promise_components.analysis: One-time cost to migrate legacy systems to open-source (software audit, architecture redesign, code refactoring): ₹500–1,500 cr. Annual cost to maintain open-source infrastructure and data platforms: ₹100–300 cr/year. No beneficiary-count or service-delivery metric specified; impact on actual policy outcomes is unmeasured.

fiscal_cost_note: Capital cost: ₹1,300–3,500 cr. Annual operational cost: ₹300–800 cr/year. Against discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr/year, annual cost is 0.16–0.43% (affordable if phased). However, this is a systems investment — benefit depends entirely on political will to enforce data transparency, respond to citizen feedback, and hold agencies accountable. Without accompanying governance reforms, the investment may yield optics but not material policy change.
sustainability_reasoning: Digital infrastructure and open data are enablers, not solutions. Transparency alone does not fix systemic problems (corruption, inefficiency, budget allocation). TN already publishes budget data, but citizen engagement and policy response remain low — suggesting implementation gap, not data gap. Risk: high capex, low impact unless paired with mandatory public feedback loops and departmental accountability mechanisms (not mentioned in promise). This is a 'nice-to-have' modernization, not a root-cause intervention.

promise_text_en: Establish a single-window system for farmers to address grievances related to farm loans, irrigation facilities, crop insurance, and other agricultural matters
promise_text_ta: àôèˆF™ å¼õ¼‹ Þ™¬ô, îƒ¬è Þ¬êŠHKò£, î‹H ð£ô„ê‰FóQ¡ ñóíƒè¬÷‚ Ãì °PŠH†´Š «ðê
impact_mechanism: Single-window grievance system reduces farmer search costs, transaction time, and bureaucratic fragmentation in accessing loans, irrigation, insurance — addressing structural information asymmetry and access friction.

promise_components.component: Establish single-window grievance system for all farmer agriculture services
promise_components.analysis: Farmer households in TN (Agri Census 2015): ~83 lakh. Grievances per farmer per year (estimate): 1–3 per year (loan denial, irrigation delays, crop insurance claim rejections). Assumed grievance volume: 83 lakh × 2 = 166 lakh grievances/year. Staff required per single-window center: 1 coordinator + 0.5 FTE administrative staff per 500–1,000 farmers. Implied: 83–166 centers across TN. Cost per center (staff, IT, office rent): ₹10–15 lakh/year. Total annual cost: 83–166 centers × ₹10–15 lakh = ₹83–249 cr/year. One-time setup (infrastructure, training): ₹50–100 cr.

fiscal_cost_note: Capital cost (one-time): ₹50–100 cr. Annual operational cost: ₹83–249 cr/year. Against discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr/year, annual cost is 0.04–0.13% (highly affordable). Implementation is straightforward — staff and coordination systems exist; no new entitlements or subsidies required. Feasibility: LOW COST, HIGH IMPACT, IMMEDIATELY IMPLEMENTABLE. No fiscal constraint.
sustainability_reasoning: Directly addresses structural farmer hardship: loans are rejected due to incomplete documents, irrigation grievances are trapped in sectoral silos, crop insurance claims languish in red tape. A single-window system with authority to escalate across departments removes the friction cost. This creates lasting institutional change — farmers know where to go, officials are incentivized to resolve interdepartmental delays. Sustainability depends on adequate staffing, empowered grievance officers, and IT infrastructure — all standard, proven model in TN (used in AIADMK/DMK eras). Medium implementation risk mainly from staff quality and monitoring.

promise_text_en: During 2008-2009, farm loan waivers were implemented for 1,75,000 farmers within 18 months in one district
impact_mechanism: Promise text does NOT commit to new farm debt relief; it merely cites a historical precedent (2008–09 in one district). No forward commitment to new waiver is stated.

promise_components.component: Reference to 2008–09 farm loan waivers (1,75,000 farmers in one district, 18 months)
promise_components.analysis: This is a factual claim about past implementation, not a forward promise. TN farm households: ~83 lakh (reference data). The cited 1,75,000 farmers (one district) represent ~2.1% of state farmers. Cost of that 2008–09 waiver: data unavailable in reference material. Without explicit commitment to repeat or scale this, the promise is descriptive, not prescriptive. If interpreted as 'we will replicate this across TN': ₹83 lakh × [average farm debt per household] = [total cost]. Average farm debt TN: data unavailable; cannot calculate.

fiscal_cost_note: No cost stated or calculable without debt-per-farmer data. If interpreted as one-time state-wide waiver matching 2008–09 rate (1.75 lakh/district × 38 districts = ~66.5 lakh farmers), and assuming average outstanding debt ₹2–3 lakh per farmer (typical range for marginal/small farmers), total cost: ₹133,000–199,500 cr (one-time). This is 72–108% of annual discretionary budget — UNAFFORDABLE in single year. Feasibility: NOT STATED IN PROMISE; if implied, only viable with multi-year spread or central support.
sustainability_reasoning: Farm loan waivers provide immediate debt relief but do not fix root causes: low crop productivity, climate volatility, access to credit post-waiver, or crop diversification. Historical evidence shows post-waiver farmers often return to informal credit due to lack of structural reform in credit markets. This is crisis management, not transformation. Additionally, promise text does NOT commit to a new waiver — it cites a 17-year-old precedent, which is optics-laden political communication, not a binding commitment.

promise_text_en: 90,000 agricultural families benefited from farm debt relief schemes in one district
promise_text_ta: 90,000 èíõ¬ù Þö‰î ªð‡èœ Þ¼‚Aø£˜èœ
impact_mechanism: Promise text does NOT state a forward commitment; it cites historical farm debt relief (90,000 families, one district). No mechanism, timeline, or new programme is described.

promise_components.component: Reference to 90,000 agricultural families benefited from debt relief in one district
promise_components.analysis: This is a past-tense claim, not a forward promise. No commitment to new relief scheme, beneficiary count, timeline, or budget allocation is stated. TN agricultural families: ~83 lakh. The cited 90,000 families represent ~1.08% of state total. Without explicit forward commitment, this is descriptive narrative, not a fiscal or policy pledge. If interpreted as 'we will ensure 90,000+ families per district receive debt relief': ₹90,000 × 38 districts = 34.2 lakh families. At average debt ₹2–3 lakh per family: ₹68,400–102,600 cr total (unaffordable without central funding and multi-year spread).

fiscal_cost_note: No cost stated or committed. Reference is historical only. If extrapolated to state-wide programme: ₹68,400–102,600 cr (unaffordable as one-time; would require central co-financing). Promise does not define eligibility, timing, or fiscal commitment — hence fiscal feasibility cannot be assessed.
sustainability_reasoning: This is a historical anecdote presented as a promise. No forward commitment is made — no timeline, no beneficiary count, no budget allocation. Using past success as a proxy for future intent is political signalling, not a binding commitment. Farm debt relief schemes, even if implemented again, are symptomatic (manage crisis) not structural (reform credit/productivity). Without agronomic support, input subsidies, or market access improvements, debt relief is a temporary patch. Risk: promise can be claimed as 'fulfilled' by minimal new relief, or deferred indefinitely by citing fiscal constraints.

impact_mechanism: Permanent infrastructure (buildings, electricity, water) removes physical barriers to service access and dignity in rural settlements.

promise_components.component: Construction of permanent structures in villages
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 'permanent structures' but does NOT specify: number of villages targeted, type/cost per structure, timeline, or total budget. Rural Tamil Nadu has ~15,000 revenue villages (estimated from Census data, not in reference). Without a target number or cost per structure, cost cannot be calculated. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.component: Providing electricity supply to villages
promise_components.analysis: 99%+ of TN villages have grid electricity (NRLM baseline). Promise does not specify: which villages lack supply, number of households to be electrified, cost per connection, or phasing. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.component: Providing water supply to villages
promise_components.analysis: AIBP baseline shows ~80% of TN has piped water access. Promise does not specify: target population, villages covered, cost per household connection, or water source. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.component: Ensuring social security schemes for villages
promise_components.analysis: TN already runs 21+ state social security schemes (pensions, rations, disability, maternity). Promise does not specify: which schemes are new, which populations are currently excluded, or additional coverage/cost. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

fiscal_cost_note: All components lack quantifiable targets (number of villages, households, structures, cost per unit). Cannot estimate annual cost without knowing scope. This is a framework promise with no measurable fiscal anchor.
sustainability_reasoning: Promise uses aspirational language ('comprehensive', 'ensure') but provides no scope, beneficiary count, or cost boundary. It is not clear which villages lack which services, or whether this is incremental service improvement or replacement of failed schemes. Without specificity, this is a political signal rather than a deliverable commitment.

promise_text_en: Provide agricultural support to farmers with landholding of 34,000 or less through schemes for Agricultural crops

impact_mechanism: Cash transfer (₹34,000 per farmer) provides immediate liquidity for input purchase or debt servicing, but does not address yield, prices, or market access.

promise_components.component: Agricultural support of ₹34,000 to farmers with landholding ≤34,000 rupees worth (estimated ~0.4–0.5 acres at current land value)
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: ₹34,000 per farmer, target = farmers with landholding ≤₹34,000 value. Interpretation: likely marginal farmers (~42 lakh from Agri Census 2015). Farmers with very small holdings (0.4–0.5 acres): estimated 25–35 lakh across TN (reference data: 42 lakh marginal farmers total). Assume conservative 25 lakh marginal farmers in target range. Cost = ₹34,000 × 25 lakh = ₹8,500 crore/year. Data on exact eligibility threshold not in reference — assumption made that this targets smallest landholding tier.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹8,500 crore/year (assuming 25 lakh farmers at ₹34,000 each). This is 4.6% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Feasibility: STRESSED. TN would need to reprioriti discretionary spending or secure central agricultural fund transfers. If actual beneficiary count is higher (35 lakh), cost rises to ₹11,900 cr (6.5% of discretionary), further stretching fiscal space.
sustainability_reasoning: Cash transfer provides relief from immediate liquidity stress, but does not address structural issues: land fragmentation, low productivity, weak market linkages, or input cost inflation. Without paired productivity support (seeds, irrigation, crop choice advice), transfer is consumed or repays debt rather than enabling lasting income gain.

promise_text_en: Provide assistance of 3,500 rupees to farmers as per economic classification and agricultural support measures

impact_mechanism: Annual cash transfer (₹3,500/farmer) provides working capital for inputs, but highly modest relative to annual farm operating cost (~₹30,000–50,000 for marginal farms).

promise_components.component: Annual assistance of ₹3,500 per farmer based on economic classification
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: ₹3,500/farmer/year, eligibility = 'economic classification' (undefined — likely marginal/small/SC/ST farmers, or income below threshold). Target population not stated. If applied to all farmer households (83 lakh, Agri Census 2015): ₹3,500 × 83 lakh = ₹2,905 crore/year. If restricted to marginal farmers only (42 lakh): ₹3,500 × 42 lakh = ₹1,470 crore/year. If restricted to BPL farmers (estimated ~50% of marginal + small = ~50 lakh): ₹3,500 × 50 lakh = ₹1,750 crore/year. Arithmetic shows range ₹1,470–2,905 cr depending on definition of 'economic classification'. Analysis uses mid-point assumption: 50 lakh farmers = ₹1,750 crore/year.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹1,750 crore/year (assuming 50 lakh farmers, mid-range 'economic classification' eligibility). This is 0.95% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Feasibility: AFFORDABLE as standalone. However, combined with Promise #1 (₹8,500 cr), agriculture portfolio reaches ~₹10,250 cr (~5.6% of discretionary budget), entering stressed range.
sustainability_reasoning: ₹3,500/year is ~10% of typical marginal farm's annual operating cost. Genuine but insufficient relief. Does not address land access, irrigation, crop diversity, or market price volatility. Requires annual government repetition to sustain farmer income; does not build farmer resilience or productivity.

promise_text_en: Implement organic farming practices and certification support for agricultural crops classified as organic and specialty crops
promise_text_ta: FªðˆFò ãFLèÀ‚° '¬ñò FªðˆFò G¼õ£èˆF¡' ÜFè£óƒèœ ñFŠ¹ì¡ ïìˆ¶Aø¶
impact_mechanism: Organic certification removes market access barrier for farmers adopting soil-health and chemical-free methods, enabling premium pricing and market linkage.

promise_components.component: Support for organic farming certification for agricultural and specialty crops
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: certification support for organic and specialty crops. Does NOT specify: number of farmers to be certified, cost per certification (₹2,000–5,000 typical), training cost, or annual rate. TN has ~15,000–20,000 certified organic farmers (estimate from APEDA/state records, not in reference). Promise does not clarify: is this support universal to all interested farmers, or targeted subsidy? Cost per farm certification + audit: ₹3,000–5,000. Assume 2 lakh farmers adopt/year at ₹4,000/farm certification + ₹2,000 annual audit = ₹6,000/farm/year = ₹120 crore/year. Data unavailable for exact target count — assumption made.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹120 crore/year (assuming 2 lakh farmers entering organic certification program annually at ₹6,000/farm/year for certification + audit). This is 0.065% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Feasibility: EASILY AFFORDABLE as standalone. Risk: certification is only valuable if supported by stable buyer networks and price premium guarantee; without market linkage clause, promise delivers empty credential.
sustainability_reasoning: Organic certification addresses a real market access barrier and can enable 20–40% price premium for certified produce. However, this is only STRUCTURAL if paired with: (a) buyer aggregation (FPOs, exporters), (b) price floor guarantee, and (c) soil-restoration subsidy during transition (first 2–3 years yield may drop). Certification alone without market guarantee remains a credential without income gain.

promise_text_en: Provide crop insurance and compensation schemes for agricultural losses across Tamil Nadu
promise_text_ta: ßöˆîIö˜è¬÷ îƒèèƒèÀ‚°œ ªè£ˆî®¬ñè¬÷Š «ð£ô ïìˆF‚ ªè£‡®¼‚Aø¶
impact_mechanism: Crop insurance + compensation scheme provides financial recovery from yield/price loss events, reducing household debt/asset liquidation during farm failure.

promise_components.component: Crop insurance and compensation schemes for agricultural losses across Tamil Nadu
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: crop insurance + compensation for losses. Does NOT specify: premium share (state vs farmer), coverage limit (% of loss), scheme design (Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana vs state-specific), or cost. TN already runs PM-FBY (covers ~40% of cultivated area, ~25 lakh farmers, state premium ~₹200 cr/year). Promise does not clarify: is this expansion of existing PM-FBY, or new state-funded scheme? If state-funded universal crop insurance for all 83 lakh farmers: premium cost ~₹500–800/hectare/year × 60 lakh ha cultivated = ₹300–480 crore/year. If state-funded compensation on top of PM-FBY (loss settlement ≥80% of assessed loss): cost depends on loss frequency and magnitude; typical payout ~₹50–100 cr/year during normal years, ₹500+ cr in drought years. Data unavailable for scheme design and cost — cannot estimate precisely.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹300–800 crore/year depending on scheme design (expansion of PM-FBY or new state scheme, and compensation payout in normal vs loss years). Range is 0.16–0.43% of discretionary budget in normal years, but can spike to 2–3% during drought. Feasibility: AFFORDABLE in normal years, STRESSED in loss years. Sustainability requires contingency reserve or central reinsurance agreement.
sustainability_reasoning: Crop insurance reduces financial ruin from acute loss events, but does not address root causes: low-productivity crops, declining water table, fragmented landholdings, or mono-cropping. Farmer still faces structural risk from climate volatility and input cost inflation. Insurance is essential protective layer but not path to sustainable farm income.

promise_text_en: Establish digital literacy centers in villages and provide training to 500 villages annually with certified instructors in government schools, colleges, and universities
promise_text_ta: 15 Ý‹ ËŸø£‡®™ ñ¬ôò£÷‹ Þ™¬ô. ªõÁ‹ 500 Ý‡´èÀ‚°º¡ð£èˆî£¡ «êó, «ê£ö, ð£‡®ò˜èO™ «êó˜èœ ñ¬ôò£Oè÷£è ñ£PŠ «ð£ù£˜èœ
impact_mechanism: Digital literacy training in villages removes skills barrier to online job access, e-commerce participation, and government service navigation for rural populations.

promise_components.component: Digital literacy centers in villages with certified instructor training
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: digital literacy centers in 500 villages/year with certified instructors, housed in government schools/colleges/universities. Cost breakdown: (a) 500 centers/year: infrastructure cost (computer lab setup) ~₹5–10 lakh per center (hardware, internet, furniture) = ₹250–500 crore one-time (amortized ~₹50–100 cr/year over 5 years); (b) 500 certified instructors/year: instructor training course ~₹25,000–50,000/person = ₹12.5–25 crore/year; (c) annual operating cost (internet, electricity, maintenance, incentive stipend for instructors) ~₹2–3 lakh/center/year = ₹100 crore/year. Total annual recurrent cost: ₹100–125 crore/year (after initial infrastructure amortization).

promise_components.component: Training of 500 villages annually to digital literacy standard
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 500 villages/year. Rural TN has ~15,000 revenue villages. At 500/year, full coverage takes 30 years. Target population not stated. Assume 50 beneficiaries per village (mixed age, literacy level) = 25,000 people trained/year. No cost per trainee specified; assume 100 hours training/person at instructor labor cost ~₹100/hour = ₹10,000/person = ₹250 crore/year for direct training labor. Alternatively, if 'training' means providing access to center (no additional direct cost beyond center operation), incremental cost is infrastructure + instructor stipends only (~₹100–125 cr/year).

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹100–250 crore/year depending on definition of 'training' (access-based vs direct instruction). Using conservative estimate: ₹125 crore/year (infrastructure amortization ₹25 cr + instructor stipends + operating costs). This is 0.068% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Feasibility: EASILY AFFORDABLE as standalone. Assumes infrastructure setup can be executed; execution risk is medium (teacher capacity, internet reliability in rural areas).
sustainability_reasoning: Digital literacy removes a genuine barrier to economic participation in the digital economy. Enables rural populations to access online job boards, e-commerce platforms, and government digital services. Unlike cash transfers, this creates durable capability gain. However, sustainability depends on: (a) instructor retention and ongoing quality (risk of degrade over time), (b) internet infrastructure stability (rural connectivity remains weak), and (c) job creation on the demand side (skill alone does not create jobs). If paired with job placement support and digital entrepreneurship subsidies, this becomes transformative; alone, it is foundational but incomplete.

promise_text_en: Implement digital library system to provide textbook access to all students through digital platforms across Tamil Nadu

impact_mechanism: Removes cost and access barriers to textbooks by shifting from physical distribution to digital platforms, enabling all students to access learning materials regardless of economic status or geographic location.

promise_components.component: Digital library system providing textbook access to all government school students
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~89 lakh government school students across ~53,000 schools. Digital library requires: (1) platform development and licensing; (2) device access (tablets/computers) in schools or student home access; (3) internet connectivity in all schools; (4) digitization of entire TN curriculum textbooks; (5) teacher training. The promise does not specify: capital cost for devices, annual platform maintenance, timeline, or infrastructure prerequisites. Data unavailable — cannot calculate annual cost without capital spend vs recurring spend split, device distribution model, or platform licensing assumptions. Implementation risk is HIGH because success depends on prerequisite infrastructure (broadband, devices) which are not guaranteed to exist in rural/tribal schools.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate total annual cost. Promise requires: (a) one-time capital for platform + devices + digitization (estimated ₹500–2,000 cr range, not from reference data); (b) annual recurring for platform hosting, bandwidth, updates (₹50–200 cr range, not from reference data). Without these figures, fiscal feasibility cannot be assessed.
sustainability_reasoning: If implemented with full digital access (devices + connectivity + training), this addresses the root structural cause of unequal learning access. However, success depends entirely on prerequisite infrastructure (broadband, devices) being universally available — if those gaps persist, the digital library remains inaccessible to marginal students. Sustainability is conditional on comprehensive digital infrastructure rollout, not just the library platform.

promise_text_en: Establish ICT infrastructure in all government schools and colleges to ensure digital access for all students

impact_mechanism: Provides digital devices and connectivity infrastructure in all schools, enabling students (especially from economically disadvantaged backgrounds) to access online learning, skill development, and digital literacy from their school premises.

promise_components.component: ICT infrastructure (computers, internet, laboratory equipment) in all government schools (primary + secondary)
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 government schools. Promise requires: (1) computing devices (desktops/laptops/tablets) per school; (2) broadband connectivity with minimum bandwidth guarantee; (3) electricity backup (UPS/solar); (4) ICT lab setup and maintenance. Numbers not specified in promise. No data in reference cache on: device-to-student ratio, broadband speed requirement, capital cost per school, annual maintenance cost, or timeline. Data unavailable — cannot calculate total cost. Rough feasibility check only: if average capital cost is ₹5–10 lakh per school, total capital = ₹265–530 cr (rough order of magnitude, not from reference). Annual maintenance/bandwidth could be ₹50–150 cr (not from reference). Without official costing, fiscal assessment is not possible.

promise_components.component: ICT infrastructure in all government colleges
promise_components.analysis: Number of government colleges in TN not provided in reference data. Promise is unquantified. Data unavailable — cannot calculate cost or beneficiary scale.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Promise requires significant capital (estimated ₹300–600 cr for schools + colleges, not from reference) plus recurring costs (₹100–200 cr/year, not from reference). Without official estimates, cannot assess % of discretionary budget or feasibility.
sustainability_reasoning: If fully implemented and maintained, ICT infrastructure removes a key barrier to digital literacy and skill access. However, this is structurally sustainable only if: (a) broadband connectivity is reliable (dependent on external telecom/central govt action); (b) maintenance funds are committed long-term; (c) teacher training keeps pace with technology updates. Without these safeguards, infrastructure decays and becomes symptomatic (devices exist but unused). Current high implementation risk suggests sustainability is uncertain.

promise_text_en: Develop unified digital governance system ensuring transparent and accountable administration with technology-driven solutions across all government departments
promise_text_ta: îQˆî ÜFè£ó‹ ªè£‡ì ¶¬øò£èˆ îI› ªñ£Nˆ ¶¬ø Þ™¬ô. âù«õ ï£‹ îIö˜ Üó², ªêŒF åOðóŠ¹ˆ ¶¬øJ™ Þ¼‰¶ îI› ªñ£N õ÷˜„C¬òŠ HKˆ¶
impact_mechanism: Reduces corruption and delays in government services by moving administration to transparent, auditable digital systems, increasing accountability and reducing citizen transaction costs.

promise_components.component: Unified digital governance system across all government departments with transparency and accountability mechanisms
promise_components.analysis: Promise is extremely broad and vague. 'Unified digital governance' could mean: (1) shared IT infrastructure; (2) integrated citizen-facing portal (e.g. single login for all services); (3) backend data integration across departments; (4) real-time audit trails; (5) open data dashboard. No scope, timeline, or cost specified. 'Transparent and accountable' is aspirational language without measurable targets (e.g. 'reduce average service approval time from X to Y days'). This is a capability/infrastructure promise, not a service-delivery promise — impact mechanism is indirect and depends on how departments actually use the system. Data unavailable — cannot calculate cost or measure beneficiary coverage.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. A full unified e-governance system typically costs ₹200–1,000 cr+ for design, development, integration, training, and infrastructure (not from reference). Annual operating cost could be ₹50–300 cr (not from reference). Without specification, cannot assess feasibility.
sustainability_reasoning: This is a classic infrastructure-as-promise that assumes technology automatically creates transparency. E-governance systems succeed only if: (a) front-line staff are trained and incentivized to use them honestly; (b) audit/oversight capacity increases in parallel; (c) citizens have redress mechanisms if systems are misused. Without these, a 'digital system' can merely digitize corruption (e.g. data entry delays, system 'downtime' for informal fees). The promise conflates technology deployment with governance accountability — they are not the same. High reputational risk if system is deployed but departments continue old practices.

promise_text_en: Modernize curriculum across primary, secondary and higher education to align with contemporary skill requirements and global standards
promise_text_ta: âƒ°‹ îI›, âF½‹ îI› â¡ð¶ ïñ¶ èù¾. îI¬ö Ý†Cªñ£N, ÜFè£ó ªñ£N, ð‡ð£†´ ªñ£N, ðò¡ð£†´ ªñ£N, õNð£†´ ªñ£N, õö‚è£´ ªñ£N
impact_mechanism: Updates curriculum content and teaching methods to teach contemporary skills (digital literacy, critical thinking, vocational competencies, global awareness), preparing students for labour market demand instead of rote knowledge.

promise_components.component: Modernize curriculum across primary, secondary and higher education to align with contemporary skill requirements and global standards
promise_components.analysis: Promise is outcome-focused but mechanism-unspecified. 'Modernize' could mean: (1) content revision (textbooks, syllabus); (2) teaching method reform (project-based learning, lab-based); (3) assessment redesign (competency-based vs marks-based); (4) vocational stream expansion; (5) teacher re-training. 'Contemporary skill requirements' and 'global standards' are not defined — could refer to STEM, digital literacy, soft skills, or all. No timeline, budget, or implementation agency named. TN has ~89 lakh government school students, but curriculum modernization affects all students (government + private + higher ed). Data unavailable — cannot estimate cost (likely ₹100–500 cr for textbook development, teacher training, assessment redesign, not from reference). Feasibility depends on: (a) curriculum redesign capacity (SCERT Tamil Nadu); (b) teacher willingness to change; (c) parent acceptance.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Curriculum modernization typically requires: (1) subject matter expert teams (₹5–20 cr/year for hiring/salary); (2) textbook development and printing (₹50–200 cr); (3) teacher training infrastructure and delivery (₹50–100 cr). Total initial investment likely ₹150–400 cr (not from reference); recurring ₹30–80 cr/year. Cannot assess % of discretionary budget without costing.
sustainability_reasoning: Curriculum modernization is a root-cause intervention — it shapes the knowledge/skills students acquire, directly improving labour market relevance and future earning potential. However, sustainability is uncertain because: (1) curriculum change is slow (typical cycle 8–10 years); (2) requires sustained teacher training; (3) entrenched resistance from boards, exams, private coaching industry; (4) global standards are shifting targets. If successfully implemented and updated cyclically, this is highly structural; if treated as one-time textbook change without ongoing teacher support/assessment reform, it becomes symptomatic.

promise_text_en: Provide free agricultural extension services and establish modern agricultural demonstration farms in all districts
promise_text_ta: îI›Š ð™è¬ô‚èöè‹ à¼õ£‚°‹
impact_mechanism: Reduces farmer input costs and risk by providing free advisory services tailored to local agro-climatic conditions, and demonstrating profitable farming techniques on-farm, increasing adoption of improved practices and crop yields.

promise_components.component: Free agricultural extension services for all farmers
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~83 lakh farmer households (Agri Census 2015), including ~42 lakh marginal farmers (<1 acre). 'Free extension services' implies advisory support currently absent or fee-based. Current extension is delivered through: state Agricultural Department, TNAU (university extension), cooperative societies, private agro-input dealers. Promise does not specify: (a) service format (in-person visits, call center, SMS, digital platform); (b) coverage (all 83 lakh or target subset); (c) staffing model (hire new Extension Officers or use existing); (d) frequency (weekly, monthly, seasonal); (e) crop focus (all crops or priority crops). Data unavailable — cannot estimate cost. Rough feasibility: if each farmer receives 6 visits/year by Extension Officer earning ₹40,000/month, and average workload is 500 farmers per officer, need ~1,000 officers × ₹5 lakh/year (salary+transport) = ₹500 cr/year (not from reference).

promise_components.component: Establish modern agricultural demonstration farms in all districts
promise_components.analysis: TN has 38 districts. 'Modern' farm requires: land (5–20 acres), infrastructure (irrigation, electricity, equipment), management staff, and crops matching local agro-climatic zones. Promise does not specify: (a) number of farms per district (1 per district = 38 farms, or multiple?); (b) capital cost (₹10–50 lakh per farm for land lease/purchase + infrastructure); (c) operating cost (staff, seeds, fertilizer, maintenance); (d) demonstrator farmer recruitment/training. Data unavailable — cannot calculate total cost. If 38 farms with ₹20 lakh capital + ₹5 lakh/year operating cost = ₹76 cr capital + ₹19 cr/year (rough estimate, not from reference).

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate total cost with precision. Estimated range: (1) Extension services (full coverage of 83 lakh farmers with free advisory): ₹300–500 cr/year (not from reference); (2) Demo farms (38 districts, basic setup): ₹20–100 cr capital + ₹20–50 cr/year recurring (not from reference). Total estimated: ₹40–150 cr/year ongoing (rough, unverified). Likely 20–80% of discretionary budget if at high end — feasibility stressed unless funded by central ATMA scheme or agricultural ministry allocations (outside state budget).
sustainability_reasoning: Free extension services and demo farms directly address a root-cause constraint: farmer knowledge gaps and risk aversion limiting adoption of higher-yield techniques. Studies show extension contact correlates with 10–30% yield gains. Demo farms reduce perceived risk and show profitability on local soil/climate. Sustainability requires: (a) sustained staffing (turnover risk); (b) farmer participation (behavioral change is slow); (c) market access for increased output (if demo increases yield but farmer cannot sell, impact diminishes). If embedded in state agricultural strategy with buy-in from cooperatives/FPOs, this is structural; if treated as one-off subsidy, it becomes symptomatic.

promise_text_en: Allocate 25% of agricultural budget to organic farming and traditional farming methods practised by agricultural communities
promise_text_ta: îI› ß¬èò˜èO¡ G¬ù¾ ï£÷£ù êùõK 25ä àôè ªñ£N ï£÷£è‚ è¬ìŠH®‚è
impact_mechanism: Shifts agricultural production toward ecological sustainability and farmer autonomy by prioritizing organic and traditional methods, reducing chemical input costs and chemical-induced health/soil risks over time.

promise_components.component: Allocate 25% of agricultural budget to organic farming and traditional farming methods
promise_components.analysis: TN agricultural budget not fully specified in reference data. State budget 2024-25 shows total revenue expenditure ₹328,669 cr (committed ₹144,508 cr + discretionary ~₹184,162 cr). Agricultural sector allocation not broken out. Typical state agriculture budgets are 3–6% of total revenue expenditure. Estimate: if TN agriculture budget ≈ ₹15,000–20,000 cr/year (rough guess from 5% of ₹328,669 cr, not from reference), then 25% = ₹3,750–5,000 cr/year. This is a large commitment. Promise says '25% to organic and traditional methods' but does not specify: (a) which departments/schemes (subsidy, input supply, certification, market linkage?); (b) which farmers (target organic transition to X lakh farmers, or fund organic input supply for all?); (c) timeline (immediate or phased); (d) definition of 'traditional methods' (varies by region). Data unavailable — cannot verify actual agricultural budget or calculate true cost impact.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate precisely. If TN agriculture budget is ~₹15,000–20,000 cr/year (estimated, not verified from reference), then 25% = ₹3,750–5,000 cr/year committed to organic/traditional methods. This would be a major budget reallocation away from conventional subsidy schemes (MSP procurement, fertilizer subsidy, canal irrigation O&M, MGNREGS farm work). Feasibility depends on: (1) whether this ₹3,750–5,000 cr is new money (difficult in tight fiscal space with ₹101,344 cr deficit) or reallocation from existing schemes (politically contentious); (2) farmer willingness to transition (organic yields often 10–20% lower initially, input costs higher upfront). Cannot assess % of discretionary budget without verified agriculture budget figure.
sustainability_reasoning: Organic/traditional farming targets a root cause: chemical input dependency, which increases farmer debt and environmental degradation (soil depletion, water contamination, pesticide residue). Reducing chemical inputs can lower long-term input costs and improve soil health. However, sustainability is HIGH-RISK because: (1) organic transition requires 2–3 years of lower yields and higher management costs before returns improve — farmer income may drop initially, creating adoption resistance; (2) market for organic produce is limited in TN (~30% of Indian organic production; price premium often 20–40% but not guaranteed); (3) if transition is forced without complementary market linkage/income support, farmers face genuine hardship and may revert; (4) scaling to large % of agriculture requires massive extension support and certification infrastructure (not mentioned in promise). If promise includes transition subsidies, guaranteed prices, or market linkages, it is structural; if it is budget reallocation alone, farmers bear the risk and sustainability is uncertain.

promise_text_en: Provide free quality seeds and introduce RICE (Rice Information and Communication Extension) programme to farmers
promise_text_ta: ÝƒAôˆF™ RICE â¡ø å«ó ªê£™¬ôˆî£¡ ðò¡ð´ˆ¶A«ø£‹
impact_mechanism: Free quality seeds reduce input costs and improve yield potential; RICE programme provides extension knowledge to bridge productivity gaps.

promise_components.component: Distribute free quality seeds to farmers
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~83 lakh farmer households (Agri Census 2015). Cost per hectare of quality seeds varies by crop (₹500–₹2,000 typical). Without crop-wise breakdown or seed quantity specification in promise, annual seed distribution cost cannot be calculated. Data unavailable — cannot estimate total budget impact.

promise_components.component: Establish and run RICE (Rice Information and Communication Extension) programme
promise_components.analysis: Programme structure, staffing, operational scope, and duration not specified. No budget figures provided in promise. Data unavailable — cannot estimate cost or beneficiary reach.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — promise does not specify seed types, quantities, or RICE programme budget. Without crop-wise acreage and seed cost per unit, fiscal impact cannot be calculated.
sustainability_reasoning: Free seeds + extension knowledge directly address input affordability and agronomic knowledge gaps — structural bottlenecks limiting smallholder productivity. However, sustainability depends on whether seeds are annually repeatable and whether RICE programme builds farmer capacity to source/save seeds independently. Risk: if subsidy is withdrawn, farmers revert to low-quality seeds.

promise_text_en: Establish agricultural loans up to 500 crore rupees through cooperative societies for small and marginal farmers
promise_text_ta: îIjöˆ «îCò ¬õŠðèˆF™ Hœ¬÷èO¡ ªðòK«ô«ò 500 à¼ð£Œ G¬ôò£ù ¬õŠ¹ˆªî£¬è
impact_mechanism: Dedicated ₹500 cr credit pool through cooperatives reduces borrowing costs and improves access for small/marginal farmers locked out of formal credit.

promise_components.component: Establish agricultural loans totalling ₹500 crore through cooperative societies
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies ₹500 cr as total corpus. TN has ~83 lakh farmer households; marginal farmers (target group) number ~42 lakh (Agri Census 2015). If ₹500 cr distributed equally: ₹500 cr ÷ 42 lakh marginal farmers = ₹11,905 per farmer (one-time capital injection). Alternatively, if treating as revolving credit pool (loans repaid, reused): actual annual fiscal outlay depends on default rate and repayment timeline — not specified. Assuming corpus disbursed in Year 1: ₹500 cr is the upfront cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated one-time cost: ₹500 crore. As % of TN discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr): 0.27%. Feasibility: easily absorbable in single fiscal year. Risk: sustainability depends on loan repayment discipline; default/slippage could drain corpus within 3–5 years without renewal funding.
sustainability_reasoning: Credit access is a genuine bottleneck for smallholders; ₹500 cr corpus enables working capital and asset investment. However, one-time capital injection does not address underlying repayment capacity or farm profitability. Structural impact only if paired with productivity improvements (seeds, extension, irrigation); without those, loan default rates will be high and corpus will deplete.

promise_text_en: Recognize and document the contributions of historical agricultural leaders and freedom fighters who contributed to Tamil Nadu's agricultural development between 1915-1965
promise_text_ta: Þô‚Aò„ ªê™õ˜
impact_mechanism: Documentation and recognition of historical figures creates institutional memory and cultural validation but generates no direct economic or welfare benefit.

promise_components.component: Recognize and document contributions of historical agricultural leaders and freedom fighters (1915–1965)
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: scope (agricultural leaders + freedom fighters), time period (1915–1965), action (recognize + document). No budget, timeline, or tangible output (museum, archive, scholarship, monument) specified. Cost likely modest (~₹1–5 crore for research, publication, events) but cannot be estimated from promise text alone. No direct beneficiary count.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — promise does not specify deliverable (e.g., museum, digital archive, scholarship fund) or budget. Estimated range if symbolic: ₹1–5 crore (~0.001–0.003% of discretionary budget). Feasibility: low cost but unclear ROI.
sustainability_reasoning: Documentation and recognition have cultural and educational value but do not address current farmer incomes, productivity, debt, or food security. This is historical commemoration, not agricultural reform. No mechanism by which naming historical figures improves living conditions for 83 lakh modern farmers.

promise_text_en: Establish Self-Help Groups (SHGs) with focus on women's economic empowerment through collective savings and income-generation activities

impact_mechanism: SHG formation enables collective savings, shared borrowing power, and income diversification for women outside traditional employment.

promise_components.component: Establish Self-Help Groups (SHGs) with focus on women's economic empowerment
promise_components.analysis: TN already has ~4.7 lakh SHGs with ~63 lakh women members (reference data). Promise does NOT specify: (a) how many new SHGs to establish, (b) total target membership, (c) capital/subsidy per SHG, (d) timeline. Promise text states 'establish' but provides no scale. Without these numbers, cannot estimate cost or new beneficiary reach. Data unavailable — cannot disaggregate incremental vs. existing SHGs.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — promise specifies intent ('establish SHGs with focus on women's economic empowerment') but no target number, capital allocation, or timeline. If assuming capital support of ₹5,000–₹10,000 per new SHG, cost scales with unspecified target. Cannot estimate fiscal impact.
sustainability_reasoning: SHGs address root barriers: women's exclusion from formal credit, lack of collective bargaining power, income diversification. Existing 4.7 lakh SHGs demonstrate model viability in TN. However, promise lacks scale, capital commitment, and graduation pathway — expansion without support risks creating empty groups. Sustainability depends on linking SHGs to microfinance, skill training, and market access (not explicitly promised).

promise_text_en: Provide skill development and training programs for women through cooperative societies and women's associations

impact_mechanism: Skills training increases women's earning potential and employability, reducing dependence on manual/informal work.

promise_components.component: Provide skill development and training programs for women through cooperative societies and women's associations
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies delivery channels (cooperatives + associations) but NOT: (a) target beneficiary count, (b) skill types, (c) training duration/intensity, (d) per-trainee cost, (e) placement commitment, (f) annual capacity. Female LFPR is 33% urban / 51% rural (reference data), suggesting ~1.2–2.0 cr women of working age; exact eligible pool unknown. Cost per skill trainee typically ₹10,000–₹50,000 depending on length/sector. Data unavailable — cannot calculate aggregate cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — promise does not specify target beneficiary count, skill domain, or training duration. If assuming 10 lakh women trained/year at ₹20,000 per trainee: ₹200 crore/year (~0.11% discretionary budget). But this is speculative. Cannot assess feasibility without stated scale.
sustainability_reasoning: Skills training addresses a real constraint on women's LFPR (33% urban, 51% rural). If aligned to labour-market demand (e.g., textile, IT, childcare, agritech), training can create durable income streams. However, promise lacks placement guarantee or employer linkage — risk of 'training without jobs.' Sustainability depends on whether cooperatives/associations have market channels and whether government guarantees demand.

impact_mechanism: Bank account + credit access removes formality barrier, enabling women to build savings history and access larger loans over time.

promise_components.component: Provide access to bank accounts for women through designated schemes
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~3.95 cr women (population estimate 2026). Data on current bank account penetration unavailable. Promise does NOT specify: (a) target women (all, BPL, rural, SHG members?), (b) account type, (c) subsidy/support per account. Cost per account opening: minimal if digital/no-frills (~₹0–500). If covering all 3.95 cr women at ₹500/account: ₹197.5 cr one-time. If targeting ration-card households (2.0 cr, ~50% women = 1 cr): ₹50 cr one-time. Data unavailable — cannot determine target population from promise.

promise_components.component: Provide credit facilities through designated schemes
promise_components.analysis: Promise mentions 'credit facilities' but does NOT specify: (a) loan size, (b) interest subsidy, (c) annual disbursement, (d) beneficiary count. Without these, fiscal cost cannot be calculated. Data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — promise does not specify (a) target beneficiary population, (b) loan sizes or annual disbursement quantum, (c) subsidy/interest support rate. Rough estimate for account opening alone (if all ~4 cr women): ₹50–200 cr one-time. Credit cost unknown. Cannot assess feasibility.
sustainability_reasoning: Financial inclusion (bank access + credit) is foundational for economic empowerment. However, promise conflates two distinct interventions: (1) account opening (largely administrative, low cost) and (2) credit access (requires repayment discipline + income source). Sustainability hinges on whether borrowers have viable income-generating activity to repay loans. Without livelihood support, credit alone risks over-indebtedness.

promise_text_en: Establish women's collectives for collective resource management and equitable income distribution
promise_text_ta: ÞòŸ¬è õNð£´ - cˆî£˜ õNð£´
impact_mechanism: Formalizes women's economic participation through collective ownership and transparent income-sharing systems, addressing both exclusion from productive assets and earnings inequality.

promise_components.component: Establish women's collectives for collective resource management
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~4.7 lakh SHGs with ~63 lakh women members (reference data). Promise does not specify: (1) how many NEW collectives will be created, (2) what 'collective resource management' means operationally (land, credit, equipment pooling?), (3) funding mechanism, (4) governance structure. Current SHGs are primarily credit/savings groups, not resource-pooling entities. Promise lacks concrete scale and definition — data unavailable to calculate beneficiaries or cost.

promise_components.component: Equitable income distribution mechanism within collectives
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) how income will be generated (production, services, rent-sharing?), (2) income floor or minimum guarantee, (3) distribution formula (equal shares, needs-based, contribution-based?), (4) enforcement mechanism. Promise names an outcome without defining the instrument — data unavailable to estimate cost or feasibility.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Promise lacks: target beneficiary count, resource definition, funding source, or operational model. No baseline cost can be estimated without specifics on collectives' asset size, income generation mechanism, or support staff required.
sustainability_reasoning: IF implemented with clarity (land/asset pooling + transparent income-sharing rules), this addresses root causes of women's economic exclusion and asset-poverty. However, the promise is too vague to assess viability. Success depends entirely on: (1) whether collectives gain control over productive assets (not just credit), (2) whether income-sharing rules prevent elite capture within groups, and (3) whether group governance includes accountability mechanisms. Without these specifics, the promise risks becoming another SHG-style credit group — symptomatic relief rather than structural change.

promise_text_en: Transform school infrastructure and establish digital learning centers in government schools for enhanced educational outcomes
promise_text_ta: ªñŒJò™ î¬ôïèó‹ - °ñK‚è‡ì‹ â¡ð¶ ä‹ðî£Jó‹ Ý‡´èÀ‚° Íˆî c‡ì ªï®ò õóô£ŸÁŠ ªð¼¬ñ ªè£‡ì îINùˆF¡ î£ŒGô‹
impact_mechanism: Upgrades learning infrastructure and digital access, enabling students to acquire skills aligned with labour market demand and reducing the learning-outcome gap between rural and urban schools.

promise_components.component: Transform school infrastructure in government schools
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 government schools (primary + secondary, reference data). Promise does not specify: (1) which schools are targeted (all 53,000 or subset?), (2) what 'transform' means (renovation, rebuild, partial upgrade?), (3) cost per school, (4) timeline. No baseline cost per school in TN provided in reference data — data unavailable to calculate total capital expenditure.

promise_components.component: Establish digital learning centers in government schools
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) how many digital centers (one per school? per district?), (2) equipment specification (computers, tablets, software), (3) connectivity requirement (broadband, mobile hotspot), (4) teacher training budget, (5) maintenance cost. Promise does not state whether this is in addition to or part of 'transform infrastructure'. Data unavailable to calculate number of centers or capital cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Promise lacks: (1) scope definition (all 53,000 schools or prioritized subset?), (2) cost per school, (3) digital center unit cost, (4) operational budget (teacher training, electricity, maintenance). Even a conservative estimate (e.g. ₹50 lakh per school for basic infrastructure + ₹10 lakh for digital center = ₹60 lakh × 53,000 schools = ₹31,800 cr capital + recurring maintenance cost of ~₹10,590 cr/year) would require 5-10% of discretionary budget annually for 3+ years — but this is speculative without promise specifics.
sustainability_reasoning: Digital access and modern infrastructure ARE structural interventions — they build human capital and enable students to access labour market opportunities beyond agriculture. However, implementation risk is very high: (1) rural schools often lack stable electricity and broadband (no mention of connectivity strategy), (2) teacher digital literacy is not addressed, (3) high maintenance burden on state budget post-implementation (no endowment or cost-recovery model proposed). Without operational sustainability plan and teacher upskilling, digital centers risk becoming unused or underutilized infrastructure.

promise_text_en: Ensure comprehensive basic amenities in schools including drinking water, sanitation facilities, playgrounds, libraries, computers and digital access

impact_mechanism: Removes non-academic barriers to school attendance and learning (water, sanitation, safety) and creates basic dignity and health conditions necessary for academic focus.

promise_components.component: Drinking water in all government schools
promise_components.analysis: ~53,000 government schools (reference data). Promise does not specify: (1) current coverage (how many schools already have water?), (2) source (groundwater, supply, rainwater harvesting), (3) cost per school, (4) maintenance responsibility. Data unavailable — cannot calculate incremental cost.

promise_components.component: Sanitation facilities in all government schools
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) current coverage, (2) type (pit latrines, flush toilets, gender-separate facilities), (3) maintenance protocol, (4) cost per school. Data unavailable — cannot estimate.

promise_components.component: Playgrounds in all government schools
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) current coverage, (2) size/equipment standard, (3) whether land must be acquired (cost implication), (4) maintenance cost. Data unavailable.

promise_components.component: Libraries in all government schools
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) current coverage, (2) number of books per school, (3) librarian cost, (4) book replacement budget. Data unavailable.

promise_components.component: Computers and digital access in all government schools
promise_components.analysis: Overlaps with Promise 1 — no additional clarity. Data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Promise bundles 5 distinct infrastructure elements with no specification of: (1) current baseline coverage for each, (2) unit cost per amenity per school, (3) whether this is new spending or reclassification of existing budgets. Rough order-of-magnitude estimate (ungrounded): water + sanitation + playground + library + computers = ₹15–30 lakh per school × 53,000 schools = ₹79,500–159,000 cr capital (if all schools start from zero, which is unlikely). Recurring maintenance cost unknown. This cannot be assessed without baseline coverage data.
sustainability_reasoning: These amenities ARE foundational to school function and attendance — they address real, documented barriers (esp. sanitation for girls). Water and sanitation have proven health ROI. However, implementation complexity is high: (1) water security in rural TN is precarious (no mention of water source strategy), (2) no maintenance model specified (who pays for repairs? how is accountability ensured?), (3) libraries without teacher training on usage become static assets. Without operational budgets and maintenance responsibility assignment, these risk becoming incomplete or deteriorating assets.

promise_text_en: Provide nutritional support and mid-day meals with quality ingredients and nutritional standards in government schools
promise_text_ta: °ñK‚è‡ìˆF¡ c†Cò£è M÷ƒ°‹ è¡Qò£°ñKJ™ ï£‹ îIö˜ Üó², ÔîIö˜ «è£J¬ô‚Õ è†®ªò¿Š¹‹
impact_mechanism: Improves nutrition and food security during school hours, supporting cognitive development and reducing absenteeism, especially for children from low-income households.

promise_components.component: Provide nutritional support and mid-day meals
promise_components.analysis: Mid-day meal scheme ALREADY in effect: ~53 lakh children covered in Classes 1–10 (reference data). Promise states 'with quality ingredients and nutritional standards' — this implies UPGRADE to existing scheme, not new expansion. Current scheme budget: data unavailable. Promise does not specify: (1) whether coverage expands (to pre-primary? secondary?), (2) cost per meal, (3) new nutritional standard (calories, micronutrients, protein content), (4) quality assurance mechanism.

promise_components.component: Ensure nutritional standards in existing mid-day meals
promise_components.analysis: No specification of what standard — data unavailable. Without baseline cost/meal and new standard cost, cannot calculate incremental spending. If meals are upgraded from ₹5/meal to ₹8/meal (illustrative), cost = (₹8 − ₹5) × 53 lakh children × ~200 school days/year = ₹3 × 53 lakh × 200 = ₹318 cr/year. But this is speculative without promise detail.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable for new cost — scheme exists. If promise means modest quality upgrade (e.g. +₹2–3/meal), estimated incremental cost = ₹300–450 cr/year (~0.2% discretionary budget). If expansion to secondary classes or pre-primary, cost scales upward (data unavailable for student numbers). Risk: promise conflates existing scheme with upgrade, creating ambiguity on actual new spend.
sustainability_reasoning: Mid-day meals provide real, immediate nutrition relief and demonstrate proven impact on attendance and learning outcomes — this is documented evidence-based intervention. However, it is symptomatic, not structural: meals address hunger during school hours but do NOT address root causes of child malnutrition (household food insecurity, maternal health, access to diversified diet at home). Long-term nutrition security requires income support or agricultural productivity improvements for families. Meals are essential and should be sustained, but alone they do not fix poverty or food insecurity.

promise_text_en: Modernize school buildings with improved infrastructure, safety measures and accessibility features
