impact_mechanism: Removes physical and safety barriers to school access (structural damage, unsafe buildings, inaccessibility for disabled students), enabling all children to attend and learn safely.

promise_components.component: Modernize school buildings with improved infrastructure
promise_components.analysis: ~53,000 schools (reference data). Promise does not specify: (1) current state (how many schools are in poor condition?), (2) what 'modernize' means (repair, rebuild, partial upgrade), (3) cost per school, (4) timeline. Data unavailable.

promise_components.component: Install safety measures in school buildings
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) what safety measures (fire exits, lightning rods, structural reinforcement, earthquake proofing?), (2) current baseline, (3) cost per school. Data unavailable.

promise_components.component: Add accessibility features for disabled students
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) scope (ramps, accessible toilets, signage, learning materials in Braille?), (2) current coverage, (3) cost per school. Data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Promise is architectural and structural; without building survey data (number of schools needing repair, damage extent), cost per school cannot be estimated. Conservative rough estimate (ungrounded): if 20% of schools (10,600) need significant structural work at ₹50 lakh per school = ₹5,300 cr capital. If all 53,000 schools need modest accessibility upgrades at ₹10 lakh per school = ₹5,300 cr capital. Total = ₹10,600+ cr capital (5–8% of discretionary budget over 2–3 years). But baseline condition data unavailable.
sustainability_reasoning: Safe, accessible school buildings ARE structural — they remove barriers that prevent children (especially disabled children and girls) from attending school. One-time capital investment with lasting benefit. However, implementation risk is real: (1) large capital budget required upfront (state may struggle), (2) post-construction maintenance critical — decay is rapid without endowment, (3) universal accessibility requires ongoing cost (ramps wear, accessible toilets need servicing). Without maintenance budget and accountability, buildings will deteriorate and benefits erode within 10 years.

promise_text_en: Establish maternity and childcare support centers with access to healthcare and nutritional assistance for mothers
promise_text_ta: àôèˆF™ Í¡ø£‹ ªð¼‹ õ™ôó¬ê GÁMò ï‹ Ü¼¬ñŠ ªð¼‹ð£†ì¡ Ü¼‡ªñ£N„ «ê£öÂ‚°‹
impact_mechanism: Provides integrated healthcare, nutrition, and childcare during critical early motherhood period, reducing maternal and infant mortality and enabling mothers to return to income-generating activity.

promise_components.component: Establish maternity and childcare support centers
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) how many centers (one per district? per block? per 100,000 women?), (2) service model (day-care, residential, part-time), (3) staffing (nurses, ANMs, childcare workers), (4) coverage (all mothers or targeted to low-income?). TN has ~3.95 cr women (reference data), but data unavailable on number of mothers of children under 5 or annual births. Cannot estimate scale.

promise_components.component: Access to healthcare for mothers
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) type (antenatal, postnatal, family planning), (2) whether this is new service or coordination with existing PHCs/CHCs, (3) cost. Data unavailable.

promise_components.component: Nutritional assistance for mothers
promise_components.analysis: No specification of: (1) form (cash allowance, food supplementation, fortified provisions), (2) duration (how many months post-birth?), (3) eligibility criteria. Data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Promise lacks: (1) target number of mothers/year, (2) cost per mother per month, (3) center operational cost. Illustrative rough estimate (ungrounded): if 15 lakh mothers/year targeted, and center-based care + healthcare + nutrition supplement = ₹300/month per mother for 12 months, cost = ₹300 × 12 × 15 lakh = ₹540 cr/year (~0.3% discretionary budget). But this is speculative without promise specifics.
sustainability_reasoning: Maternity/childcare support DIRECTLY addresses root causes: (1) maternal malnutrition → low birth weight → chronic childhood stunting, (2) lack of childcare → mothers unable to work, (3) limited healthcare access → high maternal mortality and morbidity. If properly implemented (integrated healthcare, nutrition, safe childcare, income-enabling flexibility), this is transformative. However, high implementation risk: (1) TN already has PHCs, ANM networks, and nutrition programmes — promise does not clarify whether this is coordination of existing services or new parallel system (risk of duplication or fragmentation), (2) no mention of male partner/household engagement (critical for long-term behavior change), (3) sustainability depends on state nursing/childcare workforce — current supply data unavailable. Without clarity on integration with existing health system and commitment to workforce development, centers risk becoming isolated interventions.

promise_text_en: Provide subsidized healthcare services and insurance schemes for pregnant women and nursing mothers

impact_mechanism: Reduces maternal/neonatal mortality and morbidity by removing financial barriers to antenatal/postnatal care and insurance protection.

promise_components.component: Subsidized healthcare services for pregnant women and nursing mothers
promise_components.analysis: Promise lacks specificity: no definition of 'subsidized' (50% subsidy? 100% free?), no list of covered services (routine checks, complications, delivery, postnatal care?), no stated beneficiary count. TN has ~3.95 cr women; annual pregnancies ~79 lakh (assuming 2% birth rate). Cost per beneficiary varies from ₹5,000 (basic antenatal) to ₹50,000+ (complicated delivery). Data unavailable — cannot calculate without service scope and subsidy rate.

promise_components.component: Insurance schemes for pregnant women and nursing mothers
promise_components.analysis: No details: coverage amount, scheme type (govt-backed, partnered, standalone), premium structure, duration of coverage (pregnancy only vs 6 months post-delivery?). TN already has Ayushman Bharat/Arogya Sri covering maternity up to ₹5 lakh per case. Promise does not clarify if this is expansion, replacement, or standalone. Data unavailable — cannot estimate incremental cost or beneficiary overlap.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate. Promise lacks parameters: subsidy depth (% or ₹/beneficiary), service bundle, insurance premium/payout, beneficiary count, and duration. Without these, any cost figure would be invented. If assumed 100% free comprehensive maternity care for ~79 lakh annual pregnancies at avg ₹20,000/case = ₹1,580 cr/year (0.86% of discretionary budget), but this is speculative and likely understates actual service delivery cost.
sustainability_reasoning: Maternal health access is a root cause of women's morbidity, mortality, and economic vulnerability — removing financial barriers is structural reform. However, promise is undermined by vagueness: without defined service scope, insurance terms, and eligibility, it cannot be implemented consistently or costed. Risk of either token subsidy (optics) or budget overrun.

promise_text_en: Ensure timely and free distribution of maternity entitlements through designated implementation centers
promise_text_ta: ï‹ ªð¼‹ð£†ì¡ Ü¼‡ªñ£N„ «ê£ö¡ è†®ò î…¬êŠ ªð¼¾¬ìò£˜ «è£J½‚° ªõO«ò õ£êL™ àœ÷ Ü¼‡ªñ£N„ «ê£ö¡ C¬ô Üõ¡ è†®ò «è£J½‚°œ«÷«ò GÁõŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Ensures pregnant/nursing mothers receive statutory cash/goods entitlements (maternity benefit, vouchers, nutrition support) without administrative delay or out-of-pocket loss.

promise_components.component: Timely distribution of maternity entitlements through designated centers
promise_components.analysis: Promise defines mechanism (designated centers, timely distribution) but not the entitlements themselves. TN already operates maternity benefit schemes (e.g., PMMVY under central govt; TN's own schemes). 'Designated implementation centers' suggests new institutional structure, likely within existing ICDS/health infrastructure (~50,000+ ASHA workers, ~20,000+ ANM centers). Establishing 300+ new centers could cost ₹10–50 lakh per center (building, staff, systems) = ₹300–1,500 cr capital + ₹100–300 cr annual operations. But benefit amount not stated. Data unavailable — cannot calculate per-beneficiary cost without entitlement quantum.

promise_components.component: Free distribution (no cost to beneficiary) and removal of delays
promise_components.analysis: Implicit: eliminates waiting time and out-of-pocket fees for entitlement access. No cost to beneficiary is structural change. But measurable impact depends on current delay/denial rates (data unavailable). If current system already covers 80%+ of eligible women with <30-day delay, incremental benefit is low. If coverage is 40% with 90-day delay, impact is high. Cannot assess without baseline data.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate total cost. Capital cost for 300 new designated centers: ₹300–1,500 cr (at ₹10–50 lakh/center). Annual operations + entitlement distribution: data unavailable — depends on entitlement amounts, beneficiary count, and administrative overhead. If 79 lakh annual beneficiaries × avg ₹5,000/beneficiary entitlement = ₹3,950 cr/year (2.1% discretionary budget). Total likely ₹4,000–5,000 cr/year after center operations, which is feasible but requires central co-funding given fiscal deficit.
sustainability_reasoning: Timely, accessible entitlement delivery removes procedural barriers to maternal welfare and income security — a structural fix. However, sustainability depends on durable institutional capacity: 300 new centers must be staffed, funded, and monitored long-term. Risk: centers become under-staffed, entitlements delayed again, or politically captured. Requires legal framework and independent oversight.

promise_text_en: Establish 300 new schools across the state, with progression from primary to middle to high schools, ensuring continuous education pathway and removing educational barriers
promise_text_ta: ªê…C¬òˆ î¬ôïèó£è‚ ªè£‡´ 300 Ý‡´è÷£è â‹ Ü¼¬ñŠ ªð¼‹ ð£†ì¡èœ A¼wí‚«è£¡ ñè¡ Ýù‰î‚«è£¡, Ýù‰î‚«è£¡ ñè¡ ñ£K‚«è£¡, ñ£K‚«è£¡ ñè¡ «è£«ùK‚«è£¡
impact_mechanism: Creates new educational infrastructure (300 schools) in underserved areas, reducing travel distance/dropout and enabling continuous K–12 pathway for ~300,000–600,000 new students.

promise_components.component: Establish 300 new schools with primary → middle → high school progression
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 govt schools (primary + secondary combined). Adding 300 new schools = 0.57% increase. Assuming each school serves 1,000–2,000 students (mixed primary/secondary), 300 schools could enroll ~300,000–600,000 students — roughly 6.7–13.5% of current ~89 lakh govt school enrollment. Cost per school: primary ₹3–5 cr (land, building, equipment); middle ₹5–8 cr; high ₹8–12 cr. Average ₹6 cr/school × 300 = ₹1,800 cr capital cost. Annual recurrent (salaries, utilities, materials): ~₹3–4 lakh/school × 300 = ₹90–120 cr/year. Total first-year cost: ₹1,890–1,920 cr.

promise_components.component: Ensure continuous education pathway (no gaps, all classes co-located)
promise_components.analysis: Mechanism not specified: does 'progression' mean 300 schools each spanning K–12 (unlikely), or clusters of schools covering K–5, 6–8, 9–12? If each school is K–12, 300 schools = ~900 separate blocks (3 levels × 300), which is capital-intensive. If clusters, implementation risk rises (coordination needed). Assume mixed: 100 combined primary–middle + 100 middle–high + 100 primary-only = 300 schools. Feasibility: medium. Benefit: eliminates forced transitions; reduces dropout from school change (estimated 5–10% of dropouts in rural TN are transition-related).

fiscal_cost_note: Capital: ₹1,800 cr (₹6 cr/school avg × 300). Recurrent: ₹100 cr/year (salaries ~₹60 cr/year for 1,800 new teachers + support staff; non-salary ~₹40 cr/year). Total 5-year cost: ₹1,800 + (₹160 cr/year × 5) = ₹2,600 cr. Annual ongoing: ₹160 cr = 0.087% of discretionary budget (very low). Feasible within discretionary envelope, but requires capital mobilization over 3–5 years. State could front-load via bonds or central assistance.
sustainability_reasoning: New school infrastructure removes physical barriers to enrollment and continuity — a durable, structural fix. However, sustainability depends on: (1) teacher recruitment and retention — TN has chronic teacher shortages; (2) quality of pedagogy — building schools without quality teaching wastes capital; (3) local demand — if schools are sited without enrollment surveys, many may remain under-utilized. Promise must be paired with teacher hiring commitment and quality assurance mechanism.

promise_text_en: Create permanent positions for teachers and support staff in schools with transparent recruitment processes ensuring merit-based selection
promise_text_ta: ióŠªð¼‹ð£†® «õ½ï£„Cò£˜ ªð‡EùˆF¡ ióˆ¬î àô°‚°Š ð¬øê£ŸPò ªð¼¬ñI° ióŠªð¼‹ð£†® «õ½ï£„Cò£˜ Üõ˜èO¡ õ£›‚¬è õóô£Á ðœO
impact_mechanism: Replaces temporary/contract teaching with permanent positions, securing teacher income stability and reducing turnover, which improves classroom continuity and student learning outcomes.

promise_components.component: Create permanent teacher positions in government schools
promise_components.analysis: TN employs ~5 lakh govt school teachers (est. from 53,000 schools × avg 9–10 teachers/school). Current state: unknown proportion on permanent vs contract/temporary rolls. Central govt data (2022) shows ~30% of govt teachers in India on contract; TN likely similar or higher. If 30% of 5 lakh = 1.5 lakh teachers are contract/temporary, converting to permanent would cost: avg teacher salary ₹25–30 lakh/year (salary + benefits). Incremental cost (difference between contract ₹8–12 lakh and permanent ₹25–30 lakh): ~₹15–18 lakh/teacher × 1.5 lakh = ₹2,250–2,700 cr/year. Data unavailable — actual count of contract teachers in TN schools not provided.

promise_components.component: Transparent, merit-based recruitment process
promise_components.analysis: Mechanism: independent commission, written exam, interview panel oversight. No cost specified (administrative overhead ~1–2% of salary cost = ₹30–50 cr/year). Benefit: reduces patronage hiring, improves teacher quality. Implementation: requires TPSC/TRB restructuring, likely 18–24 month timeline. Medium risk: political pressure to relax merit criteria common in state hiring.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated incremental cost (contract → permanent): ₹2,250–2,700 cr/year (assuming ~1.5 lakh teachers converted and ₹15–18 lakh salary premium). This is 1.22–1.47% of discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr. Feasible but requires budgetary reallocation or revenue increase; NOT a marginal add-on. Transparent recruitment overhead: ~₹30–50 cr/year. Total: ~₹2,300–2,750 cr/year ongoing.
sustainability_reasoning: Permanent positions create secure income for teachers, enabling professional commitment and reducing poverty-driven teacher absenteeism — addressing a root cause of educational quality. Merit-based recruitment improves teacher capability. However, high fiscal risk: TN budget already under stress (fiscal deficit ₹101,344 cr); this adds 1.2–1.5% permanent cost. Sustainability requires: (1) revenue growth or spending cuts elsewhere, (2) political will to enforce merit against patronage. Risk of partial implementation (promise covers 50%, many hired non-merit).

promise_text_en: Provide support and incentives for marginalized communities including SCs, STs, minorities and other deprived groups in educational access and opportunities
promise_text_ta: ñ£ù ñøõ˜èœ ñ¼¶ð£‡®ò˜, ÌLˆ«îõ¡, ió¡ Üö°ºˆ¶‚«è£¡, ió¡ ²‰îóLƒè‹, ªð¼‹H´° ºˆî¬óò˜ ÝA«ò£K¡ G¬ù¬õŠ «ð£ŸÁ‹
impact_mechanism: Removes systemic barriers (caste, religious, economic bias in school access/treatment) for SC/ST/minority students, increasing enrollment and completion rates among historically excluded groups.

promise_components.component: Support and incentives for SCs, STs, minorities, and deprived groups in educational access
promise_components.analysis: SC/ST population in TN: ~2.0 cr (est. 25% of 7.9 cr). Current school enrollment disparities: SC/ST girls have ~5–10% lower enrollment than general girls (data unavailable — cannot quantify gap). 'Support and incentives' likely means: (a) targeted scholarships (e.g., ₹500–2,000/month), (b) identity-based enrollment drives, (c) reservation enforcement in teacher corps. Cost depends on mechanism: if scholarship at ₹1,000/month × 12 months for 50 lakh additional SC/ST students = ₹600 cr/year. If incentive is one-time boarding/book allowance ₹2,000/student for 20 lakh students = ₹400 cr. Data unavailable — cannot calculate without specific scheme design and target enrollment.

promise_components.component: Ensure opportunities (not just access) in educational outcomes
promise_components.analysis: Implicit: remedial coaching, mentoring, anti-discrimination enforcement. No cost stated. High implementation risk: requires sustained cultural change in school attitudes and teacher behavior — policy alone insufficient. Needs monitoring, complaint redressal, and accountability mechanism (typically ₹20–50 cr/year for oversight + training).

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate precisely. If scholarship scheme for 50 lakh SC/ST students at ₹1,000–2,000/month = ₹600–1,200 cr/year. If smaller cohort (20 lakh) at higher incentive (₹5,000/year) = ₹100 cr/year. Oversight/monitoring: ₹30–50 cr/year. Likely range: ₹130–1,250 cr/year depending on scheme scope. At midpoint ₹690 cr/year = 0.38% of discretionary budget (feasible, but requires explicit budget line).
sustainability_reasoning: Targeted removal of systemic educational barriers for historically excluded groups addresses root causes (discrimination, resource gaps). However, sustainability highly dependent on: (1) sustained political commitment to equity over multiple electoral cycles, (2) teacher culture change — scholarships alone do not prevent discrimination or low expectations in classroom, (3) family-level barriers (migration for labor, caste occupations) remain unaddressed. Risk: scheme becomes patronage tool (selective enforcement) or budget is cut after 2–3 years. Requires institutional reform (reservations, caste-sensitization training, independent oversight) alongside cash transfers.

promise_text_en: Conduct skill development training programmes with employment placement support for youth and ensure access to quality career guidance
promise_text_ta: îI›Š ¹ˆî£‡´ â¶ªõ¡Á ªîKòM™¬ô, îIö˜èOì‹ Þ¼‰¶ HK‰¶ ªê¡ø ªî½ƒè˜, è¡ùì˜, ñ¬ôò£Oèœ
impact_mechanism: Bridges gap between school/graduation and job market by equipping youth with market-relevant skills and employment networks, reducing structural unemployment and underemployment.

promise_components.component: Skill development training programmes with employment placement support
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~80 lakh youth (ages 18–25 est., ~10% of 7.9 cr population). Current skill training capacity: ~2–3 lakh/year through govt/private ITIs and skill councils (data unavailable). Promise implies scaling: if targeting 10 lakh youth/year across sectors (IT, manufacturing, hospitality, agriculture, services), cost per trainee ~₹30,000–50,000 (8–12 week course, stipend, equipment, placement support). 10 lakh × ₹40,000 = ₹400 cr/year. Placement support (job matching, employer liaison, follow-up): ~₹5,000–10,000/trainee = ₹50–100 cr/year. Total: ₹450–500 cr/year. Actual beneficiary coverage unknown — promise vague on scale.

promise_components.component: Employment placement support and career guidance
promise_components.analysis: Mechanism: placement cells in training centers + employer partnerships. Cost: hiring placement officers (~1 per 100 trainees = 100 officers at ₹8–10 lakh salary = ₹8–10 cr/year) + employer engagement/MoUs + career counselors (~500 at ₹6 lakh salary = ₹3 cr/year). Total placement infrastructure: ₹15–20 cr/year. Placement success metrics undefined — promise does not commit to minimum placement rate (e.g., 70% within 6 months). High risk: without employer coordination, placement remains aspirational.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost: ₹450–520 cr/year (training + placement + career guidance). At midpoint ₹485 cr/year = 0.26% of discretionary budget (feasible). However, this assumes ~10 lakh trainees/year; if promise targets smaller cohort (e.g., 2 lakh/year), cost drops to ₹97 cr/year. Promise scale is unspecified. Also requires employer demand signal; if jobs don't exist, placement becomes token internships with no wage income.
sustainability_reasoning: Skill + placement directly addresses structural unemployment and underemployment — a root cause. However, sustainability depends heavily on: (1) job creation in target sectors (TN cannot create jobs by itself; depends on FDI, manufacturing growth, service sector expansion), (2) skill-job matching accuracy (training for obsolete skills wastes funds), (3) sustained employer engagement (without ongoing partnerships, placement fails). Risk: programme becomes subsidy for trainees with no employment gain. Requires complementary policies: labor law reform, FDI promotion, infrastructure for manufacturing clusters. Skill training alone is insufficient without demand-side job creation.

promise_text_en: Provide free laptops/tablets to all students and establish digital learning infrastructure with internet connectivity in all schools

impact_mechanism: Distributes digital devices and connectivity infrastructure to enable equitable access to digital learning resources across all government schools.

promise_components.component: Free laptops/tablets for all students in TN government schools
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 government schools (primary + secondary) and ~89 lakh government school students. Cost per device (laptop/tablet): data unavailable — cannot calculate total device cost. Assuming mid-range tablet at ₹15,000–25,000: 89 lakh devices × ₹20,000 (estimate) = ₹1,78,000 cr initial capital. Promise provides no quantity, device spec, or phased rollout timeline. Arithmetic incomplete without device cost specification.

promise_components.component: Digital learning infrastructure with internet connectivity in all schools
promise_components.analysis: Requires network installation, server infrastructure, bandwidth subscriptions for ~53,000 schools. Annual operational cost (bandwidth + maintenance): data unavailable — cannot calculate. One-time infrastructure cost: data unavailable. Promise provides no budget, no phasing, no technology standard.

fiscal_cost_note: Device cost alone estimated ₹1,78,000 cr (unverified device price); annual ops cost unknown. If accurate, device cost is 96.6% of FY2024-25 discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr) — manifestly unaffordable in single year. Requires multi-year phasing and central/PPP funding. Implementation cost fundamentally cannot be verified without device price specification in promise.
sustainability_reasoning: Digital access addresses structural barrier to equitable learning outcomes, especially for rural and marginal students. However, devices alone do not ensure learning gains without teacher training, content, and electricity reliability. Fiscal unaffordability in announced timeframe creates delivery risk.

promise_text_en: Strengthen teacher training programmes and establish continuous professional development system for educators

impact_mechanism: Builds educator capacity through ongoing professional development, directly improving classroom instruction quality and student learning outcomes.

promise_components.component: Strengthen teacher training programmes
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~9 lakh registered teachers (approx, derived from ~89 lakh students and 1:10 student-teacher ratio estimate). 'Strengthen' is non-specific: no baseline standard, no target competency, no duration of training. Cost per teacher training: data unavailable — cannot calculate. Promise provides no budget allocation, no phasing, no measurable outcome metric.

promise_components.component: Establish continuous professional development (CPD) system for educators
promise_components.analysis: CPD implies recurring (not one-time) training cycles. Cost model unknown: mandatory hours/year? frequency? modality (in-service, online, residential)? Ongoing annual budget for CPD: data unavailable. Promise provides no implementation framework or cost estimate.

fiscal_cost_note: Annual cost unknown — no baseline training spend provided. Reasonable estimate: if ₹5,000–10,000 per teacher per year for CPD, then 9 lakh teachers × ₹7,500 = ₹67.5 cr/year (rough, unverified). This is ~0.04% of discretionary budget — fiscally feasible. However, actual cost structure not declared in promise; risk of underestimation.
sustainability_reasoning: Teacher quality is a root cause of learning outcomes. Continuous CPD directly addresses pedagogical capacity and skill gaps. Sustainability depends on institutionalizing funding and making CPD compulsory (not voluntary). If framed as one-time training burst, benefit decays; if embedded as annual requirement with career incentives, it becomes structural.

promise_text_en: Implement comprehensive school meal schemes with nutritional standards and ensure adequate funding for mid-day meal programmes

impact_mechanism: Establishes nutrition standards and dedicated funding for mid-day meals, ensuring consistent nutritional intake for schoolchildren and enabling attendance.

promise_components.component: Implement comprehensive school meal schemes with nutritional standards
promise_components.analysis: Mid-day meal scheme currently covers ~53 lakh children (Classes 1–10). Promise does not specify: who is 'all students' (Classes 1–12? pre-primary?)? cost per meal? nutritional calorie/protein target? Promise provides no budget, no nutritional standard detail, no implementation mechanism beyond 'nutritional standards'.

promise_components.component: Ensure adequate funding for mid-day meal programmes
promise_components.analysis: 'Adequate funding' is undefined. Current spend: data unavailable. If scheme covers 53 lakh children and cost is ₹5–7 per meal (TN rate ~₹3–5 per meal as of 2024), then 53 lakh × 200 school days × ₹6 (midpoint) = ₹636 cr/year. Promise does not declare new funding quantum or target spend level.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost to maintain existing scheme at ₹6/meal: ₹636 cr/year (~0.35% of discretionary budget). If expanded to all students (K–12, ~100+ lakh) at ₹6/meal: ₹1,200 cr/year (~0.65% of discretionary budget). Feasible within discretionary budget. Risk: promise vagueness on 'adequate' funding makes enforcement difficult; incremental cost unknown.
sustainability_reasoning: Nutrition is a root cause of school attendance, health, and cognitive development. Adequate meal funding directly removes a barrier to enrollment and learning. Sustainability depends on protecting meal budget from fiscal cuts and maintaining per-meal cost standards; if politicized or under-funded, benefit erodes.

promise_text_en: Ensure one free school meal per student daily and allocate adequate budget for implementing meal schemes

impact_mechanism: Guarantees daily school meal provision to all students, removing hunger as a barrier to attendance and learning.

promise_components.component: One free school meal per student daily
promise_components.analysis: Scope not explicit: all government school students or all students (including private)? Assuming government schools, ~89 lakh students. 1 meal/day × 200 school days/year × 89 lakh students. Cost per meal: data unavailable from promise. Using reference TN subsidy baseline of ₹3–5/meal: 89 lakh × 200 × ₹4 (midpoint) = ₹712 cr/year. Promise provides no per-meal cost, no expansion to pre-primary or Classes 11–12.

promise_components.component: Allocate adequate budget for implementing meal schemes
promise_components.analysis: Same as Promise 2: 'adequate' is undefined. No new budget quantum declared. Promise assumes current ₹3–5/meal cost will increase or scale without specifying to what level.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost for 89 lakh students at ₹4/meal: ₹712 cr/year (~0.39% of discretionary budget). Feasible. If expanded to Classes 1–12 and pre-primary (est. ~120 lakh), cost rises to ~₹960 cr/year (~0.52% of discretionary budget). Both scenarios are affordable. Primary risk: vagueness on 'adequate' budget allows under-funding; deterioration in meal quality/nutrition possible.
sustainability_reasoning: Daily meals directly address malnutrition and absenteeism — root causes of dropout. Promise is concrete (1 meal/day). Sustainability depends on protecting budget line and preventing meal-quality degradation through per-student-cost erosion. If adequately funded annually, this is durable.

promise_text_en: Build comprehensive school infrastructure with safe drinking water, sanitation facilities, and physical infrastructure development across all schools

impact_mechanism: Upgrades school physical and WASH infrastructure, removing basic access barriers (water, sanitation, safety) that impair attendance and learning.

promise_components.component: Safe drinking water in all schools
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 schools. Current coverage: data unavailable — cannot determine retrofit vs. new-build need. Cost per school (bore well, RO unit, storage, maintenance): ₹2–5 lakh per school (estimate, unverified). Total 1-time cost: 53,000 × ₹3.5 lakh = ₹1,855 cr. Annual maintenance: ~10% of capex = ₹185.5 cr/year. Promise provides no cost estimate, no current deficit quantified, no phasing timeline.

promise_components.component: Sanitation facilities in all schools
promise_components.analysis: ~53,000 schools. Cost per school (toilets, hand-wash stations, O&M system): ₹5–10 lakh (estimate, unverified). Total 1-time: 53,000 × ₹7.5 lakh = ₹3,975 cr. Annual maintenance: ~10% = ₹397.5 cr/year. Current coverage: data unavailable. Promise provides no cost, no deficit assessment.

promise_components.component: Physical infrastructure development across all schools
promise_components.analysis: 'Physical infrastructure' is unspecified: classrooms, roofs, electrification, labs, library, playground, disabled access? Cost per school for full upgrade: ₹20–50 lakh (estimate, unverified). Total 1-time: 53,000 × ₹35 lakh = ₹18,550 cr. Promise provides no scope definition, no cost, no standard specification.

fiscal_cost_note: 1-time capex estimate (water + sanitation + general infrastructure): ₹1,855 + ₹3,975 + ₹18,550 = ₹24,380 cr (rough, unverified). Annual O&M: ~₹750 cr. One-time cost is 13.2% of discretionary budget — requires multi-year spread (3–5 years = ₹4,876–8,127 cr/year, or 2.7–4.4% of discretionary budget) or central/bond funding. Feasible if phased. Promise provides no phasing, no cost breakdown, high estimation uncertainty.
sustainability_reasoning: WASH and basic safety are root causes of absenteeism and learning loss, especially for girls and marginalized students. Infrastructure is durable once built. Sustainability risk: inadequate O&M budget allocation will cause deterioration; need ring-fenced annual maintenance fund. Implementation risk (high) due to vagueness and cost scale.

promise_text_en: Establish examination reform system to reduce stress and encourage continuous evaluation and holistic learning approach
promise_text_ta: î…¬êˆ îI›Š ð™è¬ô‚ èöè‹ åJô£†ì‹ î…¬êŠ ªðKò «è£J™ è™ªõ†´ è£õ®ò£†ì‹
impact_mechanism: Reduces exam-induced stress and shifts evaluation toward continuous, formative assessment, enabling more holistic skill and knowledge development.

promise_components.component: Reduce stress through examination reform
promise_components.analysis: 'Reduce stress' is subjective and unmeasurable without baseline anxiety metrics. Reform scope not specified: eliminate board exams? shift to continuous evaluation? reduce test frequency? Promise provides no concrete mechanism, no age-group target, no measurable stress reduction target (e.g., % decline in student anxiety, suicide prevention).

promise_components.component: Encourage continuous evaluation and holistic learning approach
promise_components.analysis: 'Continuous evaluation' and 'holistic' are descriptive, not quantified. No cost stated. Implementation model unknown: require state exams to adopt CCE (Continuous Comprehensive Evaluation)? train teachers in portfolio assessment? Promise provides no budget, no timeline, no specification of what 'holistic' means (competencies? portfolios? skills-based?).

fiscal_cost_note: Cost unknown — no training, assessment infrastructure, or technology spend declared. Potential costs: teacher PD for new evaluation methods (₹50–100 cr), online assessment platform development (₹20–50 cr), digital portfolio systems (₹30–50 cr). Rough estimate: ₹100–200 cr 1-time + ₹20–30 cr annual. Both feasible within discretionary budget if spent. Promise provides no budget allocation.
sustainability_reasoning: Exam stress is real, but is a symptom of high-stakes competition and curriculum overload, not a root cause of learning failure. Changing assessment format without reducing competitive pressure or curriculum density achieves minimal lasting change. Promise is vague on mechanism and measurable outcome — framed as pedagogical reform but lacks concrete deliverables (e.g., 'no board exams before Grade 10' or 'portfolio-based assessment mandatory'). Risk of announcement without implementation.

promise_text_en: Ensure teachers, administrators and educational staff receive dignified salaries and timely payment of wages
promise_text_ta: î¬ôõ˜èœ, è¬ô, Þô‚Aò Ý˜õô˜èœ ñŸÁ‹ àôè‹ º¿¬ñ‚°‹ ðóM õ£¿‹ â‹Iù„ ªê£‰îƒèœ
impact_mechanism: Direct wage security for ~850,000 government school staff (teachers, administrators, support) reduces turnover, absenteeism, and improves classroom quality.

promise_components.component: Ensure dignified salaries for government school teachers, administrators, and educational staff
promise_components.analysis: TN operates ~53,000 government schools with estimated ~850,000 teaching and non-teaching staff. No specific salary floor or increase amount stated in promise. Current TN teacher pay scales exist but 'dignified' and 'timely payment' imply (a) raising base salaries above current levels, or (b) eliminating payment delays. Without stated salary target or timeline, cost cannot be calculated. Data unavailable — cannot calculate exact annual fiscal impact.

promise_components.component: Ensure timely payment of wages (end delays)
promise_components.analysis: Promise targets payment timeliness, not salary quantum. If delays are currently occurring, eliminating them requires: (a) cash-flow management to avoid arrears, or (b) administrative reform to process payroll on schedule. No cost premium if arrears are simply prevented; cost = zero if only process improvement. If promise implies retroactive clearing of existing arrears, amount not specified.

fiscal_cost_note: Salary enhancement cost unknown — no target salary level stated. If promise means 10% salary raise across 850,000 staff earning avg ₹40,000/month: 850,000 × 40,000 × 0.10 × 12 = ₹4,080 crore/year (~2.2% of discretionary budget) — but this is illustrative, not based on promise text. Timely payment (process reform) = ~₹0 if only eliminates delays. FEASIBILITY: Salary hike of 2–3% of discretionary budget is affordable; larger increases require reallocation or central support.
sustainability_reasoning: Teacher pay security directly improves retention, reduces absenteeism, and lifts school quality — addressing a root cause of learning outcomes. However, without specifics on salary targets and funding source, the promise remains aspirational. Timely payment is housekeeping, not transformative; but paired with dignity-level salaries, it anchors long-term system stability.

promise_text_en: Conduct annual examination of schools for performance evaluation, infrastructure assessment, and quality standards verification
promise_text_ta: «è£J™ Ìêè˜èÀ‚° Üó² «õ¬ô Ü¬ùˆ¶‚ °®Jù¼‹ Ìêè˜ Ýèô£‹ â¡ø F†ìˆ¬î ï£‹ îIö˜ Üó² àìù®ò£è G¬ø«õŸÁ‹
impact_mechanism: Annual school audits (performance, infrastructure, standards) create accountability feedback loops and identify resource gaps, enabling data-driven improvement.

promise_components.component: Conduct annual examinations of all ~53,000 government schools
promise_components.analysis: Scope: ~53,000 government schools. Audit frequency: annual. Components: (a) performance evaluation (test scores, dropout rates, teacher attendance), (b) infrastructure assessment (building condition, sanitation, labs), (c) quality standards (curriculum alignment, equity). No budget, timeline, or audit agency named. Cost requires staffing: assume minimum 1 auditor per 10 schools (5,300 auditors) + logistics + tools. Rough estimate: 5,300 × ₹50,000/year salary + ₹15 cr overhead = ₹280 crore/year. Data unavailable for audit protocol specifics — cost estimate illustrative only.

promise_components.component: Verification of quality standards across all schools
promise_components.analysis: Implies checklist-based inspection (teacher qualifications, infrastructure codes, enrollment equity, safety). Cost absorbed in annual audit framework above. No additional cost component specified.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost (illustrative): ₹280–400 crore/year for audit staff, tools, and oversight (~0.15–0.22% of discretionary budget). FEASIBILITY: Affordable within discretionary budget. However, cost depends on audit depth and staffing model — contract auditors vs. dedicated cadre will differ significantly. Central support for design already available (NITI Aayog model). No binding commitment to cost in promise text.
sustainability_reasoning: Annual audits create feedback loops that identify infrastructure gaps, teacher performance issues, and equity failures — enabling targeted intervention. Sustainable only if audit findings trigger resource reallocation and corrective action; audit-without-follow-up becomes tick-box compliance. Requires political will to act on audit findings, especially when they expose systemic failures.

promise_text_en: Introduce formal school management system with transparent resource allocation and participatory governance structures

impact_mechanism: Transparent school budgeting and participatory governance (likely via School Management Committees) reduce misallocation, empower parents/communities, and align spending to local needs.

promise_components.component: Introduce formal school management system with transparent resource allocation
promise_components.analysis: Promise targets budget transparency and resource-use clarity. Mechanism likely: (a) public posting of school budgets, (b) itemized spend tracking, (c) audit trails. No specifics on budget format, disclosure platform, or oversight body. Cost = IT platform for budget management + staff training. Assume one person per 100 schools (~530 staff) + platform licensing ₹5 cr/year = ~₹35 crore/year. Data unavailable for full specification.

promise_components.component: Establish participatory governance structures (likely School Management Committees with parent representation)
promise_components.analysis: Promise implies formalizing decision-making bodies with parent/community input. SMCs already exist in many schools; formalization = standardized charter, training, decision thresholds, grievance escalation. Cost = training staff (~1 per school, ₹53,000 × ₹30,000/year = ₹159 crore one-time setup + ₹10 crore/year ongoing support). No budget stated in promise.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated one-time setup: ₹160–200 crore (staff training, governance charters). Ongoing annual cost: ₹45–60 crore (~0.025–0.033% of discretionary budget). FEASIBILITY: Highly affordable as one-time cost; ongoing cost minimal. Requires central/state funding for IT platform design (shareable across all schools) to keep per-school cost low.
sustainability_reasoning: Transparent budgeting and participatory governance directly combat misallocation, elite capture, and neglect of marginalized students. Creates accountability to parents, not just bureaucrats. Sustainability depends on SMC capacity-building and state's willingness to share real decision-making power; if committees are decorative (no authority over hiring, budget), impact is optics. Evidence from other states (e.g., Rajasthan) shows mixed results — strong where state backed devolution with training; weak where committees lacked authority.

impact_mechanism: Curriculum review mechanism ensures teaching content reflects contemporary needs (digital literacy, environmental awareness, vocational skills, minority perspectives) — improving relevance and equity.

promise_components.component: Establish curriculum review mechanism (design, staffing, timeline)
promise_components.analysis: Promise calls for formal periodic review (frequency unspecified). Mechanism likely: (a) expert committee (subject specialists, teachers, researchers), (b) stakeholder consultation (students, parents, employers), (c) pilot testing, (d) revision cycle. Cost = committee staff (~20–50 full-time experts), consultation forums, pilot schools, publication. Estimate: ₹10–15 crore/year for ongoing operations. No specifics in promise text — cost illustrative.

promise_components.component: Ensure curriculum is modern, relevant, and inclusive (addresses contemporary needs, equity)
promise_components.analysis: Outcome targets, not mechanism. 'Modern' = digital/AI exposure; 'relevant' = job market skills; 'inclusive' = representation of marginalized groups (Dalits, Muslims, LGBTQ+, disabled, tribal). Requires curriculum audit, expert input, and pilot feedback. Cost embedded in review mechanism above. Data unavailable for detailed breakdown of inclusivity audit.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost: ₹10–20 crore/year for curriculum committee, consultation, and pilot implementation (~0.005–0.011% of discretionary budget). FEASIBILITY: Minimal fiscal burden; primarily administrative and intellectual. Major risk: political resistance to inclusive curriculum (e.g., minority representation, gender-diversity content, caste-critical framing).
sustainability_reasoning: Curriculum reform directly shapes student learning outcomes, career readiness, and social consciousness — addressing root causes of education relevance and equity. Sustainability depends on depoliticizing curriculum (expert-led, transparent process) and insulating from electoral cycles. Risk: if review process becomes partisan battleground (e.g., Hindu nationalism vs. secularism, gender ideology), curriculum thrashes with each government change, undermining coherence.

impact_mechanism: Targeted support for transgender and gender-diverse students (safe spaces, anti-bullying policy, counseling, name/pronoun recognition) enables school retention and mental health — directly reducing exclusion.

promise_components.component: Provide special support and assistance to transgender and gender-diverse students
promise_components.analysis: Promise scope: TN government schools (~89 lakh students; ~53,000 schools). Estimated trans/gender-diverse student population in TN: 0.5–2% of enrollment (conservative est. 4,000–18,000 students; high uncertainty). Support likely includes: (a) safe facilities (separate/inclusive toilets), (b) policy on name/pronoun recognition, (c) anti-bullying mechanisms, (d) counseling/mental health services, (e) staff training. Cost breakdown unavailable in promise text. Per-student support cost estimate: ₹500–2,000/year for counseling, training, materials. For 10,000 students: ₹5–20 crore/year. Data unavailable — beneficiary count and support package not specified.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost (illustrative): ₹5–25 crore/year depending on student population estimates and support intensity (~0.003–0.014% of discretionary budget). FEASIBILITY: Fiscally minimal; major cost = policy design, staff retraining, facility modification (one-time ~₹10–20 crore). Easily absorbed. Implementation risk high due to social resistance in conservative communities.
sustainability_reasoning: Targeted support addresses systemic exclusion of trans/gender-diverse students (bullying, facility denial, name erasure), enabling school continuation and psychological safety — a root cause of dropout. Sustainability depends on normalizing inclusive policies across all schools (not siloed support centers) and staff training depth. Risk: if support reduces to counseling without broader policy change (safe toilets, anti-bullying enforcement), remains symptomatic. Strong implementation in urban schools, weaker in conservative rural areas — requires monitoring.

promise_text_en: Establish online grievance redressal system for students to report issues and ensure timely resolution of complaints

impact_mechanism: Online grievance system creates low-friction reporting of bullying, discrimination, safety issues, and poor teaching — enabling rapid intervention and reducing student silence/suffering.

promise_components.component: Establish online grievance redressal system for students
promise_components.analysis: Scope: ~89 lakh government school students. System components: (a) web/mobile portal for complaint filing, (b) anonymous or named options, (c) centralized intake and logging, (d) routing to school/district/state per severity, (e) tracking & closure confirmation. Cost = platform development/licensing, backend staffing, training school administrators. Estimate: ₹5–10 crore for platform + ₹20–30 crore/year for 100–150 dedicated staff (complaint intake, routing, follow-up tracking) across state. Data unavailable for exact staffing model.

promise_components.component: Ensure timely resolution of complaints
promise_components.analysis: Outcome target: SLA (Service Level Agreement) on complaint closure, e.g., acknowledgment in 24 hours, investigation completion in 7–30 days depending on severity. Requires: (a) empowered intake officer per district, (b) investigation protocol, (c) resolution authority (school principal, district superintendent, state ombudsman depending on escalation), (d) appeals mechanism. Cost embedded in system staffing above. Monitoring/audit function: ~₹10 crore/year.

fiscal_cost_note: One-time platform cost: ₹5–10 crore. Annual operating cost: ₹30–40 crore (~0.016–0.022% of discretionary budget) for staffing, training, monitoring. FEASIBILITY: Affordable and high-impact. Requires integration with existing district administration (shared infrastructure) to avoid duplication.
sustainability_reasoning: Online grievance system removes barrier to reporting (embarrassment, fear, access to authority) and creates accountability for resolution — enabling intervention in abuse, bullying, and neglect. Root cause: opacity and slow response to student concerns. Sustainability depends on (a) political will to act on complaints even if they implicate school staff, (b) adequate investigation capacity, (c) protection for complainants from retaliation. If system receives complaints but investigation stalls or retaliation occurs, becomes optics. Success in Tamil Nadu likely higher than average (literacy, mobile penetration) but risks remain in conservative/remote areas.

impact_mechanism: Certification enables smallholder farmers to access premium markets for sustainable produce, increasing farm income without requiring land redistribution or subsidy.

promise_components.component: Establish Green Forest (GF) certification system for farmers
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies certification establishment but provides no data on: (1) target number of farmers to be certified; (2) annual certification cost per farmer; (3) market premium assumed; (4) implementation timeline. Reference data shows 83 lakh farmer households in TN (Agri Census 2015), but promise does not specify coverage percentage. Data unavailable — cannot calculate total programme cost or beneficiary uptake without explicit targets.

promise_components.component: Enable sustainable agricultural practices through certification
promise_components.analysis: Mechanism is market access via certification, not direct subsidy or income floor. Sustainability depends on: (1) actual farmer adoption rate (not specified); (2) whether certified produce commands premium in TN markets (not quantified); (3) whether certification training and audit costs deter marginal/small farmers. Data unavailable — no specification of training subsidy, audit fee structure, or expected yield/income gains.

fiscal_cost_note: No budget allocation stated. Estimated implementation cost (certification training, third-party audit setup, farmer registration database, awareness campaigns) likely ₹50–150 cr one-time + ₹20–40 cr/year recurring if targeting 10–20 lakh farmers. Percentages of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr) would be <0.1% one-time, negligible recurring. FEASIBILITY: Low direct cost, but effectiveness depends on market demand and farmer willingness — high delivery risk.
sustainability_reasoning: Certification creates market access (symptomatic relief from low farm-gate prices) but does NOT address structural causes: land fragmentation (42 lakh marginal farmers with <1 acre), input costs, credit access, or minimum income guarantee. If market premium fails to materialize or buyer networks do not expand, farmers derive no tangible gain. Sustainability depends entirely on external private market demand, not state control.

promise_text_en: Promote Community Forest Rights (CFR) for forest-dependent communities and ensure sustainable forest management

impact_mechanism: Community Forest Rights (CFR) recognition grants forest-dependent communities direct control over forest resources, enabling sustainable livelihood diversification and income from forest products (bamboo, honey, non-timber forest produce).

promise_components.component: Promote Community Forest Rights (CFR) for forest-dependent communities
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies CFR promotion but provides no data on: (1) number of forest-dependent communities in TN eligible for CFR; (2) geographic scope (forest area, districts); (3) implementation timeline; (4) annual income gain per community. Reference data contains no TN-specific forest-dependent population count. Data unavailable — cannot estimate beneficiary universe or fiscal cost without explicit coverage definition.

promise_components.component: Ensure sustainable forest management through CFR
promise_components.analysis: Mechanism is rights-based governance (communities manage their own forests) rather than state subsidy. Effectiveness depends on: (1) legal recognition of rights (statutory framework); (2) capacity building for sustainable harvesting; (3) market linkage for forest products. No data provided on training budget, product aggregation schemes, or market-support infrastructure.

fiscal_cost_note: No budget allocation specified. Estimated cost: (1) legal documentation and rights registration ₹20–40 cr one-time; (2) capacity-building for sustainable forest management ₹10–20 cr/year; (3) product aggregation and marketing infrastructure ₹30–60 cr phased. Total: ~₹60–120 cr one-time + ₹10–20 cr/year. As % of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr): <0.1% one-time, negligible recurring. FEASIBILITY: Moderate — depends on inter-departmental coordination (Forest, Tribal Welfare, PANCHAYATS) and federal clearance under Indian Forest Rights Act 2006.
sustainability_reasoning: CFR recognition addresses a ROOT CAUSE: lack of forest-dependent communities' control over livelihood assets. By transferring governance and usufruct rights, communities gain stable income streams (bamboo, honey, herbs) without relying on state welfare. This is structural (redefines property rights and income base), but sustainability hinges on legal enforcement against encroachment, market linkages, and capacity for sustainable harvesting — all requiring sustained state support beyond initial registration.

impact_mechanism: Joint Forest Management (JFM) and recognition of traditional conservation contributions creates co-governance structures that incentivize forest-dweller participation in conservation while providing income from managed forest resources.

promise_components.component: Facilitate joint forest management (JFM) partnerships
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies JFM facilitation but provides no data on: (1) number of JFM committees to be established; (2) villages/forest patches to be brought under JFM; (3) annual budget per JFM unit for equipment, training, honoraria for forest watchers; (4) benefit-sharing ratio between state and community. Data unavailable — cannot quantify cost or scale without explicit targets.

promise_components.component: Recognize and compensate forest conservation contributions of traditional forest dwellers
promise_components.analysis: Mechanism is recognition (legal status) + compensation (direct payment for conservation labor). Compensation rate and coverage undefined. If assuming ₹200–300/day for ~50 days/year of conservation work per dweller, cost per person = ₹10,000–15,000/year, but target population unknown. Data unavailable — cannot calculate total cost without beneficiary count.

fiscal_cost_note: No budget allocation stated. Estimated JFM infrastructure (50–100 committees across TN) + honoraria for forest watchers (~5,000–10,000 persons at ₹12,000/year) = ₹60–120 cr/year. As % of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr): ~0.03–0.07%. FEASIBILITY: Low to moderate cost; delivery risk is medium (requires trained JFM committees, transparent benefit-sharing, conflict resolution mechanisms).
sustainability_reasoning: JFM addresses a ROOT CAUSE: forest dwellers' exclusion from conservation governance and lack of direct compensation for ecosystem stewardship. Recognition + compensation incentivizes long-term sustainable practices. However, sustainability depends on: (1) legal clarity on rights vs. state ownership; (2) stable funding for honoraria (not subject to budget cuts); (3) prevention of elite capture within communities. Without structural legal reforms, JFM remains donor-dependent and fragile.

promise_components.analysis: Promise uses term 'equitable distribution across various categories' but provides no specification: (1) definition of eligible categories (traditional forest dwellers, artisans dependent on forest materials, livestock herders, NTFP collectors, landless agricultural workers, etc.); (2) quantity of rights per category (e.g., hectares of usufruct land, NTFP harvesting quotas); (3) geographic scope and total forest area available; (4) timeline for distribution. sustainability_reasoning: Promise aims to address ROOT CAUSE (lack of livelihood security for forest-dependent populations), but the 'equitable distribution' framing is vague and potentially unsustainable. Without clear operational definitions of 'equitable' and 'various categories,' the scheme risks elite capture (better-organized groups claiming disproportionate rights) and conflicts with traditional rights-holders. Legal clarity and transparent inclusion criteria are essential for sustainability.

promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies implementation of rights provisions 'under forest rights distribution framework' but does NOT clarify: (1) scope: does this align with existing Indian Forest Rights Act 2006, or create new TN state-level protections?; (2) coverage: number of tribal households in TN (reference data unavailable); (3) rights granted (Individual Forest Rights [IFR], Community Forest Rights [CFR], or both?); (4) implementation budget and timeline. promise_components.analysis: Extension to non-tribal forest-dependent groups (e.g., dalit artisans, pastoral communities) is welcome but scope undefined. Recurring cost for enforcement and litigation support: ₹20–40 cr/year. As % of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr): ~0.05–0.1% one-time, ~0.01–0.02% recurring. FEASIBILITY: Moderate — heavily dependent on legal clarity and inter-departmental (Forest, Tribal Welfare, Revenue) coordination.
sustainability_reasoning: Statutory forest rights (if rigorously implemented) address ROOT CAUSE: insecurity of tribal and forest-dependent livelihoods against state forest closure or eviction. Legal rights create enforceable claims. HOWEVER, sustainability is contingent on: (1) clear statutory definition (not vague administrative provisions); (2) enforcement against encroachment and illegal seizure; (3) access to justice (legal aid for disputes). Without judicial enforcement capacity, rights remain paper protections.

promise_text_en: Establish 'Uyarneri Poga Vidiyal Scheme' across 7 districts (Kanyakumari, Tirunelveli, Thoothukudi, Virudunagar, Madurai, Sivagangai, Ramanathapuram) to provide irrigation and agricultural infrastructure support
promise_text_ta: Ü«î êñò‹ Ü¼‰îFò˜èÀ‚è£ù àKò ÞìŠðA˜¬õ ï£‹ îIö˜ Üó² àÁF ªêŒ»‹
impact_mechanism: Irrigation and agricultural infrastructure in water-scarce southern districts increases agricultural productivity, reduces crop failure risk, and enables higher cropping intensity and income.

promise_components.component: Establish 'Uyarneri Poga Vidiyal Scheme' across 7 districts
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies scheme name and geographic scope (Kanyakumari, Tirunelveli, Thoothukudi, Virudunagar, Madurai, Sivagangai, Ramanathapuram) but provides NO data on: (1) type of irrigation infrastructure (borewell, check dams, micro-irrigation drip/sprinkler, canal renovation, etc.); (2) number of farmers to benefit; (3) cost per unit (e.g., ₹ per borewell, ₹ per hectare irrigated); (4) timeline; (5) estimated increase in irrigated area or productivity. Data unavailable — cannot calculate total cost without specification of infrastructure type and scale.

promise_components.component: Provide irrigation support
promise_components.analysis: Generic 'irrigation support' undefined. If borewell drilling: typical cost ₹2–4 lakh per borewell (2–3 lakh for drilling + ₹0.5–1 lakh for pump/casing). If canal renovation: ₹5–20 lakh per km depending on condition. If drip irrigation: ₹0.5–1 lakh per hectare (subsidy share ~50–60%). TN reference data provides no irrigation density or deficit estimate for these 7 districts. Data unavailable — cannot estimate coverage or cost.

promise_components.component: Provide agricultural infrastructure support
promise_components.analysis: Generic 'agricultural infrastructure' could include godowns, seed banks, equipment depots, etc. No specificity on what infrastructure is planned. Data unavailable — cannot scope or cost.

fiscal_cost_note: No budget allocation or beneficiary target stated. ILLUSTRATIVE CALCULATION: If targeting 50,000 farmers across 7 districts (avg. 7,100/district) with mixed irrigation (20,000 borewells @ ₹3 lakh, 15,000 drip systems @ ₹0.75 lakh subsidy, 15,000 hectares of canal renovation @ ₹10 lakh/km, assuming 50 km total) = (₹60 cr + ₹11.25 cr + ₹5 cr) = ₹76.25 cr one-time, plus annual maintenance ₹5–10 cr. As % of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr): ~0.04% one-time, ~0.003–0.005% recurring. This is ILLUSTRATIVE ONLY — actual cost depends entirely on infrastructure type and scale, which are unspecified. FEASIBILITY: Low to moderate cost if well-targeted, but DELIVERY RISK is HIGH due to vague specification, lack of baseline irrigation data, and typical delays in government infrastructure projects.
sustainability_reasoning: Irrigation infrastructure, if properly maintained, addresses ROOT CAUSE: water scarcity and rain-dependent agriculture in drought-prone districts (Madurai, Sivagangai, Ramanathapuram). Reliable irrigation enables stable cropping and income. HOWEVER, sustainability hinges on: (1) appropriate technology selection (borewell drilling without hydrogeological survey risks aquifer depletion); (2) farmer collective management and cost-sharing for maintenance; (3) groundwater recharge programs to prevent well failure; (4) tariff design that prevents subsidy-dependent collapse. Without these, infrastructure becomes a stranded asset as aquifers deplete or maintenance lapses.

Promise provides infrastructure frame but is silent on these operational prerequisites — high risk of non-utilisation if operation model undefined.

impact_mechanism: Employment as ASHA/Anganwadi workers provides stable income floor + health-system integration for denotified community women, creating sustained economic inclusion.

promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies ASHA recruitment priority but provides no: (i) target number of ASHA positions to be created/reserved, (ii) monthly/annual stipend per ASHA (current TN ASHA stipend ~₹5,000–6,000/month estimated from national norms, but NOT specified in promise or reference data), (iii) timeline for recruitment. promise_components.component: Recruit women from denotified communities as Anganwadi workers with priority employment
promise_components.analysis: Same constraint: no target worker count, monthly stipend, or timeline. TN Anganwadi worker stipend ~₹4,500–5,500/month (national average, NOT confirmed in reference data). Without position targets, cost unavailable. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

fiscal_cost_note: ESTIMATED ANNUAL COST: Assumes illustrative scenario — if 10,000 ASHA + 5,000 Anganwadi worker positions created (NOT stated in promise), at ₹60k ASHA/year + ₹54k Anganwadi/year = ₹600 cr + ₹270 cr = ₹870 cr/year (~0.47% discretionary budget). However, promise provides NO recruitment targets — this is speculative. Actual cost depends entirely on unspecified position count.
sustainability_reasoning: ASHA/Anganwadi employment is structural — it provides stable, government-backed income floor + integrates women into health/nutrition systems. However, success depends on: (1) adequate recruitment targets (promise silent), (2) regular stipend payment (historically delayed in some states), (3) meaningful work volume/community engagement. This is high-impact IF implemented; medium implementation risk due to vagueness on scale.

impact_mechanism: Tamil learning centers provide mother-tongue instruction pathway, improving foundational literacy and reducing early dropout, particularly for marginalised groups.

promise_components.component: Establish Tamil language learning centers in all government schools
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies all govt schools in TN (reference data: ~53,000 primary + secondary schools). However, does not specify: (i) whether centers are new facilities or repurposed classrooms, (ii) per-center cost (construction? staffing?), (iii) daily operation model (supplementary after-school? replacement curricula?). Reference data provides school count but not per-school centre cost. Data unavailable — cannot calculate capital + operational cost.

promise_components.component: Establish Tamil language learning centers in all government colleges
promise_components.analysis: Promise scope extends to colleges (TN ~400–450 govt colleges estimated, NOT specified in reference data or promise). Same constraint: no per-college cost, operational model, or staffing detail. Data unavailable — cannot calculate.

fiscal_cost_note: ESTIMATED ANNUAL COST: Speculative scenario — if 53,000 govt schools each require ₹5 lakh one-time setup (₹26,500 cr capital) + ₹2 lakh/year operation = ₹10,600 cr/year operations alone (~5.8% discretionary budget annually, unsustainable). Colleges (est. 450 × ₹10 lakh capital + ₹5 lakh/year) add ₹450 cr capital + ₹225 cr/year ops. TOTAL illustrative annual cost (schools + colleges): ₹10,825 cr (~5.9% discretionary budget) — EXCEEDS safe threshold. However, promise provides NO cost estimate — actual feasibility unknown.
sustainability_reasoning: Tamil learning centers sound culturally resonant but addressing the wrong problem. Root cause of early dropout in govt schools is: poverty (children work), hunger (no meals), distance, caste discrimination, not Tamil language access. Tamil IS medium of instruction in most TN govt schools already. Establishing centers without addressing dropout drivers (food security, transport, caste inclusion) is a symbolic gesture, not structural change. High implementation cost + modest learning impact = low sustainability.

promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 government schools (primary + secondary combined). sustainability_reasoning: Book distribution addresses a symptom — lack of reading material — but does not fix systemic causes of poor literacy: teacher absenteeism, low pupil-teacher ratios, weak pedagogy, or student dropouts. impact_mechanism: District-level employment agencies create a formal job-matching infrastructure for educated youth (10th pass and above), reducing information asymmetry between job-seekers and employers and enabling systematic skill-to-placement pathways.

promise_components.component: Establish man power agencies in all districts (TN has 38 districts) targeting educated youth above 10th standard

impact_mechanism: Internship-integrated schooling embeds work-based learning into the curriculum, building employability skills during school years and creating smoother transition from education to formal employment.

promise_components.component: Establish Internship Integrated Schooling system across schools

impact_mechanism: Digital learning infrastructure (tablets, software, connectivity, teacher training) expands access to online curricula and interactive pedagogy, potentially improving learning outcomes and enabling reach to remote schools.

promise_components.component: Provide Digital Learning to 'one category of schools', then expand to other categories and districts with infrastructure support
promise_components.analysis: Promise uses vague language: 'one category' (primary? secondary? aided? government only?) and 'expand with necessary infrastructure' (no timeline, cost, or rollout sequence). TN government schools: ~53,000. Infrastructure requirement: devices (tablets/computers), internet (WiFi/broadband), software licenses, teacher training, content creation. Cost per school: data unavailable. Beneficiary clarity: no data on how many students will access devices or which grades.

fiscal_cost_note: Cost estimation requires: (a) definition of 'one category' (assume secondary schools, ~10,000–15,000 institutions), (b) device cost (tablet ₹15,000–25,000 per unit), (c) broadband/WiFi infrastructure (~₹50 lakh per school), (d) software/content licenses, (e) teacher training. Rough lower bound: 10,000 schools × 500 devices × ₹20,000 + 10,000 × ₹50 lakh infrastructure = ₹1,000 crore devices + ₹5,000 crore infrastructure = ₹6,000 crore one-time. Amortized 5-year cost + recurring bandwidth/licences: ₹1,200–1,500 crore/year (~0.65–0.81% of discretionary budget). Feasible within budget, but only at reduced scale or phased rollout.
sustainability_reasoning: Digital learning can enhance pedagogy, but devices and connectivity alone do not fix root causes of learning poverty: (a) weak teacher subject knowledge, (b) absenteeism, (c) poor curriculum design, (d) lack of foundational numeracy/literacy. Evidence from Indian digital learning pilots shows limited learning gains without concurrent teacher training and curriculum redesign. Risk: devices become unused assets without systemic pedagogical change.

sustainability_reasoning: Sports talent development is a legitimate social mobility lever, but this promise is primarily signalling with no operational mechanism. Phrases like 'promote excellence' and 'develop talent' lack specificity on which sports, talent selection criteria, training duration, or post-school employment pathways. Risk: funds dispersed as ad-hoc coach stipends and tournament budgets without systematic talent pipeline. sustainability_reasoning: If executed at scale with quality trainers, industry partnerships, and job placement linkages, skill development addresses the root cause of low income and limited livelihood options. Risk of becoming a subsidy for trainees with no employment outcome.

promise_text_en: Introduce 'Organic Farming' module in school curriculum, with support for implementation and expansion to other subjects and districts

impact_mechanism: Integrates organic farming knowledge into school curriculum, creating awareness and pipeline of practitioners for sustainable agriculture.

TN govt schools: ~53,000 (primary + secondary). If module is 2 hours/week × 36 weeks/year × all students in Classes 6–10 (~45 lakh students est.), cost depends on whether delivered by existing teachers (retraining) or new hires. Retraining cost: ~₹5,000/teacher × 53,000 schools ÷ average school size = ~₹500 cr one-time + ₹20–50 cr/year for materials/demo farms. Without stated scope, cannot quantify.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated one-time curriculum development + teacher training: ₹500–1,000 cr. Annual maintenance (materials, demo farms, refresher training): ₹50–100 cr/year (~0.03–0.05% of discretionary budget). sustainability_reasoning: Organic farming awareness in schools creates long-term cultural shift and skill base, supporting sustainable livelihoods for farmer households (~83 lakh). However, this is educational foundation-building, not direct income support. Impact depends critically on (a) how well module is taught (teacher training quality), (b) whether school-based knowledge translates to farm adoption (usually requires extension support and input access, not stated here), and (c) price premium realization for organic produce (market-dependent, not guaranteed by curriculum alone).

Dairy/animal husbandry potential in TN: ~3.95 crore women, ~51% rural (2.0 crore women) + marginal farmers (42 lakh) are candidate beneficiaries. If one center per district (38 districts) with 500 trainees/year = 19,000 trainees/year. Cost per trainee (residential/non-residential) in state dairy schemes: data unavailable — cannot estimate. Assuming ₹30,000/trainee: ₹57 cr/year, but this is speculative.

promise_components.component: Introduce courses on bee-keeping and related profitable vocations
promise_components.analysis: Promise names bee-keeping but does not specify: beneficiary target, course duration, number of beneficiaries trained/year, budget, or link to market access. Bee-keeping suits landless and marginal farmers (42 lakh marginal farmers in TN could be target base, but promise makes no such specification). Cost per bee-keeping trainee: data unavailable. If 5,000 bee-keepers trained/year at ₹20,000/trainee: ₹10 cr/year (speculative).

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost (dairy centers + bee-keeping courses, if conservative): ₹70–100 cr/year (~0.04–0.05% of discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr). Feasibility: MODERATE COST. However, all figures are speculative because promise provides no beneficiary targets, no per-unit cost, and no implementation model (government centers, NGO partnerships, private training providers). Actual cost could range ₹50–300 cr/year depending on scale and delivery model — without stated numbers, cannot commit to projection.
sustainability_reasoning: Dairy, animal husbandry, and bee-keeping address income diversification and rural livelihood security, particularly for women and marginal farmers. These vocations create asset-based income streams (livestock, hives) rather than wage-dependent income. However, success depends critically on: (a) quality of training and ongoing extension support, (b) link to credit (to buy dairy cattle or bees), (c) market access (dairy cooperatives, honey buyers), and (d) veterinary/technical support — none of which are mentioned in the promise. Risk: training without access to productive assets and markets yields low adoption.

promise_components.analysis: Promise is generic: no specification of (a) which classes; (b) hours/week; (c) curriculum content (existing models or new development); (d) trainer qualifications; (e) which schools (all 53,000 or sample); (f) learning outcomes or evaluation. Life Skills Education is already mandated in CBSE/ICSE curricula and partially in state board; promise does not clarify if this is expansion, deepening, or reinforcement. sustainability_reasoning: Life Skills Education improves individual competency and psycho-social well-being — genuine benefits that support school completion and workplace readiness. However, it does not address structural causes of dropout (poverty requiring child labor, poor school infrastructure, teacher absence, discrimination). It is a supportive intervention, not a foundational fix. Impact is real but limited unless paired with removal of barriers to school attendance (e.g., food security, safety, learning quality).

promise_components.analysis: Promise names 'Karate and other sports' but does not specify: (a) which classes; (b) mandatory or elective; (c) hours/week; (d) trainer qualifications (certified karate instructors, sports teachers); (e) equipment/facility requirements; (f) which schools (all 53,000 or sample); (g) link to formal employment (e.g., armed forces recruitment). If karate/sports 2 hours/week for Classes 6–12 (~45 lakh students), training costs include: (i) coach hiring/certification at ~₹50,000/year × 3–5 per school (large schools) or shared district-level coaches; (ii) mats, pads, uniforms (~₹500–1,000/student one-time); (iii) space (most schools lack dedicated sports halls).
