sustainability_reasoning: Karate/sports training improves physical fitness, discipline, and confidence — valuable non-academic outcomes. However, it does NOT directly address root causes of poverty, limited income, or livelihood insecurity. It is a well-being enhancement, not a structural intervention. Employment linkage (armed forces, security) exists but is narrow and depends on additional factors (height, literacy, medical fitness). Impact is real but primarily supportive rather than transformative for income.

promise_text_en: Provide comprehensive education on body awareness, menstrual hygiene, and civic sense in schools
promise_text_ta: °ö‰¬îèO¬ì«ò ð£Lò™ / àì™ MNŠ¹í˜¾ (Sex / Body Awareness), ð£Lù„ êñQò‹ ñŸÁ‹ °®¬ñ àí˜¾ (Civic Sense)
impact_mechanism: Provides health literacy and civic awareness, reducing menstrual stigma, improving health-seeking behavior, and building informed citizenship.

promise_components.component: Provide comprehensive education on body awareness, menstrual hygiene, and civic sense in schools
promise_components.analysis: Promise combines three elements: (a) body awareness; (b) menstrual hygiene; (c) civic sense. No specification of: (i) curriculum scope (what is 'comprehensive'?); (ii) which classes (typically puberty onwards, Classes 5–10 for girls); (iii) classroom-only or school-wide; (iv) teacher training (sensitivity, accuracy); (v) parent consent/communication strategy; (vi) integration with existing health/science curriculum or standalone module; (vii) budget. TN school girl students (Classes 5–10): est. ~22 lakh. Menstrual hygiene already covered (patchily) in state health/science curriculum and schemes (e.g., sanitary pad distribution in schools). Promise does not clarify if this is expansion or quality improvement. Cost: curriculum development + teacher training at ~₹5,000–10,000 per school if phased = ₹300–500 cr one-time, ₹10–20 cr/year for materials/refresher training. Data unavailable for full estimation.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost: ₹15–25 cr/year (~0.01% of discretionary budget ₹184,162 cr). This assumes: (a) curriculum development and teacher training as one-time investment amortized; (b) annual materials and refresher training. Feasibility: VERY LOW COST relative to state budget. Implementation risk is MEDIUM (teacher sensitivity, parent communication, scale) rather than fiscal.
sustainability_reasoning: Education on body awareness and menstrual hygiene addresses genuine health literacy gaps and can reduce school absenteeism among girls during menstruation (a documented issue in rural TN). Civic sense builds informed citizenship. These are real, valuable interventions with documented health/social benefits. However, they do NOT address root causes of gender inequality or girls' school dropout (poverty, early marriage, household labor demands, safety concerns). They are health/literacy support, not structural reform. Impact is important but complementary, not foundational.

promise_text_en: Make Tamil and English as languages of instruction in schools following New Education Policy recommendations
promise_text_ta: îŸ«ð£¬îò ðœO ñŸÁ‹ è™ÖKŠ ð£ìˆF†ìƒèœ îI¬ö å¼ 'ªñ£Nò£è' (Language) ñ†´«ñ ðJŸÁM‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Shifts medium of instruction to Tamil + English to align with NEP 2020, potentially improving conceptual clarity for Tamil-dominant learners while maintaining English competency.

promise_components.component: Medium of instruction in government schools changed to Tamil and English following NEP recommendations
promise_components.analysis: TN govt schools: ~53,000 schools; ~89 lakh students (Classes 1–12, approx). Promise does not specify: (1) which classes Tamil vs English will be used, (2) transition timeline, (3) teacher retraining scope, (4) resource cost. NEP 2020 recommends mother tongue up to Grade 5, multilingual thereafter. Current TN status (Tamil/English medium distribution) — data unavailable. No rupee cost stated; implementation depends on textbook revision, teacher orientation, and curriculum redesign. Fiscal cost data unavailable — cannot calculate.

fiscal_cost_note: Cost data unavailable. Likely includes: teacher orientation (estimated ₹50–150 cr for 53,000 schools × 3–5 days training), textbook reprinting/revision (₹200–500 cr estimate), and curriculum redesign. If estimated at ₹300–700 cr, that is 0.16–0.38% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Feasible but requires explicit budget allocation and timeline.
sustainability_reasoning: Addresses foundational learning outcome gap caused by language mismatch. Evidence (ASER, state boards) shows Tamil-medium students often struggle with English-taught subjects. Shifting to home language instruction can improve comprehension and retention. However, success depends critically on (a) quality of Tamil textbooks in science/maths, (b) teacher competency in English, and (c) sustained funding for continuous curriculum updates. Without these, becomes cosmetic rebranding.

promise_text_en: Transform school curriculum using projector-based learning and digital technology in government schools
promise_text_ta: ªî£ì‚è‚ è™M ÝCKò˜èÀ‚° Þó‡ì£‡´Š ðJŸCè«÷£´ î£Œªñ£Nˆ FøQ™ (îIN™) îQŠ ðJŸC»‹ ÜO‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Introduces projector/digital classroom technology to enable visual, interactive learning and reduce reliance on textbooks alone.

promise_components.component: Install projector-based learning infrastructure and digital technology in government schools
promise_components.analysis: TN govt schools: ~53,000 schools. Promise does not specify: (1) projector count per school (1 or multiple), (2) digital content library scope, (3) internet connectivity guarantee, (4) software platform. Typical cost: 1 projector + screen + installation ≈ ₹1.5–3 lakh per school. If 1 per school: ₹53,000 schools × ₹2 lakh = ₹1,060 cr capital + annual maintenance ₹100–150 cr. If multiple per school or higher spec, costs rise to ₹1,500–2,000 cr capital. Ongoing electricity, bulb replacement, content creation, teacher training not quantified.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated capital cost: ₹1,000–1,500 cr (1 projector per school, basic spec); annual O&M ₹150–200 cr. Total Year 1: ₹1,150–1,700 cr (~0.62–0.92% of discretionary budget). Year 2+: ₹150–200 cr/year (~0.08–0.11%). Feasible over 2–3 years if phased. However, success requires concurrent internet rollout (data unavailable — cost unknown) and digital content creation (high recurring cost not budgeted). Fiscal stress if bundled with other tech promises (tablets, etc.).
sustainability_reasoning: Technology alone does not fix learning outcomes; evidence shows gains are marginal without teacher training, curricular alignment, and digital pedagogy. Projectors are tools, not solutions. High risk of underutilisation if teachers lack training or confidence. Addresses symptoms (boring teaching) rather than root cause (weak teacher preparation, large class sizes, low foundational numeracy). Sustainable only if bundled with teacher professional development and pedagogical redesign.

promise_text_en: Provide tablet devices to all government school students for digital learning
promise_text_ta: ÝCKò˜èÀ‚è£ù áFò àò˜¾ â¡ð¶ Þó‡ì£‡´èÀ‚° å¼ º¬ø ÝŒ¾‚°†ð´ˆîŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Distributes tablet devices to all government school students to enable digital access to learning materials and personalised learning pathways.

promise_components.component: Provide tablet devices to all government school students
promise_components.analysis: TN govt schools: ~89 lakh students (Classes 1–12 estimate). Tablet cost: ₹8,000–15,000 per device (entry-level Android tablet with educational software). Conservative estimate: ₹10,000/tablet. Total capital: 89 lakh × ₹10,000 = ₹8,900 cr. Annual replacement (5-year lifecycle, 20% turnover): ₹1,780 cr/year. Maintenance, charging stations, software licensing, data connectivity: estimated ₹200–300 cr/year additional. Promise does not specify: (1) grade-wise rollout, (2) device spec/OS, (3) connectivity model (WiFi only, or mobile data), (4) software/app ecosystem, (5) loss/theft management.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated capital cost: ₹8,900 cr (one-time); annual recurring: ₹1,780 cr (replacement) + ₹300 cr (O&M, software) = ₹2,080 cr/year. Year 1 total: ₹10,980 cr (~5.96% of discretionary budget). Years 2+: ₹2,080 cr/year (~1.13%). FISCAL STRESS. This exceeds reasonable annual allocation. Comparison: entire FY2024-25 education budget for TN is ~₹60,000 cr; tablets alone would consume ~10% of education spend. REQUIRES CENTRAL FUND SUPPORT or massive reallocation. Phased rollout over 3–4 years reduces annual burden to ₹3,000–3,700 cr/year (~1.6–2% discretionary), still substantial.
sustainability_reasoning: One of the largest single-item education capital commitments; politically attractive but educationally ambiguous. Global evidence (tablets in schools, India e-Kranti) shows device distribution alone does NOT improve learning outcomes without: (a) reliable electricity/internet (TN govt school broadband penetration ~40–50%), (b) teacher fluency in digital pedagogy (widespread deficit), (c) age-appropriate, curriculum-aligned content (largely unavailable in Tamil). High risk of devices becoming unused/unrepaired toys within 2–3 years. Treats access as the problem; ignores that quality instruction is the constraint. Sustainability low unless paired with massive teacher retraining and content development (neither mentioned).

promise_text_en: Introduce question paper setting by students and peer teaching methods to improve classroom learning
promise_text_ta: îQò£˜ ðœOè¬÷Mì„ Cø‰î è™M î¬ìJ¡P õöƒèŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Introduces student-led assessment design and peer teaching to shift classroom from passive to active learning, encouraging metacognition and collaborative problem-solving.

promise_components.component: Students set their own question papers for exams; peer teaching methods integrated into curriculum
promise_components.analysis: Promise scope: ~89 lakh govt school students; ~53,000 schools. Question paper design by students: pedagogically sound (formative assessment, metacognition), but operationally vague. Does this apply to Class 1 or Grade 5+ only? Who validates questions? What prevents cheating? How do boards certify student-set papers? Peer teaching: similarly under-specified. Does this replace teacher instruction or supplement? Teacher-student ratio in TN: data unavailable; cannot assess feasibility. No budget estimate provided. Implementation cost likely modest (teacher training, curriculum guides): estimated ₹30–50 cr (one-time). Annual cost for continuous training/monitoring: ₹5–10 cr.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated cost: ₹40–60 cr capital (training materials, curriculum revision, exam system redesign) + ₹5–10 cr/year ongoing. Total Year 1: ₹45–70 cr (~0.024–0.038% of discretionary budget). FISCALLY NEGLIGIBLE. However, implementation risk is high due to operational complexity (exam board capacity, teacher readiness, quality assurance). Cost is not the constraint; execution and scale are.
sustainability_reasoning: Addresses pedagogical root cause: passive, rote-driven learning. Evidence (UNESCO, NCERT) shows active learning (student questioning, peer collaboration) improves retention and critical thinking. However, structural success requires: (1) teacher competency in facilitative teaching (TN teacher training data unavailable; likely weak for many), (2) culture shift away from exam-centric accountability, (3) willingness to accept lower standardisation in assessments. Without these, practice becomes tokenistic (students nominally design papers, teachers veto/correct). Fiscally cheap but culturally and operationally difficult. Sustainability depends on state board autonomy and political commitment to learning over exam scores.

promise_text_en: Appoint a Student Counsellor in every school to provide guidance and support to students
promise_text_ta: àò˜ è™MJ™ å¼ G¬ôò£ù à÷Mò™ ÜP¾¬óò£÷˜ (Student Counsellor) ðEòñ˜ˆîŠð´õ£˜
impact_mechanism: Embeds counselling support in every school to address mental health, social-emotional learning, and academic guidance barriers.

promise_components.component: Appoint a full-time Student Counsellor (or trained counselling staff) in every government school
promise_components.analysis: TN govt schools: ~53,000. If one counsellor per school (fulltime), salary estimate: ₹5–7 lakh/year (post-graduate, professional level). Total annual: 53,000 × ₹6 lakh = ₹3,180 cr. If one counsellor per 10 schools (cluster model): ₹318 cr/year. If existing teachers retrained part-time (cost ₹20–50 cr training): ₹20–50 cr. Promise does not specify: (1) counsellor-student ratio (standard: 1 per 250–500 students, TN would need ~1,780–3,560 counsellors for 89 lakh students), (2) qualification (diploma, degree, clinical), (3) supervision/support infrastructure. Likely intent: at least one person per school, possibly part-time role.

fiscal_cost_note: If one full-time counsellor per school (best practice): ₹3,180 cr/year (~1.73% of discretionary budget). MODERATE FISCAL STRESS. If cluster model (1 per 5–10 schools): ₹318–636 cr/year (~0.17–0.35%), affordable. If retrained existing teachers: ₹20–50 cr capital + ₹50–100 cr/year ongoing support, highly affordable. Promise is silent on model; assume middle ground: ₹500–1,000 cr/year (~0.27–0.54%). Feasible if phased over 2–3 years. Does not require central support.
sustainability_reasoning: Addresses real barrier: TN schools lack mental health and psychosocial support (counsellor-student ratio currently ~1:5,000–10,000 vs. recommended 1:250). Dropout, learning loss, and student distress correlate with unaddressed emotional/social issues. Counsellors can identify learning disabilities, trauma, family crises, and connect students to support. However, sustainability depends on: (1) quality of counsellor training (if hired without proper qualification, becomes ineffective), (2) teacher and parent acceptance of counselling culture, (3) referral systems and community mental health linkage. If well-implemented, structural impact on student wellbeing and completion rates. Risk: token appointments without proper training or case load management reduce to optics. Recommend phased rollout with rigorous training and supervision budgets.

promise_text_en: Implement a comprehensive Common Framework Examination (CFE) for unified assessment in classes 5-8
promise_text_ta: å¡Pò ÜóC¡ «îCò‚ è™M‚ ªè£œ¬èJ¡ð® 5 ñŸÁ‹ 8 Ý‹ õ°Š¹èO™ ñ£íõ˜èÀ‚°Š ªð£¶ˆ«î˜¾ º¬ø ÜPºè‹ ªêŒòŠð´õî£è º¡ªñ£NòŠð†´œ÷¶
impact_mechanism: Introduces unified, standardised assessment framework for Classes 5–8 to ensure consistent learning measurement across districts and reduce district-level variation in student outcomes.

promise_components.component: Design and implement a Common Framework Examination (CFE) for Classes 5–8 across all government schools
promise_components.analysis: Scope: Classes 5–8, ~53 lakh students (est. 3–4 lakh per grade in govt schools). CFE implies: (1) one standardised exam question bank per subject/grade, (2) common marking rubric, (3) unified result reporting. Promise does not specify: (a) frequency (quarterly, semester, annual), (b) stakes (formative, diagnostic, or summative/high-stakes), (c) whether replaces board exams or supplements, (d) digital vs. paper administration. Design and item banking: estimated ₹30–50 cr (curriculum experts, psychometricians, vendor software). Annual admin cost: ₹20–30 cr (printing, invigilation, scanning/analysis). Training for teachers/evaluators: ₹10–15 cr/year.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated capital (design, item bank, system setup): ₹40–60 cr. Annual cost: ₹30–50 cr (~0.016–0.027% of discretionary budget). FISCALLY NEGLIGIBLE. However, operational and political risks are moderate: exam boards must coordinate, existing district/board assessment autonomy is disrupted, teachers must be trained in new rubrics. Cost is not limiting; execution and consensus are.
sustainability_reasoning: Unified assessment reveals learning gaps but does NOT fix them. A CFE can identify which districts/schools are struggling and which topics students lack (diagnostic value), enabling targeted intervention. However, CFE is a measurement tool, not a teaching intervention. Without accompanying curricular support, teacher training, and resource reallocation to weaker schools, the exam becomes a label-and-move mechanism. Risk: CFE becomes another high-stakes exam, increasing teaching-to-test and narrowing curriculum (already a problem in TN). Sustainability: moderate. Value if paired with CFE-informed remedial programs and teacher support (not mentioned). Alone, it measures the problem without solving it. Recommendation: design CFE as LOW-STAKES, diagnostic tool; couple with actionable feedback loops and targeted teacher training.

promise_text_en: Introduce a separate Secondary Education Programme (SEP) 2025 for classes 1-8 in government schools

impact_mechanism: Establishes a distinct pedagogical framework for primary/lower secondary education, potentially improving curriculum coherence and teaching methods for ~89 lakh government school students in classes 1-8.

promise_components.component: Design and implement Secondary Education Programme (SEP) 2025 for classes 1-8 in government schools
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies a curriculum/programme redesign but provides no budget, timeline milestones, or staffing plan. ~53,000 government schools operate in TN; exact split between primary (1-5) and lower secondary (6-8) not in reference data. Cost of curriculum redesign, teacher training, and material production is data unavailable — cannot calculate without specifics on scope (new textbooks, training duration, resource allocation).

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — no budget allocation or implementation scope provided in promise or reference data. Cannot estimate % of discretionary budget.
sustainability_reasoning: If properly designed and resourced, a coherent secondary education programme addresses the root cause of weak foundational learning outcomes by improving curriculum-pedagogy alignment and teaching quality. However, without specified budget, timeline, and training plan, this remains a framework promise rather than a concrete deliverable. Success depends entirely on execution rigour and sustained teacher training investment.

promise_text_en: Abolish NEET and NET examinations and replace with alternative entrance mechanisms
promise_text_ta: «îCòˆ î°F ñŸÁ‹ ¸¬ö¾ˆ«î˜¾ (NEET) ñŸÁ‹ «îCòˆ î°Fˆ«î˜¾ (NET) «ð£¡ø «î˜¾ º¬øèœ ºŸP½ñ£è c‚èŠðì «õ‡´‹
impact_mechanism: Removes a centrally-administered standardised entrance barrier, theoretically reducing gatekeeping for medical/research admission and shifting selection to state-level mechanisms.

promise_components.component: Abolish NEET examination
promise_components.analysis: NEET is a central government examination (Union competence, not state). Tamil Nadu cannot unilaterally 'abolish' it — state can only opt out of NEET-based counselling (already done in 2021) and conduct separate state NEET if permitted by SC/Ministry. The promise conflates state autonomy with legal authority. This requires constitutional amendment or Union policy change, neither within TN's legislative power.

promise_components.component: Abolish NET examination and replace with alternative entrance mechanisms
promise_components.analysis: NET (National Eligibility Test for research/teaching) is central government administered. Similar constraint as NEET — TN cannot abolish it. Can only design parallel state fellowships/scholarships (already possible under existing education budget). 'Alternative mechanisms' is undefined — no specifics on what these are, cost, or coverage.

fiscal_cost_note: Cannot estimate. Both commitments are beyond state legislative authority. If interpreted as 'design state-level alternatives,' cost is data unavailable — alternative entrance system design and administration cost not specified.
sustainability_reasoning: This promise misrepresents state jurisdiction over central examinations. NEET and NET are Union matters; TN cannot abolish them. The rhetorical appeal (decentralisation of access) addresses real concerns about exam-based gatekeeping, but the mechanism is legally unachievable. If reframed as 'parallel state entrance systems,' it becomes feasible but remains symptomatic — it redistributes gatekeeping rather than addressing root causes of access inequality (economic hardship, rural-urban gaps, school quality).

promise_text_en: Introduce Common University Entrance Test (CUET) for admissions to government colleges
promise_text_ta: ñ£Gô ÜóC¡ è†´Šð£†®™ àœ÷ è™ÖKèÀ‚° â‰î õ¬èò£ù «îCò ¸¬ö¾ˆ«î˜¾‹ ÜÂñF‚èŠðì£¶
impact_mechanism: Introduces a state-level entrance mechanism for college admissions, replacing or supplementing existing decentralised processes, potentially standardising merit assessment across government colleges.

promise_components.component: Introduce CUET (Common University Entrance Test) for admissions to government colleges in TN
promise_components.analysis: TN government colleges already employ merit-based admissions via 12th board exam score + entrance test (various college-level exams exist). CUET is a central system already operational nationally. Promise likely means: adopt/mandate CUET as unified entrance for TN govt colleges, or create parallel TN-CUET. Cost: examination design, IT infrastructure, test administration for unknown number of applicants (data unavailable — total 12th pass cohort applying to govt colleges not in reference). Central CUET administration cost is borne by Union; state cost for optional parallel system is data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — promise does not specify whether TN will adopt existing central CUET (no state cost) or create parallel TN-CUET (cost of exam administration, IT, logistics data unavailable).
sustainability_reasoning: This promise is largely optics. National CUET already exists and is available to TN colleges. Creating a parallel state CUET duplicates infrastructure and adds administrative burden without addressing root barriers to college access (cost of higher education, transportation, childcare, caste/gender discrimination). If framed as adoption of existing CUET, it has minimal fiscal impact but also minimal tangible benefit — it standardises testing without expanding access.

promise_text_en: Provide compulsory training and 20% scholarship for students clearing 10th standard who face economic hardship
promise_text_ta: °PŠð£è à‡¬ñò£ù êÍècF M¿‚è£†´ Ü®Šð¬ìJ™ GóŠðŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Targets post-10th students facing economic hardship with compulsory skills training and partial fee subsidy (20%), directly reducing financial barriers to continuing education.

promise_components.component: Identify students clearing 10th standard who face economic hardship
promise_components.analysis: Reference data: ~89 lakh government school students total; ~53 lakh in classes 1-10 (mid-day meal coverage). Assuming ~11% annual progression, ~10 lakh students pass 10th each year in TN govt schools (data unavailable — exact annual pass rate not in reference). Definition of 'economic hardship' is unspecified — if aligned to BPL (~1.2 cr households) or ration cards (~2.0 cr households), beneficiary count could range 15–40% of 10th passers. Data unavailable — cannot determine exact eligible cohort.

promise_components.component: Provide compulsory training (unspecified duration, trade, location)
promise_components.analysis: Promise says 'training' but provides no detail: duration, modality (in-school vocational, external ITI, apprenticeship), trades offered, duration. Cost per trainee data unavailable. If aligned to existing ITI/vocational schemes, cost is ~₹2–5 lakh per trainee per annum (data unavailable — TN ITI training cost not in reference). 10 lakh × ₹3 lakh (rough mid-point) = ₹300 crore/year minimum.

promise_components.component: Provide 20% scholarship on fees
promise_components.analysis: 20% fee reduction applied to identified beneficiaries. Govt school 11th-12th is free; higher education (junior college, diploma) varies. If applied to junior college (₹200–500/year in govt institutions), 20% = ₹40–100 per student. If applied to diploma/degree, cost higher. Data unavailable — actual fee structure and eligible cohort size for post-10th education in TN govt system not in reference.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹300–500 crore/year (compulsory training for ~10 lakh students at ₹3–5 lakh per trainee, assuming ~20% of passers are economically disadvantaged + 20% fee subsidy cost ~₹50 crore). This is ~0.3–0.5% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Affordable but requires ring-fencing for sustained delivery.
sustainability_reasoning: Combining skills training + fee subsidy addresses both immediate financial barriers and structural employability gaps — students acquire vocational competence while affordability is improved. This is genuinely structural IF training is linked to actual job placements and employer demand is validated. Risk: if training is generic/disconnected from labour market, it becomes symptomatic relief. Success depends on integrating training with employer partnerships and job placement guarantees.

promise_text_en: Support 5000 teacher trainees with scholarships and grants to pursue higher education
promise_text_ta: è™ÖKèO™ ðEò£ŸÁ‹ 5000‚°‹ «ñŸð†ì «ðó£CKò˜èÀ‚°Š ðE «ñ‹ð£´ õöƒèŠðì£î G¬ôJ™
impact_mechanism: Funds higher education access for 5,000 pre-service teachers through scholarships, expanding the pool of qualified educators and reducing teacher shortages in government schools.

promise_components.component: Support 5,000 teacher trainees with scholarships and grants
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies exact number: 5,000 trainees. Scope: B.Ed, M.Ed, or both — data unavailable. Scholarship amount per trainee unspecified — typical B.Ed scholarship ₹1–3 lakh/year for 2 years; M.Ed ₹1.5–4 lakh/year for 2 years (data unavailable — TN teacher education scholarship rates not in reference). Assuming B.Ed: 5,000 × ₹2 lakh/year × 2 years = ₹20 crore total. Assuming grants are one-time (stipend, books, hostel): ~₹10–15 crore additional.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated ₹30–50 crore over 2 years (₹15–25 crore/year average). This is ~0.008–0.014% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr). Highly affordable. Cumulative 5,000 teachers trained annually = ~2–3% of estimated annual teacher recruitment need (~150,000–200,000 teachers across TN govt system — data unavailable, rough estimate).
sustainability_reasoning: Directly addresses teacher shortage and quality gaps by expanding the supply of trained educators. Cost is modest and fiscally sustainable. However, this alone addresses supply; root causes of teacher shortages (insufficient recruitment, career progression, working conditions) also require parallel action. If scholarships are not coupled with guaranteed deployment, trainees may migrate out of state. Sustainability depends on coordinated recruitment policy and retention measures.

promise_text_en: Ensure at least 85% of schools have trained teachers in place within a fixed timeline by 2025
promise_text_ta: îŸè£Lè c†®Š¹Š ðîM‚ è£ôˆF«ô«ò 85 M¿‚è£´ ð™è¬ô‚èöèƒèœ ªî£ì˜‰¶ ªêò™ðì «õ‡®ò G¬ô ãŸð†´œ÷¶
impact_mechanism: Mandates 85% of government schools employ qualified, trained teachers by 2025, directly improving instructional quality and learning outcomes for ~89 lakh students.

promise_components.component: Ensure at least 85% of schools have trained teachers by 2025
promise_components.analysis: TN has ~53,000 govt schools (primary + secondary). 85% = ~45,050 schools must have 'trained teachers' by 2025. Current baseline data unavailable — proportion of schools already meeting this standard not in reference. Deficit is data unavailable — cannot determine how many schools currently lack trained staff or how many additional trained teachers must be deployed. 'Trained teachers' undefined: B.Ed qualified? In-service trained? Both? Cost of achieving this gap is data unavailable.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — requires baseline data on (a) current % schools with trained teachers, (b) definition of 'trained,' (c) teacher recruitment vs. upskilling cost. If interpreted as recruiting 10,000 new B.Ed teachers: ₹500 cr (salary+setup); if upskilling in-service staff: ₹100–200 cr depending on training intensity. Cannot calculate without baseline.
sustainability_reasoning: Ensuring qualified teachers in 85% of schools is a foundational structural reform — addresses root cause of poor learning outcomes (untrained instructors). However, the promise is vague (no baseline, no definition of 'trained,' no cost allocation) and the 2025 timeline is tight (9 months from now). High implementation risk if teacher recruitment processes are slow or if definition of 'trained' changes mid-implementation. Sustainability is strong IF followed by retention incentives and workload management; otherwise, trained teachers will migrate to better-resourced states.

promise_text_en: Establish curriculum councils comprising educationalists, historians, and experts to develop school curricula
promise_text_ta: àò˜ è™MŠð£ìˆF™ å¼ ¶¬øJ™ õöƒèŠð´‹ ð†ìƒèœ ñŸø Ü¬ùˆ¶Š ð™è¬ô‚èöèƒèO½‹ å«ó ªðòK™, å«ó îóˆF™, å«ó ð£ìˆF†ìˆ¶ìÂ‹
impact_mechanism: Expert-led curriculum design improves pedagogical alignment with contemporary knowledge standards and state priorities.

promise_components.component: Establish curriculum councils with educationalists, historians, and experts
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies composition (educationalists, historians, experts) but provides no numbers on council size, scope (which grades/subjects), budget allocation, or timeline. Reference data shows ~53,000 TN govt schools and ~89 lakh students — scale of beneficiaries is implicit but costs undefined. No rupee figure provided in promise. Data unavailable — cannot calculate implementation cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Cost undefined: no budget figures, council size, or operational scope stated. Cannot assess against ₹184,162 cr discretionary budget without specifications.
sustainability_reasoning: Curriculum design is a foundational lever that shapes what students learn and how teaching methods evolve. If executed rigorously with domain expertise, this addresses the structural gap between outdated syllabi and labour market/citizenship needs. However, success depends entirely on expert autonomy, implementation fidelity across 53,000 schools, and adequate teacher training — vague promise suggests uncertain execution.

promise_text_en: Provide death benefits and welfare assistance to the families of road accident victims; workers compensation will be ensured through provident fund (PF) and gratuity benefits ranging from ₹5,12,000 to ₹7,15,000
promise_text_ta: «ð£‚°õóˆ¶ áNò˜èO¡ õ¼ƒè£ô ¬õŠ¹ GF (PF), ðE G¬ø¾ ï¡P‚ ªè£¬ì (Gratuity) àœO†ì æŒ×Fò G½¬õè÷£™ ð£F‚èŠð†´œ÷ù˜. Þ‰î G½¬õˆªî£¬è êŸ«øø‚°¬øò 5,12,000- & 7,15,000 «è£® õ¬ó Þ¼Šðî£è ñFŠHìŠð´Aø¶.
impact_mechanism: Targeted death benefits and worker compensation (PF + gratuity) provide immediate financial relief to accident victim families, reducing acute post-injury economic shock.

promise_components.component: Provide death benefits and welfare assistance to families of road accident victims
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies benefit amount range: ₹5,12,000 to ₹7,15,000 per deceased victim (via PF and gratuity). Baseline: 18,500 casualties recorded in 2024 (from Promise 2). Not all casualties are deaths; fatal accident count not stated separately. If assume 40% fatality rate (conservative estimate based on 67,500 accidents, 18,500 casualties ≈ 27%), fatal accidents ≈ 7,400 in 2024. Annual death benefit cost: ₹6,13,500 (midpoint) × 7,400 deaths = ₹4,540 cr/year. However, promise scope undefined: is this for all road deaths or only workers? Is this in addition to existing worker compensation schemes? Data unavailable — cannot confirm beneficiary count or whether scheme is new or expanded.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost (if covering ~7,400 annual road deaths at ₹6,13,500 per family): ₹4,540 cr/year. This is 2.5% of ₹184,162 cr discretionary budget. CRITICAL AMBIGUITY: promise does not clarify if scheme covers all road deaths or only 'workers', and does not state if this replaces or supplements existing compensation. Feasibility depends on scope clarification — if 'workers' only, cost may be lower; if all road deaths, cost estimate is valid but must be benchmarked against existing compensation schemes to avoid double-counting.
sustainability_reasoning: Death benefits provide genuine, targeted relief to grieving families facing immediate economic hardship — real symptomatic aid. However, cash compensation does not prevent accidents; it manages the aftermath. Root cause of road deaths (speeding, poor road design, inadequate enforcement, drunk driving) remains unaddressed. This promise is best paired with Promises 2–3 (enforcement and infrastructure) to be part of a comprehensive safety strategy.

promise_text_en: Improve educational quality through better infrastructure; all government schools will have digital boards installed to enhance teaching methods and student learning outcomes
promise_text_ta: Ü¬ùˆ¶Š «ð¼‰¶ GÁˆîƒèO½‹ «ð¼‰¶ õ¼¬è¬ò ÜP»‹ I¡ ðô¬èèœ (Digital Boards) Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹.
impact_mechanism: Digital boards replace chalk-based instruction, enabling multimedia teaching methods and reducing teacher preparation time, potentially improving learning outcomes if teacher training is adequate.

promise_components.component: Install digital boards in all government schools to enhance teaching methods and student learning outcomes
promise_components.analysis: Promise commits to digital board installation in all TN govt schools. Reference data: ~53,000 govt schools (primary + secondary). No cost per board, no procurement timeline, no teacher training budget, and no maintenance/electricity cost stated. Typical smart board cost (hardware + installation): ₹1.5–3 lakh per unit. At ₹2 lakh/board × 53,000 schools = ₹10,600 cr one-time hardware cost, plus annual maintenance (est. 5–10% of capital = ₹530–1,060 cr/year). Teacher training for digital pedagogy: typically ₹5,000–15,000 per teacher; TN govt schools employ ~7 lakh teachers (estimated; not in reference data), cost = ₹35–105 cr one-time. Promise provides no budget, so cost estimates are indicative, not confirmed.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated one-time hardware cost: ₹10,600 cr (₹2 lakh/board × 53,000 schools). Estimated annual maintenance: ₹530–1,060 cr/year. Estimated one-time teacher training: ₹35–105 cr. Total first-year cost: ~₹11,100–12,000 cr; annual recurring: ₹530–1,060 cr. One-time cost is 6% of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr); annual maintenance is 0.3–0.6% of discretionary budget. Feasible as multi-year roll-out but CRITICAL: promise does not specify budget, procurement model (govt purchase vs. PPP), or timeline. Without central/state co-funding or PPP, one-time ₹10,600 cr outlay strains budget severely.
sustainability_reasoning: Digital boards are a teaching tool that can enhance engagement and visualisation of concepts, but they do NOT fix structural education gaps: inadequate teacher training, poor curriculum, high dropout rates, or gender/caste-based discrimination in classroom dynamics. Learning outcome improvement requires simultaneous investment in pedagogy, teacher professional development, and curriculum reform (Promise 0). Technology alone without teacher capability is 'optics' — digital boards sitting unused are common. This promise is instrumentally sound but educationally incomplete without companion promises on teacher quality and curriculum.

impact_mechanism: Expands Chennai airport passenger capacity and connectivity by 30–40% through T3 terminal, enabling higher international traffic and economic activity.

promise_components.component: Develop Chennai airport Terminal T3 with 86,135 sq m area
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies: (1) area = 86,135 sq m, (2) cost = ₹2,467 crore, (3) capacity = 500+ aircraft movements/day. Cost per sq m: ₹2,467 cr / 86,135 sq m = ₹28,640/sq m — reasonable for international airport terminal (global benchmark ₹25,000–35,000/sq m). Cost appears internally consistent.

promise_components.component: Terminal to handle 500+ aircraft movements per day
promise_components.analysis: Current Chennai airport (T1+T2) handles ~250–300 movements/day. T3 adding 200+ movements implies ~33% capacity increase, consistent with large terminal development. Operational impact: requires additional ground handling staff, parking stands, taxiways, ATC upgrades (not mentioned in promise — implementation risk).

fiscal_cost_note: Capex: ₹2,467 crore (stated). As % of TN discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr): 1.34%. Annual debt service (assuming 20-year bond @ 6%): ~₹190–200 cr/year = 0.11% of discretionary budget. Fiscal feasibility: medium — significant but manageable if phased over 5–6 years. However, implementation depends on land acquisition, environmental clearance, central govt support for airport expansion (shared jurisdiction). Central co-funding likely necessary.
sustainability_reasoning: Addresses structural bottleneck in air connectivity — T3 enables higher tourist/business traffic and supports cargo growth (if paired with cargo handling improvements). Creates ongoing employment (ground handling, retail, hospitality). However, depends on: (1) air traffic demand actually reaching 500+ movements (post-COVID recovery uncertain), (2) central govt approval and co-funding, (3) operational efficiency (T1+T2 currently underutilized during off-peak — T3 may not fill if demand doesn't grow). Execution risk is material.

scheme_name: Cargo Handling Capacity Enhancement - Multiple Airports
impact_mechanism: Expands air cargo capacity across three major airports by 55–60%, enabling faster logistics and supporting export-oriented manufacturing and perishables trade.

promise_components.component: Develop cargo handling infrastructure at Chennai airport with 55–60% capacity improvement
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies improvement rate (55–60%) but not baseline capacity or absolute tonnage target. Current Chennai airport cargo: ~350,000 tonnes/year (est.). 55–60% improvement = +193,000–210,000 tonnes/year capacity added. Promise cost listed as ₹53,000 cr — but this is stated as total for all 3 airports (Chennai, Bangalore, Hyderabad), not individual allocation.

promise_components.component: Develop cargo handling infrastructure at Bangalore airport with 55–60% capacity improvement
promise_components.analysis: Bangalore (Kempegowda Intl) is NOT in Tamil Nadu — it is in Karnataka. Promise is out of state policy scope. This is a governance error or misstatement. Cannot assess TN fiscal responsibility for Bangalore expansion.

promise_components.component: Develop cargo handling infrastructure at Hyderabad airport with 55–60% capacity improvement
promise_components.analysis: Hyderabad (Rajiv Gandhi Intl) is NOT in Tamil Nadu — it is in Telangana. This is also out of TN governance scope. Same governance issue as Bangalore.

promise_components.component: Project timeline and cost: ₹53,000 crore total for all three airports
promise_components.analysis: ₹53,000 cr allocated across 3 airports for cargo + likely associated ground transport/supply-chain infrastructure. Allocation to TN (Chennai only) unknown — estimated 20–30% of total (~₹10,600–15,900 cr for TN share) if equally distributed. However, this is speculation — promise does not clarify. Implementation timeline not stated.

fiscal_cost_note: TN share of ₹53,000 cr cost unclear. If Chennai is 25% of total = ₹13,250 cr capex over project timeline (est. 5–7 years). Annual debt service: ~₹180–220 cr/year = 0.12% of discretionary budget. Fiscal feasibility: low-medium, depends on (1) central co-funding (likely given airport authority jurisdiction), (2) PPP or concessionaire involvement (common for cargo hubs). However, 50–70% of promise cost is for airports outside TN (Bangalore, Hyderabad) — governance authority unclear; likely requires central govt/respective state coordination, not TN-only action.
sustainability_reasoning: Addresses structural logistics constraint — cargo capacity bottleneck limits perishables/time-sensitive exports (pharmaceuticals, seafood, flowers, auto parts). 55–60% expansion supports manufacturing competitiveness. However, depends on: (1) cargo demand actually materializing (post-COVID recovery + China reshoring uncertain), (2) airfreight rates remaining competitive vs. shipping, (3) ground logistics (trucking, storage) scaling in parallel (not mentioned). Critical risk: 67% of stated cost is for airports outside TN, limiting TN's direct control and benefit. Politically ambitious but governance-unclear.

promise_text_en: Implement 'Port Safety Initiative' and establish environmental protection measures including coastal regulation zone compliance and sustainable fisheries support for coastal communities

impact_mechanism: Regulatory and livelihood framework addressing fishery sustainability and coastal safety, reducing both environmental degradation and income shocks for fishing communities.

promise_components.component: Implement 'Port Safety Initiative' — unspecified measures
promise_components.analysis: Promise does not define 'Port Safety Initiative'. Typical port safety components: vessel traffic management, emergency response, pollution control, worker safety standards. Cost and scope data unavailable — cannot calculate.

promise_components.component: Establish coastal regulation zone (CRZ) compliance measures
promise_components.analysis: CRZ is a national framework (Coastal Regulation Zone Notification, MoE&F). Promise commits to enforce existing regulation, not introduce new policy. Fiscal cost: enforcement (inspectors, monitoring equipment) — estimated ₹10–20 cr/year for TN coastal districts. Data unavailable for exact budget allocation.

promise_components.component: Establish environmental protection measures for coastal areas
promise_components.analysis: Scope unspecified — could include: mangrove restoration, marine pollution abatement, fishery habitat protection, seaweed farming, etc. Cost and mechanism data unavailable.

promise_components.component: Sustainable fisheries support for coastal communities
promise_components.analysis: Unspecified support — could be: gear subsidies, alternative livelihoods, skill training, market linkages, microfinance, etc. TN coastal population: est. ~3–4 million. If cash transfer model: ₹500/month = ₹6,000/year × 0.5 million households = ₹300 cr/year (illustrative, not stated in promise). If subsidy-based (fuel, nets, ice): ₹100–200 cr/year estimated. Data unavailable for actual design.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate total cost. Promise provides no budget, timeline, beneficiary count, or specific interventions. Rough order-of-magnitude only (illustrative): CRZ compliance enforcement ₹15 cr/year + sustainable fisheries support ₹150–300 cr/year = ₹165–315 cr/year (0.09–0.17% of discretionary budget). This is speculative; not grounded in promise detail.
sustainability_reasoning: Addresses structural drivers of coastal livelihood insecurity: (1) overfishing/stock depletion (regulatory framework through CRZ), (2) environmental degradation (mangroves, pollution), (3) income volatility (livelihood diversification). However, implementation risk is severe: (1) fishers historically resist regulation (catch-limiting measures breed resistance), (2) coastal trade-offs (conservation vs. immediate income) require long-term political commitment, (3) 'sustainable fisheries support' is undefined — without specific mechanisms (eg. seaweed farming, aquaculture co-ops, market linkages), it remains aspirational. Success depends on genuine fisher participation in design + enforceability against illegal fishing. High ambition but execution fragility.

promise_components.analysis: Promise names outcome but no operational mechanism: trade facilitation authority? Vessel scheduling system? Cargo tracking? Data unavailable — cannot estimate capex or staffing cost.

promise_components.analysis: Promise mentions 'digital infrastructure' generically — broadband, Wi-Fi hotspots, fiber-to-premises, or mobile towers? impact_mechanism: Adding value to raw agricultural output within villages, reducing post-harvest losses, increasing farmer margins, and creating non-farm rural employment.

promise_components.component: Establish Agro-Processing Units in villages
promise_components.analysis: Promise does not specify: how many units? Which villages? What scale (household-level, community-based, or small-industrial)? TN has ~83 lakh farmer households (reference data); promise does not state target coverage or farmers served per unit. Major agro-products in TN: paddy, sugarcane, coconut, spices, fruits — promise does not specify product focus or processing types (milling, canning, drying, oil extraction). promise_components.component: Value addition of agricultural products
promise_components.analysis: Promise names outcome but no specifics: margin improvement target (e.g., 20% uplift)? Quality standards? Market linkage mechanism? TN paddy MSP (reference data) is ₹2,300/quintal; value-added rice commands 50–100% premiums in retail markets, but promise does not quantify target margin or farmer share. Data unavailable — cannot estimate income gain per unit or aggregate farmer income impact.

promise_components.component: Agricultural entrepreneurship development
promise_components.analysis: Promise mentions training/support but provides no detail: financial inclusion (credit to operators)? Business mentoring? Market linkage? TN has ~4.7 lakh Women Self-Help Groups (~63 lakh women members, reference data) — promise does not state if agro-processing will prioritize women or SHG participation. Data unavailable — cannot estimate cost of entrepreneurship program or employment generation.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated annual cost: data unavailable — no unit count, capex per unit, or O&M budget provided. Agro-processing units typically cost ₹5–50 lakh to establish (varies by scale and product); establishing 10,000 units across villages would cost ₹50,000–₹50 lakh cr capex. Annual operating subsidy (working capital, staff, equipment maintenance) could be ₹500 cr–₹2,000 cr if units operate at low capacity initially. Cannot assess % of discretionary budget without scope.
sustainability_reasoning: Agro-processing creates lasting income uplift by enabling value addition, reducing post-harvest losses (a major leakage in Indian agriculture), and generating year-round non-farm employment. However, the promise is unscoped — no target unit count, village prioritization, technology selection, or market-linkage mechanism. Risk of units operating below capacity due to weak demand, farmer-operator cash-flow stress, or poor market access. Success requires complementary training, supply-chain integration, and credit support not detailed in the promise.

Promise lacks: (a) number of centers to establish, (b) capital cost per center, (c) annual operational budget (trainer salaries, materials), (d) enrollment/beneficiary targets, (e) startup capital or credit support. sustainability_reasoning: If executed with rigorous design—clear enrollment targets, industry-aligned curricula, guaranteed placement linkages, and adequate capital + operating budget—this addresses the root cause: lack of market-demanded skills in rural areas, which perpetuates low wages and limited non-farm income. However, promise as stated is unquantified; success depends entirely on implementation depth and post-training job placement, which the manifesto does not commit to.

impact_mechanism: Government procurement commitment provides guaranteed market access and stable prices for farm produce, reducing price volatility risk and enabling village producers to scale production with confidence.

promise_components.component: Develop village farming businesses
promise_components.analysis: Promise does not define 'village farming businesses': does it mean individual farms, producer collectives, agricultural cooperatives, or agro-enterprises? sustainability_reasoning: If coupled with clear procurement contracts, assured prices, and quality standards, this addresses the root cause: farmer exposure to volatile wholesale prices and dependence on intermediaries. However, manifesto provides no volume commitments, price guarantees, or implementation timeline. Sustainability depends on: (a) whether procurement volume is stable/growing, (b) whether prices keep pace with input costs and inflation, and (c) whether farmer groups have capacity to meet quality/volume standards. Without these commitments, risk is high that support remains ad-hoc or insufficient to materially improve farmer income.

promise_text_en: Establish Vocational Training Centers in all districts and provide skill development training to students
promise_text_ta: ªî£N™¶¬ø ê£˜‰î «è£K‚¬èèœ Ü¬ùˆ¶‹ å¼ Aö¬ñ‚°œ (õ£óˆFŸ°œ) º®‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: District-level vocational training centers reduce geographic access barriers, enabling youth and workers to acquire job-ready skills and transition to formal employment or self-employment.

promise_components.component: Establish Vocational Training Centers in all districts
promise_components.analysis: TN has 38 districts. Promise commits one center per district minimum. However, does not specify: (a) center capacity (annual enrollment per center), (b) capital cost per center (land, building, equipment), (c) infrastructure standards (labs, workshops, digital facilities?), (d) trainer qualifications and salary scale. Illustrative capital cost: assume ₹5 crore per center (modest ITI-standard facility) = ₹5 cr × 38 districts = ₹190 crore one-time. Annual operational cost (assume 500 trainees per center, 10-month course, trainer salary ₹5 lakh/year, materials ₹50,000 per trainee): 500 × ₹50k + 10 trainers × ₹5 lakh = ₹2.5 cr + ₹0.5 cr = ₹3 cr per center × 38 = ₹114 cr/year. BUT this is illustrative; manifesto does not specify these parameters. Data unavailable — cannot verify cost assumption.

promise_components.component: Provide skill development training to students
promise_components.analysis: Promise does not specify: (a) target enrollment (all HS students? rural students only? age cohort?), (b) duration (6 months? 2 years?), (c) cost per trainee borne by state, (d) employment outcome target. TN has ~89 lakh government school students; if even 10% enroll in vocational training, that is 8.9 lakh trainees/year. Cost per trainee (trainer time, materials, equipment depreciation): data unavailable. Cannot calculate without enrollment target and per-trainee cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated capital cost (illustrative, unverified): ₹190 crore (₹5 cr/center × 38 districts). Estimated annual operational cost (illustrative, unverified): ₹114 crore/year assuming 500 trainees/center, modest trainer costs. As % of discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr): ~0.06% capital, ~0.06% recurrent — appears affordable IF cost estimates are realistic. However, manifesto provides no cost specification; actual cost could be 2–5× higher depending on infrastructure standards, trainer salaries, and enrollment volumes. Implementation risk is high due to lack of detail on beneficiary targets and cost controls.
sustainability_reasoning: Vocational training centers address the root skill gap—many rural and urban youth lack industry-aligned technical skills, limiting earning potential. However, structural benefit depends critically on: (a) curriculum relevance to labor market demand, (b) trainer quality and industry exposure, (c) post-training job placement, and (d) regular curriculum updates as industries evolve. Manifesto does not commit to any of these quality metrics, making it high-risk that centers become underutilized or train students for jobs that don't materialize.

sustainability_reasoning: Identical to Index 2. Addresses root skill gap if curriculum and placement are rigorous; sustainability hinges on sustained funding, trainer quality, and labor market relevance—none committed to in manifesto.

impact_mechanism: Attracting more industrial establishments to existing zones (ELCOT, TIDCO, SIPCOT) increases manufacturing capacity, generates local employment, and supports skill training demand from industries.

promise_components.component: Provide opportunities for skill development training [through industrial zone development]
promise_components.analysis: Promise implicitly ties skill training to industrial zone growth, but does not specify: (a) who funds training (state or industry), (b) training curriculum (determined by employers?), (c) enrollment target, (d) cost per trainee. Mechanism is indirect: assume more industries → more employer-led training + more graduate job demand. But manifesto does not guarantee state funding for training; it may be industry-self-funded, in which case state cost is minimal but so is state control over quality/equity. Data unavailable — cannot calculate state fiscal commitment.

fiscal_cost_note: Data unavailable — cannot calculate. Fiscal impact depends entirely on incentive design, which manifesto does not specify. If incentives are tax/land subsidies, cost flows through lost revenue; if capital investment in zone infrastructure, cost is direct. Without detail, feasibility cannot be assessed.
sustainability_reasoning: Industrial zone expansion and skills training linked to industrial demand create structural jobs and income growth. However, sustainability depends on: (a) industries remaining in TN (labor costs, competing states), (b) skill training curricula staying aligned with industry needs, and (c) local workers having access to training and jobs (risk of migrant workers being preferred). Manifesto does not address local hire commitments or training access barriers, leaving structural benefit uncertain.

sustainability_reasoning: Industrial job creation and entrepreneur support address structural employment and income constraints. Manifesto does not commit to any of these, making long-term structural benefit uncertain.

Risk: farmers adopt without assured buyer network, revert to chemicals, or face income collapse.

impact_mechanism: Reduces income volatility and increases average farm returns by shifting cultivation patterns to higher-value crops and linking production to buyer demand rather than speculation.

impact_mechanism: Increases farm income and nutritional security in resource-constrained regions by shifting production from low-value cereals to nutrient-dense pulses and millets, aligned with national nutrition policy.

promise_components.component: Expand pulses and nutritious grain farming schemes in resource-constrained regions
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies crop types (pulses, nutritious grains) and target geography (resource-constrained regions) but no targets: how many farmers, how many hectares, which regions, investment per farmer, expected yield/income increase, or timeline. TN grows pulses (chickpea, pigeonpea) mainly in Dharmapuri, Krishnagiri, and interior districts. ~42 lakh marginal farmers are in resource-constrained areas. MSP paddy TN 2024-25: ₹2,300/quintal; average yield ~2,800 kg/ha. Pulse yields TN average ~1,200 kg/ha; current MSP pigeonpea ~₹6,800/quintal. Data unavailable on: current pulse coverage, cost of transition from cereals, subsidy per farmer, price floor guarantee. Cannot calculate precise fiscal cost.

fiscal_cost_note: Speculative range: Pulse farming promotion typically ₹3,000–8,000/farmer for seeds, soil prep, and training. If scheme reaches 15 lakh farmers in resource-constrained regions: ₹450–1,200 cr one-time over 2–3 years = ₹150–600 cr/year. Add ₹50–100 cr/year for pulse price support/MSP guarantee. Total: ₹200–700 cr/year = 0.11–0.38% of discretionary budget. Affordability: MEDIUM. Feasibility depends on farmer willingness to shift from rice (secure MSP) to pulses (uncertain buyer market).
sustainability_reasoning: Pulse farming addresses dual root causes: (a) farmer income (pulses often have higher MSP and lower input cost than cereals), (b) regional nutrition security (high protein crops reduce malnutrition). However, farmer adoption depends on stable MSP, reliable buyer markets, and credit access. TN pulse area has been stagnant for decades due to low yield, buyer uncertainty, and middleman exploitation. Success requires simultaneous investment in pulses research, MSP credibility, and market linkage — all are structural but currently weak.

promise_text_en: Establish agricultural produce storage and processing facilities through cooperative societies
promise_text_ta: ªïê¾ â¡ð¶ ªî£N™ Ü™ô. ïñ¶ ð‡ð£´. ãøˆî£ö 5000 Ý‡´èœ ªî£¡¬ñ õ£Œ‰î è¬ô
impact_mechanism: Create last-mile storage and processing infrastructure to reduce post-harvest losses and enable farmer value capture.

promise_components.component: Establish 5,000 agricultural produce storage and processing units through cooperative societies
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 5,000 units. Reference data: ~83 lakh farmer households in TN (Agri Census 2015); marginal farmers ~42 lakh. Cost per unit (capital + land) is NOT specified in promise or reference data — cannot calculate total investment. Operational cost per unit per annum unavailable. Coverage ratio: 5,000 units ÷ 83 lakh farmers = 0.6% farmer households per unit (assumes multi-farmer use). Mechanism requires: (a) capital subsidy/grant structure, (b) cooperative capacity-building, (c) supply chain linkage definition. None are detailed.

fiscal_cost_note: Capital cost per unit not specified; annual operational subsidy unknown. Cannot calculate feasibility against ₹184,162 cr discretionary budget without unit economics. Implementation cost likely high (₹500 lakh–₹2,000 lakh per unit typical for food processing), suggesting ₹250–₹1,000 cr capex, but unverified.
sustainability_reasoning: If executed with robust cooperative governance, storage units address a genuine structural bottleneck: post-harvest losses in India run 10–15% for perishables, reducing farmer income. However, promise provides no detail on cooperative training, market linkage, or financial sustainability mechanisms — execution risk is very high, and without those elements, facilities may become dormant assets.

promise_text_en: Establish IT and BPO sectors as a major economic growth driver with 8 million jobs creation in Tamil Nadu's IT sector
promise_text_ta: îèõ™ ªî£N™¸†ðˆ ¶¬ø ï£†®¡ ªñ£ˆî àœï£†´ Ý‚èˆF™ 7.7 M¿‚è£´ ðƒèOŠ¹„ ªêŒ¶œ÷¶, «ñ½‹ 2025Ý‹ Ý‡®™ Þ‰Fò£M¡ ªñ£ˆî àœï£†´ Ý‚èˆF™ 10 M¿‚è£´ ðƒèO‚°‹
impact_mechanism: Generate formal-sector employment and tax revenue through IT/BPO sector growth, raising household incomes and broadening the tax base.

promise_components.component: Establish IT and BPO sectors as major growth driver; create 8 million jobs in IT sector by 2025
promise_components.analysis: Promise targets '10 million jobs (target by 2025)' in header, then '8 million jobs in IT sector' in promise text — target is ambiguous (8M or 10M?). TN IT employment (all sectors) was ~16.5 lakh as of FY2023 (NASSCOM data, not in reference). To add 8 million formal IT jobs by 2025 (two years from April 2026 assessment) is a retrospective claim (deadline already past). Actual employment growth in IT requires: (a) capex in data centres, tech parks, (b) skill training pipeline, (c) regulatory reform, (d) wage-cost competitiveness. Promise mentions none of these enablers. No timeline, budget, or implementation agency specified.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated cost: Direct govt subsidy for IT park development ~₹50–200 cr per major hub; TN would need ~5–10 hubs = ₹250–2,000 cr capex. Skill training cost ~₹10,000–15,000 per trainee; 800,000 trainees = ₹80–120 cr/year. Total plausible range: ₹500–1,500 cr capex + ₹100–150 cr/year revenue. This is 0.3–0.8% of discretionary budget. However, promised jobs are almost certainly unachievable by stated 2025 deadline (assessment date is April 2026, deadline is past); fiscal 'cost' is therefore moot because delivery is infeasible.
sustainability_reasoning: IT/BPO job creation is structurally sound for income growth and tax revenue, but the 8M job target by 2025 is not credible as a current manifesto promise (deadline is already past). Even if reinterpreted as 2030, TN would need to double its existing IT workforce in 4 years — requiring sustained FDI, infrastructure investment, and skill supply that are not detailed here. Without those delivery mechanisms, this remains aspirational rather than operational.

impact_mechanism: Reduce latency and enable digital transformation in manufacturing and industrial sectors, improving productivity and competitiveness.

promise_components.component: Provide high-speed optical fibre data connectivity via 120 optical fibre networks for all manufacturing and industrial sectors
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 120 optical fibre networks. Reference data does not specify: (a) number of manufacturing/industrial facilities in TN, (b) coverage rate (% of facilities), (c) bandwidth per network, (d) capex and opex per network, (e) timeline for rollout. Industry estimate: TN has ~50,000–60,000 registered manufacturing units (not in reference); 120 networks = 0.2–0.24% direct coverage unless 'network' means a shared multi-user facility (then coverage is undefined without facility map). Cost per fibre route: ₹50–200 lakh depending on distance and terrain. Total range: ₹600–2,400 cr capex. Annual maintenance: ~₹30–50 cr. Promise does not specify subsidy vs. user-pay model.

fiscal_cost_note: Estimated capex: ₹600–2,400 cr (0.3–1.3% of discretionary budget); annual opex: ₹30–50 cr. Fiscal load depends on subsidy structure (not specified). If fully state-funded, this is moderately expensive but not infeasible. If user-pays or PPP, fiscal burden is low. Given silence on financing, assume optimistic 50% state subsidy = ₹300–1,200 cr capex commitment + ₹15–25 cr/year opex.
sustainability_reasoning: High-speed connectivity is a genuine structural enabler of industrial productivity and competitiveness, addressing the digital divide between metros and smaller industrial clusters. However, 120 networks is vague (does it cover 10% or 0.2% of factories?), and without clear coverage mapping, cost-sharing terms, and operations plan, the promise reads as opaque. The underlying policy direction (digital infrastructure for manufacturing) is sound; execution detail is absent.

promise_text_en: Implement new industrial relations laws with 15-day dispute resolution timeline and provide gratuity benefits after 3 years of service
promise_text_ta: ÜŠð®ò£ù Åö™èO™ îŸ«ð£¬îò ê†ìŠð® (The Industrial Disputes Act 1947) å¼ ñ£î è£ô‹ º¡ùî£èˆ ªî£Nô£÷¼‚° ªîKòŠð´ˆF Üõó¶ ðE è£ôˆF¡ð® Ý‡´‚° 15 ï£œèœ ê‹ð÷‹
impact_mechanism: Reduce labour dispute resolution time and provide income security to workers, lowering dispute costs for employers and improving dispute finality.

promise_components.component: Implement new industrial relations laws with 15-day dispute resolution timeline
promise_components.analysis: Promise specifies 15-day dispute resolution timeline (vs current ~6–12 months through labour courts). Industrial Disputes Act 1947 already mandates: Section 10 (conciliation within 14 days if agreed; can extend). Promise appears to mandate mandatory resolution in 15 days. Legal feasibility: (a) 15 days is extremely tight for adjudication of complex claims (wrongful dismissal, wage arrears, injury liability). TN labour courts have ~50,000+ pending cases. (b) State cannot override central labour law without central amendment. (c) Implementation requires hiring ~500–1,000 additional conciliation officers and labour judges (estimated ~₹100–150 cr capex + ₹20–30 cr/year salary). Data on current case backlog and judge-to-case ratio not in reference.

promise_components.component: Provide gratuity benefits after 3 years of service (instead of current 5 years under Payment of Gratuity Act 1972)
promise_components.analysis: Current gratuity vesting: 5 years continuous service (Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, Section 4). Promise: reduce to 3 years. Fiscal impact: Gratuity is a statutory employer obligation; reducing vesting period accelerates employer payouts. Reference data: formal sector employment in TN ~35–40 lakh (est., not in reference). Average gratuity: ₹1.5–3 lakh depending on wage, sector. Cost shift: employers pay 2 years of gratuity sooner per worker. Estimated annual cost to employers: ₹1–2 cr (spread across private sector; state bears cost only for govt employees ~30 lakh, ~₹300–600 cr one-time acceleration). State govt cannot unilaterally reduce vesting for private-sector workers (requires central amendment to Payment of Gratuity Act).

fiscal_cost_note: 15-day resolution: Capex ~₹100–150 cr (hiring courts/officers); opex ~₹20–30 cr/year. Gratuity 3-year vesting: One-time cost to state employees ~₹300–600 cr; private sector cost borne by employers (not state budget). Total state cost: ₹100–150 cr capex + ₹20–30 cr/year opex (~0.06–0.1% of discretionary budget annually). However, both components face legal feasibility constraints (central law override required).
sustainability_reasoning: 15-day resolution and early gratuity are genuine worker protections, reducing financial hardship during disputes. However, both are symptomatic relief rather than structural: they do not prevent disputes or strengthen worker skill/bargaining power. More critically, TN cannot unilaterally implement either without central government amendment (labour is a concurrent subject, but Payment of Gratuity Act and core Industrial Disputes Act are central). Promise overstates state authority.

promise_text_en: Introduce 9-day leave entitlement for monthly workers and 48-day leave for seasonal workers under Factories Act 1948
promise_text_ta: ê†ì‹ 1948 (Factories Act 1948)-Þ¡ð® å¼ ï£¬÷‚è£ù «õ¬ô «ïóñ£è 9 ñE «ïóº‹, å¼ Aö¬ñ‚è£ù (õ£óˆFŸè£ù) «õ¬ô «ïóñ£è 48 ñE «ïóº«ñ
impact_mechanism: Increase paid leave entitlements for workers, raising de facto income security and reducing burnout in seasonal and monthly-wage employment.

promise_components.component: Introduce 9-day annual leave entitlement for monthly workers (vs current entitlement under Factories Act 1948)
promise_components.analysis: Current Factories Act 1948, Section 54: Workers entitled to 1 day per 20 days of work = ~18 days/year paid leave (standard interpretation). Promise: 9 days. This is a *reduction* from current law, not an increase. Promise wording unclear: may mean '9 days above statutory minimum' (total 27 days), but text says '9-day leave entitlement', implying replacement. Assuming promise intends 9 additional days (total 27), this is a real increase. Fiscal impact: Every day of leave is paid; cost = daily wage × 9 days × number of monthly workers. Reference data: formal sector employment ~35–40 lakh (est.); monthly workers portion unknown. Assume 60% are monthly wage workers = 21–24 lakh. Average daily wage: ₹500–800 (unskilled factory work). Cost: ₹800 × 9 days × 22.5 lakh workers = ₹162 cr/year. However, Factories Act is central law; TN cannot unilaterally amend it without central amendment.

promise_components.component: Introduce 48-day annual leave entitlement for seasonal workers
promise_components.analysis: Current Factories Act: no special seasonal worker leave provision (treated as casual workers, entitled to paid leave per accrual only, typically ~5–10 days/year). Promise: 48 days/year is extremely generous (9.6 weeks, implying worker is paid for 48 days while not working). Fiscal impact: ₹600 × 48 days × estimated seasonal workers. Seasonal workers in TN agriculture/construction: ~50–80 lakh (est., not in reference). Cost: ₹600 × 48 × 50 lakh = ₹1,440 cr/year. This is a massive commitment (0.78% of discretionary budget). Who bears cost? If employer-mandated, private sector cost; if state-subsidised, state bears burden. Promise does not specify. Legal issue: Same as above — requires central Factories Act amendment.

fiscal_cost_note: Monthly workers 9-day leave: ~₹162 cr/year (if 22.5 lakh eligible). Seasonal workers 48-day leave: ~₹1,440 cr/year (if 50 lakh eligible; highly uncertain because seasonal workforce size not in reference). Combined: ~₹1,600 cr/year (~0.87% of discretionary budget, moderately expensive). However, both require central Factories Act amendment; TN cannot implement unilaterally. If implemented via state subsidy to employers (reimbursement model), state cost could reach ₹800–1,600 cr/year depending on coverage definition.
sustainability_reasoning: Leave entitlements are genuine income support for vulnerable workers (seasonal and monthly-wage), reducing destitution during slack periods. However, they do not address root causes: low wage rates, lack of skill mobility, or seasonal income volatility. More fundamentally, these are central laws; TN cannot unilaterally amend Factories Act. Promise suggests state authority that does not exist, raising credibility and implementation risk.

promise_text_en: Provide 120 kg biosolids management facilities with recycled materials for construction and zero-waste treatment processes
promise_text_ta: 50 ÜJó„Yªó¬ì (kg) ¬ð…²¬î Í†¬ì‚è£ù Ý‚è„ ªêô¾, è†´„ ªêô¾, «ð£‚°õóˆ¶„ ªêô¾, õKèœ, è†ìí‹ ñŸÁ‹ 20 M¿‚è£´ ß†ì‹ (Þô£ð‹) à†ðì å¼ Í†¬ì ¬ð…²¬îJ¡ M¬ô ãø‚°¬øò 180 à¼ð£Œ î£¡
impact_mechanism: Divert municipal waste from landfills to productive use (construction recycled materials), reducing environmental burden and creating a secondary resource market.

promise_components.component: Provide 120 kg biosolids management facilities with recycled materials for construction and zero-waste treatment processes
promise_components.analysis: Promise is internally inconsistent: header says '120 kg biosolids', body says '50 kg biosolids, 180 cu.m treatment capacity'. Units are also unclear: '120 kg' per facility or total? Daily, annual? TN daily municipal waste: ~40,000 tonnes (est., not in reference). If 120 kg is per facility per day, and TN needs ~400,000 facilities to process 50% of waste. If 120 kg total, this is negligible (3 kg per lakh population). Assuming '120' refers to number of facilities (matching pattern from other promises): 120 facilities × 50 kg biosolids per facility per day = 6,000 kg/day = 2.19 tonnes/year. This is ~0.005% of daily TN waste (40,000 tonnes) — symbolic at best. Treatment capacity 180 cu.m per facility = 21,600 cu.m/year (120 facilities). Methane capture and energy recovery possible, but economics require scale (minimum 1,000 TPD facility typically). Promise is either misquantified or aspirational.

fiscal_cost_note: Capex per biosolids facility: ₹5–20 cr typical (depends on technology: composting, anaerobic digestion, brick-making). For 120 facilities: ₹600–2,400 cr capex. Annual opex: ~₹50 cr (maintenance, transport, staff). Estimated cost: ₹600–2,400 cr capex + ₹50 cr/year opex (0.3–1.3% of discretionary budget capex; 0.03% opex). However, promise numbers are so vague and internally inconsistent that cost estimate is speculative. If 120 is truly the facility count, fiscal burden is high. If promise means something else (120 kilos per unit, or 120 sites), cost is much lower but undefined.
sustainability_reasoning: Zero-waste treatment and recycling are structurally sound approaches to municipal waste management and circular economy. However, the promise is so poorly specified (contradictory units, vague scale, no technology detail) that it reads as optics rather than operational commitment. If executed at scale (1,000+ facilities processing 50%+ of waste), it would be transformative; at 120 small facilities, it is largely symbolic. Credibility is undermined by quantitative incoherence.

promise_text_en: Provide 6 months maternity leave to all women employees under the Maternity Benefit Amendment Act 2017

impact_mechanism: Extends paid leave protection during post-natal recovery period, enabling mother-infant bonding and health stabilization without income loss for formal-sector women employees.

promise_components.component: 6 months maternity leave for all women employees under Maternity Benefit Amendment Act 2017
promise_components.analysis: The Maternity Benefit Amendment Act 2017 already mandates 26 weeks (6 months) paid maternity leave for women employees in establishments with >10 workers, and 6 weeks unpaid leave post-statutory period. This is FEDERAL LAW, not a new state benefit. The promise restates existing central legislation. Beneficiary scope: formal-sector employed women only. Data unavailable on number of formal-sector women employees in TN — cannot estimate cost without employment statistics.

fiscal_cost_note: Zero incremental state cost — this is central law already in force. State government cannot expand beyond federal mandate without constitutional override. If promise intends to apply this to informal sector (domestic workers, agricultural workers: ~63 lakh SHG members, female LFPR 51% rural = ~2 crore women workers), cost data unavailable — cannot calculate without wage bill detail.
sustainability_reasoning: This restates federal law already binding on employers; no new state action required or possible. Benefit real for covered formal-sector women, but promise implies state-level innovation where none exists. Addresses symptom (income during leave) not root cause (wage inequality, caregiving responsibility skew, informal-sector exclusion from any leave protection).

promise_text_en: Grant 3 months maternity leave to each child after the first child
promise_text_ta: «ðÁè£ôŠ ªð‡èÀ‚° æ˜ Ý‡´‹, ÜŠªð‡E¡ èíõ¼‚° 3 ñ£î‹ M´Š¹‹ è†ì£òñ£‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Extends maternity leave duration for subsequent children, recognizing recovery and caregiving needs beyond first birth.

promise_components.component: 3 months maternity leave for each child after the first child
promise_components.analysis: The Maternity Benefit Amendment Act 2017 already provides 26 weeks (6 months) total maternity leave for first two living children, with additional 50 days (7+ weeks) for third child onwards. This promise appears to reframe existing central law as a 3-month (12-week) benefit per child after first. If interpreted as a NEW STATE ENHANCEMENT beyond federal law, no mechanism exists — states cannot unilaterally extend maternity leave entitlements to private-sector workers (labour law is concurrent). If applied only to state government employees, beneficiary count data unavailable; fiscal cost unknown without payroll statistics.

fiscal_cost_note: State payroll for women employees: data unavailable. If promise applies only to state government employees (women: data not in reference), cost cannot be estimated. If intended for private sector, constitutionally not enforceable by state alone without central amendment.
sustainability_reasoning: Promise conflates federal maternity law (which TN state cannot unilaterally alter) with state-level discretion (which is absent for private-sector labour law). Genuine relief if applied to state payroll, but no pathway to deliver for informal/private-sector majority of TN women workers. Does not address underlying issue: absence of caregiving support infrastructure (childcare, elder care) that forces women out of workforce.

promise_text_en: Provide 72 days maternity leave and 50% work from home facility for nursing mothers after maternity leave
